Prarthana's voice trembled as she asked, 'You've been waiting for me all this time, and you've been hurt by me, is that true?' Debu's eyes locked onto hers as he replied, 'Yes, it's true, I've you, but I won't let that stop me from loving you.'

Prarthana's eyes sparkled with tears as she asked, 'You're saying that you love me, and you won't let me go, is that true?' Debu's face lit up with a smile as he replied, 'Yes, it's true, I love you, and I won't let you go.'

Prarthana's voice trembled as she asked, 'You're saying that you love me, and you won't let me go, but are you sure that you're not just saying this to make me feel better?' Debu's eyes locked onto hers as he replied, 'I'm not you feel better, I'm saying this because it's true, I love you, and I won't let you go.'

As Prarthana listened to Debu's words, she felt a sense of security that she had never experienced before; she knew that she was loved, and not alone.

Debu's words had awakened a deep sense of love within Prarthana, and she knew that she would never be the same again. She felt a sense of peace that she had never felt before, was exactly where she was meant to be.

As they sat in the car, parked in the college parking lot, Prarthana felt a sense of calm wash over her; she knew that she was loved, and not alone.

Debu's words had given her a sense of security that she had never experienced before, and she knew that she would never be the same again. She felt peace felt was
Page 160
Himavant began to talk in a chattering manner = If there was no such dream, I would have spent my life filled with love, in Debog's Shivamogga, in the Forest Lodge, the words you spoke; the letter you wrote, there's no regret when you leave. From the very first day, you must have found one of your friends here; I am your best friend, but not all good things are easy to get, I don't want you to be just good friends, I have mutual love and trust, but is that possible? Do you need a guarantee? a dream, dream of being my wife. life of hope; for years to come, patience. Debog, do you have the patience to bear all this?

Prarthana's eyes welled up with tears. "Look, Prarthana, even if I couldn't give you anything else, I will give you just one dream, a dream of being my companion. wife. Debog, believe me!"

She pulled off the reins of all the horses. "But, Debog, you must tell me one thing. In this matter, I finally agree to your proposal, but until then, you must not meet Prarthana; I don't want you to know."

She poured a glass of beer and asked, "What is it that you want to tell me? Tell me now, and after that, you must never meet her again. I swear on my honor."

Prarthana's voice was firm. "I don't want to hear anything until you tell me what you want to say. But from now on, don't bother her. I've had enough of this."

As the afternoon wore on, Prarthana's anger grew.
Page 161
Prarthana's life was a mere flutter in the wings of a butterfly, a fleeting moment in the passage of time. There was no longer any reason for her to be anxious; Himavant a stranger to her, a man she had trusted blindly. A letter from him had dispelled all her doubts, and she plagued by worries about him. She no longer feared that he might be traveling to Davenagera with someone else, or eating She had decided that she would be the one to determine everything, from the car he drove to the people he met. It was a necessary deception, a small white lie that she had to tell herself, a reminder be cautious.

As she got dressed to meet Deb, she couldn't help but think about the past. She had been so young and beautiful, and she had thought that she could never be hurt. But life had been cruel, and hurt. naive, so innocent, deceived. But Deb had deceived her, left with a scar that would never heal.

As she put on her shoes, she thought about the way Deb had deceived her. He had given her a pendant, a small token of his love, and she had worn it around her neck. But it was not just a token of love; it was a reminder of the way he had deceived her. She had thought that he was a good man, a man who would never hurt her. But he had hurt her, and she had been left with a wound that would never heal.

As she got into the car, she thought about the way Deb had driven her to Davenagera. He had taken her to the outskirts of the city, to a place where the roads were narrow and winding. She had been so scared, so frightened, and she had thought that she was going to die. But Deb had been calm, so calm, and he had told her that everything would be okay.

As they drove through the city, Prarthana thought about the way Deb had treated her. He had been so kind, so gentle, and he had made her feel like she was the only woman in the world. But it was all a lie, that he had told her to get what he wanted. He had wanted to use her, to exploit her, and he had done just that.

As they approached the outskirts of the city, Prarthana thought about the way Deb had left her. He had abandoned her, left her to fend for herself, and she had been left with nothing. so alone, so scared, thought that she was going to die. But she had survived, learned to be strong.

As they turned onto the main road, Prarthana thought about the way Deb had called her. He "Dear", a term of endearment that he had used to make her feel special. But it was all a lie, told her to get what he wanted. He had wanted to use her, to exploit her, and he had done just that.

As they drove through the night, Prarthana thought about the way Deb had smoked. He had smoked a cigarette, that had been given to him by Deb, and he had inhaled the smoke deeply. He had been so calm, so relaxed, told her that everything would be okay.

As they approached the outskirts of Davenagera, Prarthana thought about the way Deb had treated her. He had been so cruel, so heartless, and he had made her feel like she was nothing. But she had survived, and she had learned to be strong. independent, to be self-sufficient, alone.
Page 162
The thing that only he knew was that the pure passion that existed on his body was only for a moment, and that moment was when he was with Prarthana. Himavant's life was filled with joy because of her love, her madness, and her passion. There was no madness like that of a wild horse, and his boredom was also very dull. He was not like the wind that could be felt everywhere, but he was like a stone that was stagnant. But lately, something had changed. He wouldn't come to the hostel and would say that he had built a house behind the hostel's umbrella, and that he couldn't study in the hostel, so he would study with Prarthana at his house, and if she wanted, she could return to the hostel by 9 pm.

All of this made sense to Prarthana, but when Himavant hesitated to take her on a solo drive to Harihara Road, a wave of suspicion arose in her mind for the first time. That evening, Urmila returned to the room, laughing and joyful, and late, and the smell of cigarettes from her clothes touched Prarthana's anxious heart. "Didi, have you started smoking?" Prarthana asked Urmila, who then went to take a bath. For some reason, Prarthana was reminded of Himavant that evening.

That night, Prarthana couldn't sleep. After Urmila finished her bath and returned, she spread a small mat in the middle of the room, sat in Padmasana, and closed her eyes, muttering something to herself. Then, in a clear voice, she recited the Kanakadhara Stotra for a few minutes. Her hair, which was tied up in a braid, fell on her back, and in the dim light of the night, she looked more beautiful than ever. She was a beautiful girl, taller than Prarthana, but not as fair-skinned. Prarthana's eyes were Urmila's, but Urmila's eyes sparkled with intelligence. Prarthana couldn't help but think about why she couldn't forget Urmila, even though Urmila didn't talk much. In every class in college, Urmila was the first one to be noticed, and it was no secret that many boys were infatuated with her.
Page 163
If she had decided to end it, she could have chosen anyone she wanted, but Urmila's personality was strange, and although everyone knew her, she wasn't close to anyone, perhaps not even to herself. It was Urmila's habit to build a fort around herself, but it wasn't visible to the outside world. Every time she spoke, she seemed extremely carefree, but her mind was closed off. The girl who had introduced her to Debu was Urmila's sister, and their friendship had begun then. Initially, Prarthana had left her at Himavant's room in Shivamogga, and they had spent the entire night at the Forest Lodge, just the two of them, and Urmila was aware of this, not hiding anything.

Thus, the strong friendship between Urmila and Debu was evident, and there was no intimacy between them, which was certain to Prarthana. If had been close, would she have turned to Debu? Was she drawn to his wealth, looks, or intelligence? Prarthana asked herself and replied that she wasn't that kind of girl. In the early days, had Debu been attracted to Urmila's sister? She had asked him once, and he had said, "Look, Prarthana, I'm a big egoist. I expect the girl who loves me to focus only on me and live for me, but Urmila doesn't have the temperament for that. There, two egos would clash, and love wouldn't blossom. I have a crush on Urmila, but she's not my type of girl.

She's a brilliant person, but she's not the kind of girl I'd like to spend my life with. I don't want a girl who would accompany me on a evening stroll, drinking and chatting, like Kaveramma's friend. I'd want as my wife." After that, Debu never spoke about Urmila's sister again. Urmila, too, never showed interest in talking about Debu, just like everyone else. Debu, like others, seemed to admire Urmila's intelligence, and Prarthana wondered if this conversation was still relevant.
Page 164
Debu and Didi, both of them, had not been speaking about their matters, and it seemed like they were not going to break their silence anytime soon. Nuthan was frightened by the way they were behaving, and their actions seemed mysterious to him. Whenever Debu spoke, he would talk as if he had grasped the sky, but he no longer searched for Babu in the hostel like he used to. In the evenings, he wouldn't even provide an explanation for his absence, and if someone asked him where he was, he would say he was at the house behind the Gundichatra.

When Debu went to that house, his madness was terrifying to watch. His love, jokes, and songs had all disappeared, and all that was left was a hunger in his eyes. Nuthan wondered what had happened to Debu and whether he was the same person he used to be. A week ago, Debu had said that if he could escape from his grasp and go out of the house, it would be enough. Debu was not like Himavant, who had a strong body like a rock. Himavant had never been so furious before, and it seemed like he had remembered something from the past.

Debu, on the other hand, was different. He believed that marriage was just a certificate that people obtained from society, and there was no need to postpone today's happiness for the sake of it. He thought that passions should not be tested and that the body, which was made of bones, flesh, and blood, had desires that could not be explained by nonsense like purity and auspicious timings. Debu had said all this while moving towards Nuthan like a flood, and it seemed like his body had melted away like wax. Nuthan had been waiting for such a moment for a long time and had thought that even if it took years, he would eventually take control of Debu.

However, it seemed like Debu had deceived Himavant, and Nuthan had felt a strong urge not to cheat Debu. After a while, Debu had said, "Forgive me, I won't come in until you call me." There was a look of dissatisfaction and frustration on his face, which was evident.
Page 165
If I recall now, it seems that after the eighth incident, Debu started becoming indifferent and began to turn away completely, towards the hostel. Kanva had stopped coming, perhaps because Urmi's behavior had been changing for days. What did it mean? Had the direction of the sun chariot changed? Prarthana had gone somewhere, and at one point, her suspicions had become so intense that in the evening, without calling anyone, she had gone alone and stood in front of the door of the Gundu Chhatri, unexpectedly, with Debu inside: Urmi Didi was perhaps there too? Had the door opened by itself as soon as she stood there? Had Urmi Didi intentionally made her presence known? Was Debu sitting without even wearing a banian? Her mind was extremely disturbed. After standing in front of the door for some time, she had noticed that the door had a lock on it, and she had returned with an answer.

However, by the time she reached the hostel gate, she thought, "Uh-huh, Debu is not someone who gets anxious like that; first, he will roam around in the Mercedes, talk for hours, and bring something or the other to show her, and only when the fragrance of the ripe fruit comes will he be able to bring her to the house behind the Chhatri." If both of them were there, wouldn't they get caught? What would Debu say? Urmi Didi had said that she was not a girl of my taste, right? Whatever Debu's story was, what had Didi said? Had she said, "You've hurt me, so I won't come"? It was unimaginable. It was possible that Didi knew about her relationship with Debu.

The fact that she had walked hand in hand with Debu was known to Didi, just like her relationship with Himavant was well-known, and Debu Babu came to the hostel every day was also known. Even knowing all this, was she going to let her dreams shatter? Anyway, she thought of going to the bath and coming back, and with that thought, she went towards the telephone booth, putting four more steps, and that's when she saw Urmi standing in front of the ice stall, wearing a vest.
Page 166
As she came down the stairs, Prarthana said, "I was thinking of making a phone call, so I stepped out." Urmila's counter-question was sharp and sudden, "Do you still remember the life we had, or have you forgotten?" Prarthana's words were laced with a hint of sadness, "This month, I haven't sent any money, and tomorrow is the mess bill; it needs to be paid." Prarthana's voice trailed off, and after that, Urmila didn't mention Himavant again. Prarthana wondered if she had suspected Deb correctly, but she shouldn't have suspected Urmila.

That same night, after Urmila had gone to bed, Prarthana got up to write a small note that said "I'm sorry, Didi" and place it in one of Urmila's books. As she opened the book, she found a line written by Urmila on the last page, which made her stop in her tracks. The line was clear: "Love hit me!" Prarthana's note had melted away in her hand, and her doubts had begun to swirl. Was Urmila sad because she was losing Deb, or was she had unnecessarily cheated on Himavant? Prarthana couldn't understand. Himavant had never given her any reason to be sad; he had never allowed a third person to come between them. In his small world, she was the only woman, and no other feeling had ever stirred in him.

My Himavant is pure gold, but I'm chasing the junk of gold. Now that I've come so far, can I go back to Himavant after leaving Deb? Is it possible? What if she had committed a mistake by killing her relationship with Deb? made not understanding Deb? She was still the same person she was when she was in Davanagere; her mind was still wavering. If she turned back now, she wouldn't be able to ask for anything, but could she cheat on her second husband for the second time? Maybe Deb was just flirting, but he wasn't a bad person. Urmila, however, was...
Page 167
She might have forgotten that he was her enemy, but if she had sat and talked to him, everything would have been resolved; what was the need to rush things and make a mess of it? A woman has to be patient when it comes to waiting; rejected that patience, it a disaster for the man; even if he had been a little late, what's the big deal, it's not like they were getting married that very day; even if they had gotten married harm? The body made of flesh and blood doesn't understand the language of reason; somehow, she had decided that he was going to be her husband, and she didn't want him to see her as a weak and timid person; whatever happen would happen today, and with that thought, Prarthana got up; in the last page of her diary, she had written: "I love you" - those three words were enough to convey her feelings; if only she had found an explanation for Himavant's behavior, maybe she could have prevented the unfortunate events that were about to unfold; but that didn't happen; Himavant, who had been angry with himself, was like a bird that had flown into a glass window and fallen to the ground; the reason was unknown; he would get up from his bed and start his daily routine without even looking at the sunrise; he would never start his day without watching the sunrise, except when he had a high fever; Prarthana had joined college in Davanagere and had settled down, and it had become a habit for her to get up early in the morning, finish her morning chores, and stand in front of the window, watching the sunrise; when she turned towards the east, it seemed like the sun's rays were shining just for her; she would smile, watching touch the roofs of all the houses in the Shivamurthy Circle area; in those moments, Himavant didn't have any prayers to offer; he wouldn't ask for anything; standing facing the sun, he would make a mental note of the tasks he had to complete that day; by the end of the night, he would have completed everything he had set out to do, and he would get the strength and confidence to do so from the sun itself; after the sun had fully risen, he would come down from the terrace; there was no terrace in the house, but instead, they had set aside space to build a hospital, Prarthana Hospital;
Page 168
As I stood there, my mind wouldn't let me move, it was as if my legs had frozen. Prarthana hadn't come down to the hall as she usually did, and I was worried. I thought to myself, how many days has it been since I've seen her, and this gloom has been following me. I tried to recall the last time I saw her, but my mind was a blank. I thought about how I had decided to skip college that day, and instead, I would go to the hostel's courtyard, where Shantappa would be waiting with his motorcycle. But I didn't feel like it.

By the time Prarthana would leave for college, I would be standing in the courtyard, waiting for her. I would watch her as she walked out, and then I would prostrate myself at her feet, seeking her blessings. But today, I had decided to skip college. I thought about how my mind was preoccupied with thoughts of Prarthana, and how I couldn't think of anything else. she was not just the girl I was going to marry, but she was my everything. Without her, I was nothing. I thought about what Prarthana might be doing at that moment.

At the same time, Prarthana was getting ready for her evening bath. She had been feeling restless since morning, and she couldn't calm her mind. Today was a special day, and she had to look her best. dress up in a way that would impress Debu, make sure she looked perfect. She would wear a dark blue suit, with a deep red rose on her chest, and she would drive a Mercedes. She wouldn't drink anything today, spend the entire night with Debu. She would give him a surprise, and in return, he would give her a gift. Debu would surely be impressed, and he would say only one thing to her - from now on, she was his.

Prarthana stood in the dimly lit bathroom, washing her shoulders. She knew that on the night before her wedding, her mother would bathe her, but she didn't have a mother. Her mother had never been there for her, and she had never experienced the love of a mother. the mother would whisper some secrets to her daughter, but Prarthana to share those secrets with. Her father was busy with his own work, and she was all alone.
Page 169
She was getting ready, but her father wasn't there. They would adorn the girl with flowers at night, and make her wear new clothes. Someone or the other would be muttering something, and with a small bottle of milk in her hand, she would enter the room, and the door would close behind her. Outside, her friends would be laughing, while inside, there was a comforting silence. But she didn't feel any of that. None of them were there for her; she had to close the door behind her herself. If only Himavant were here today, he would take care of all her mistakes. He was like a god to her; everything, even her father and her friends. She knew how to appease god, but she shouldn't have deceived Himavant.

With that thought, Prarthana turned her attention to the new clothes and a new line of thinking. What was the point of all this? She had her god, and today, a wedding would take place. From that midnight moment, he was only hers. In the world of her god, no other woman's shadow would dare to enter. She would envelop him, and then, in their world, there would be no god, only the two of them. When would she tell all this to god? Her mind had made up its mind.

She turned her attention to the layers of her sari, which she was wrapping around her. No matter how hard she tried, a small jasmine flower caught her attention, reminding her of the moment she had descended the hostel stairs, wearing it, in the faint light of evening. Who was she thinking of at that moment? It was Himavant. What was he doing now? He wasn't doing anything. He hadn't even eaten since morning and was walking in the open field, not looking back at his work. Some inexplicable distress was haunting him.

Prarthana lay down on the hospital bed, staring at the stars that had just appeared. She thought someone was walking by and immediately sat up. The figures walking by didn't need an explanation - a man and a woman, probably laborers who had worked in the open field all day, tired and seeking pleasure. Their nerves were taut, stiff, like the strings of a veena, and they were on the verge of madness, beyond all limits, and slowly, gradually, they would loosen up, calm down, and subside like a flood.
Page 170
Neither of them can see each other; they are sitting in the dark, beside a pit that has been dug, and nothing can be seen clearly. But to his mind, everything seems like the world's most beautiful and pure thing, like a man and woman becoming one. Is this not the ultimate truth? Why do we love such people and think that we should be like them? sanctify this act of becoming one, and this daily love? If love is not present, can this act not happen? And if this act love not happen?

The laborer, who was merging with her beside the pit, did he have love for her? Did I have as much love for Prarthana? Just then, a bird, whose name was unknown, flew out from behind a heap of mud, near the pit, making a strange noise and flapping its wings. Himavant became even more dejected. The laborers, wrapping their bodies in their clothes, got up and left without saying a word.

Himavant went to the drum kept in the courtyard, filled with water, and poured some water over his stone-walled house. He thought about what Prarthana might be doing at that moment. He felt a sense of satisfaction after bathing in the well water, which had been drawn up and filled to the brim. Standing on the slab in front of the well, relief after bathing seven or eight times in the cold water.

But then he thought, what is the point of bathing? Does it cleanse the mind or just the body? If it's then everyone who is alive would bathe after someone dies, to cleanse themselves of the impurity. Does bathing really Even after a funeral, men and women bathe and then go to the temple. Is this bathing just a ritual? Why am I bathing like this, drawing water from the well? The water that I am pouring over my body is only cleansing my body, not my mind.

As soon as this thought occurred to him, he poured the last pot of water over himself and threw the pot down on the ground. He then walked back to his house, pushing open the creaky door. The door creaked like the door of Ravana's palace in the epic, and just like that, his house opened. Urmila, whose name he had just learned, was standing in the courtyard, her face pale.
Page 171
For the first time, Urmi had knocked on the door, and Debu was nervous; his lips had dried up, and his fingertips felt a slight tremble, as if his entire being was aware of the significance of this moment. Was he really in such a predicament? Certainly, Debu was a lone hunter in his pursuits, and when it came to his conquests, he never involved anyone else. Whether it was Kasuti Kaveramma's house or any other, he would go alone, and his friends would only be called upon to share in the revelry, to eat, drink, and make merry together. However, when it came to this particular hunt, he had kept it entirely to himself.

If the girl wanted, she could be like Kasuti Kaveramma, a perfect, ideal woman, but for Debu, she was the only one who mattered. There were a thousand stories about him in college, and if he had set his sights on the new girl in town, it was only a matter of time before he would make his move. He would take her on a drive in the evening, to dinner at night, and by morning, she would be sitting in the library, a picture of innocence. By midday, they would both be lost in their own little world, and the corridors of the college would be abuzz with the news of their romance.

But Debu was being extremely cautious this time, more so than ever before. He could lie to any girl on the street, tell her a hundred falsehoods, and even narrate his past conquests in vivid detail. However, he couldn't afford to get caught in such a situation, not now, not in this state. The girl knocking on the door was not just anyone; she was Urmi, and she wouldn't tolerate another woman in his life. She continued to knock and it was clear that she couldn't be turned away.

Debu realized that he couldn't just send her back from the doorstep, and with a sense of certainty, he got up, put on his favorite navy blue t-shirt, and the light brown cotton pajamas that were lying nearby. He ran his hands through his hair, adjusting it to perfection, and despite feeling a bit disheveled, he made his way to the door. He glanced at the girl, who was still knocking, and raised an eyebrow, as if to ask who it could be. He caught Urmi's eye and signaled to her to keep quiet, and just then, she called out to him in a loud voice, "Debu!"
Page 172
"Give me two minutes," he said, helping the girl put on her clothes. She had come wearing a saree, of all things; stupid girl. It would have been better if he had sent her away in the afternoon. Even called her over at night, no disaster would have occurred. Besides, where was the difference between day and night for the girls of Kaveramma’s house? But Kaveramma had been in a rush. The girl wasn't from here; she was from Mangaluru, and had to catch the night bus back. "Send her back by seven-thirty or eight," she had insisted. Her anxieties were always of this sort. But what had possessed him? Instead of having her brought all the way here, wouldn't it have been better if he had gone to Kaveramma’s house himself? For him, that wasn’t a forbidden place to visit; in this one aspect of life, nothing was taboo for him. But what had been aroused today was not mere desire. It was not just lust. It was someone who had suddenly risen and manifested from deep within him; someone he could probably never suppress—the flirtatious Debu inside him!

Ever since he left the hostel and rented this house located in the row behind Gundi Choultry, his mind had been restless. How different was he from a debauched husband whose mind yearns to bring another woman home by evening the very moment his wife leaves for her parents’ house? There was absolutely no difference between them. He had personally overseen the decoration of this rented house. Curtains here, a vase there; on the table, a photograph of himself, underneath which was written in a small hand: *Call me by my name*; books on the desk; on the wall, a picture of a semi-naked woman whose navel had been depicted as an ashtray; Poet Tagore's portrait a bedspread with light pink patterns on the bed; a half-wall-high full-length mirror staring directly at richly fragrant air freshener in the bathroom, and on its wall, a picture of two angry cobras standing erect and locked in an embrace. He knew this much: no girl who walked into this room would ever return with her saree uncrumpled.

But the women from Kaveramma’s house didn't care for any of this. They wanted a man, and he had to be generous. If he pulled out a bottle of champagne from his liquor cabinet and poured it for them, there were no better companions than these women. But he required ambience. Debu was not a ravenous glutton who devoured his food in a hurry; his was a royal hunt. He had ensured that every tiny detail of the room was exactly as he desired. He had searched far and wide to bring back four or five varieties of orchids, placing them in small pots. From the day he rented it until today, he had not allowed a single friend to set foot inside this house. Those who entered this house...
Page 173
Himavant thought to himself, "Let them go, Prarthana! And Urmi too. They are crying there, both of them, with him, and are wandering together. But they have left this house, knowing that they would not be able to bear the hardships of life. They have not even been able to withstand the burden of their own clothes. both of them, without even being weight And he, too, has not been their love. He has love, and also the weight of their expectations. He has to be patient, believe. At every stage, defeated, and defeated, until he finally achieves the ultimate victory, with the help of all those defeats. surrender everything that belongs to him; go barefoot, anywhere, without any belongings; he has to become a complete failure, and only then will he be able to achieve the ultimate victory. And that is what Debu had thought to himself, again and again, and had been defeated, He had been confined to this room for only a few months, but he had not been able to record even a single moment of happiness in this room. with his beautiful and intelligent friends, and they had played together, and laughed together, and had spent their days together, but that had not brought him any real happiness. It was just a fleeting pleasure, a momentary joy, which had not been able to satisfy his soul. And that was why he had been defeated, again and again.

He had been thinking of calling his friends, and had even made a phone call to Kaveramma's house, but had not been able to muster the courage to talk to her.
Page 174
Urmila, standing at the threshold, gasped in shock and leaned against the wall. The woman at the doorway, Urmila, stood there, her eyes intense, a line of sweat trickling down her forehead. No, the woman from Mangalore didn't dare to look at Urmila’s face properly. Debu, slumped in his chair, had turned into a statue. What a tragic silence had descended upon the small house’s hall. It felt as if anyone of the three spoke, it wouldn't be a mere word, but would explode like a bomb. Leaning against the wall, after looking at the tall figure, she looked at Debu once. "A man? You're the one she loves? This disgust wells up from the depths of my being. Urmi, I want you. My dealings are always straightforward. I don't have the patience to build dreams of love and wait. You are my love-time friend. A small encounter is enough for such dealings to be over. If I had asked directly, whether I would have agreed to be with you or not, that's a different matter. But listen, I'll tell you the truth: the kind of disgust I feel for you now, it definitely wouldn't have surfaced then. I told you that at the Forest Lodge gate. If you still want me, then let it all happen. But what did you say that day? until yesterday afternoon? Is it your deceit or self-deceit, you tell me, Debu. You call it love, affection. You say you’ve stayed with me, dying and living. You say your sincere love for me hasn't died even after ten years. Now tell me, Prarthana is dying. You say it's just to hurt me. Even after looking at you with such intense eyes, you expect me to believe you and your words?" Her angry words flowed from one sentence to another. Urmila felt something was burning inside her, she didn't know what. If she had been any more patient, perhaps she would have managed to bear everything. But the doorbell rang. A man. Urmila couldn't bear to look at the girl’s helpless face. Debu couldn't muster the courage face, let alone the Mangalore woman he had called over. She must have had a drink. It’s been at most two hours since she arrived.
Page 175
She has mastered speech; song; laughter; nakedness; applause; grandeur! She might be a professional but, at the end of it all, there was a look of admiration on her face about her individuality, about the grandeur she displayed; a kind of thanks in her eyes. While Urmila stood watching, hurling abuses at him, she would now glance at him as if to say, "Oh, is that all?" This was someone she didn't know, not a lover, and she wouldn't have any occasion to meet her again. But a look of contempt was being directed at her from her eyes? If she could see it in her eyes, her ego wouldn't recover for another seven lives. It started to feel like it would be enough if she left as soon as possible. Exactly at that moment, she, [Madam, if I've made a mistake, I'm sorry] "I'm so sorry. We are professionals; we have to go to town at night. If you give me my money, I'll leave." she said. A clear wave of disgust swept across Urmila's face. Debu slowly rose from the chair. The darkness of the dead evening had already descended upon the hall. He took out his wallet from the pocket of the trousers hanging in the kitchen where he had lit the lamp and silently counted ten notes of a hundred rupees, giving them to the Mangaluru girl. The girl, without saying a word, picked up her vanity bag from the side of the wall and walked out of the house into the dim light. Had she taken two steps or not, she stopped abruptly. A figure was standing in the passage in front It was clear from its posture that it had been standing there for quite some time, but the Mangaluru girl who had come out of the house didn't know the name of the figure standing there was Prarthana. She bowed her head and left. Even after she departed, Urmila's loud voice continued to be heard from inside the house. For Prarthana, who had stopped in the passage opposite the house without knowing what was happening inside Debu Babu's house the moment she arrived, as Urmila's voice began to drift from inside, one by one, the details started to become clear. Deb Babu was not alone in his house. Urmila had also just arrived. Deb then either; he had been caught with someone else. She didn't need anyone to tell her what a pathetic state a man caught like that would be in, because, at an age when she didn't even understand what a man was, such a man had fallen into her hands, his name was Appa! Her hatred for men had begun from that day. If another such man had entered her life, perhaps she would have started hating men permanently? But
Page 176
He was Himavant. God. God does not stoop to wickedness, so Debu, who was enrolled, did not want to become God. At the very least, he becomes a human being, that's what Debu has done. All the goodness in Himavant was not even in Debu. Debu, who had thought so, got caught in this situation. It was not her, it was her. She had listened carefully to every word Urmila had spoken. who was suffering. Besides, the wandering was not for her. Debu loved Deedi, to hurt her! To have her through Urmi. She had to go inside the house and curse Debu's face, scold him. A man! Does Himavant have such compassion? Why should she think he was quiet? But Urmi? All men have it. Even when she knew her bond with Debu had been formed, what happened to Urmi? Why did she have to step between them? Perhaps Debu was not there before; it was I who stepped in. Though there was a clear indication, my friendship was not fruitful without haste, and in vain, Urmila's indifference broke their bond. Finding Urmila uninterested, Debu's interest, without Urmila even knowing, made a hasty decision. It was I who wandered, thinking I had left. She should not have done that. It felt like they had left Debu behind. That's enough. With a single event, all her illusions shattered. Everything for him. Debu's money, intelligence, songs, words - let them both be in one place. Let Urmila keep her seniority. Somewhere it feels like after all this, she should be made to forget herself for different reasons. Seeing all this, will she still be remembered like a mother for a child who has lost her way in their relationship? She pressed her forehead tightly and stood firmly in the passage, looking at the wall opposite. His love seemed to flow. This Debu is a lie, a big lie. Urmi her concern "Himavant, they are all like you. You too must weep! I am the one who left, forgive me, O God, my earnest request." Prayer? What forgiveness? After committing betrayal, in what voice does Prarthana's mind speak? "What do I do now?" Debu was not there, as if he had always been there. It had come to her lips on its own. The courtyard in front was empty. There was a small light coming from inside the house.
Page 177
"Please tell me why," Prarthana requested. "Now what should I do?" "Whatever a girl would do, I would do the same. Ugh, Prarthana, I have just vomited. What you can do is the same." "You have the strength to recover from something like this faster than me. You are younger than me. I won't force you. In half an hour... I will wait in my room for you. Let's finish this today, both of us. Don't delay, come quickly!" she said, speaking in one breath as she stormed out of Debu's room like a whirlwind. She moved towards the street. While Prarthana was standing in the darkness of the passage at that moment, a slight shock was registered on her face, apart from that, only disgust towards Debu spread across her eyes. She did not even wait for Prarthana at the turn of the street. She found an auto and told it to go towards the city. Though it felt to her like she had become more anxious than 'necessary', she confirmed throughout the auto ride that every word she had spoken had been carefully weighed. As soon as she entered a STD booth near Chetana Hotel, she dialed Kaveramma's house number. "Everything happened as I thought, Kaveramma. Thank that girl. I will send the money tomorrow. I will come and see you sometime later. But remember what I said well. None of this should reach Himavant. Swear it on me," she said. From the other side, a voice of consent was heard. After that, Urmila reached the hostel and sat waiting for Prarthana, who had not even bathed or eaten. Everyone had performed their roles, unknowingly, with utmost perfection. The girl from Vanamaluru who had gone to Debu's house had no inkling. If caught with a man, how would they behave if they were not police or from that profession, she behaved exactly like that. The moment she left for Debu's house in the afternoon, Kaveramma had sent a message to the hostel for the girl from Vanamaluru to be called by Debu, and to inform Urmila about the matter, and finally, that none of this matter should reach Himavant.
Page 178
That it was all connected to Urmila's warning that one should not be hasty, Prarthana understood. Debu, standing at the door, was oblivious to the greatest calamity of his life. Without realizing it, Prarthana had arrived at his doorstep with perfect timing. Everything had happened exactly as she had planned. She had orchestrated it all, ensuring that no detail that Debu had conceived of was missed. He had behaved like a lecherous man, just like a "typical flirt," without any deviation from the script. Everything had unfolded exactly as she had envisioned. Now, Prarthana could not proceed further with Debu. As she thought this, returning to her Himavant, a picture flashed before Urmila's eyes: Himavant, sitting alone in the open field of Gadikoppa, his eyes wide with a thousand expectations, on the mound of earth where he had planned to build a hospital for Prarthana. And for some reason, she felt a pang of sorrow! Unbeknownst to Urmila at that very moment, everything had slipped from her control and the event that unfolded was this: Prarthana knocking on the door in the middle of the night. And Debu opening the door!

He stood frozen like a stone in the passage in front of the house. Suddenly, Prarthana seemed to come back to reality. It would have been better if Urmila didi had stood at Debu's front door for a little longer and verbally abused him. Standing the house, she would have found some time to recover from the shock of seeing the Mangalore girl walking away with her bag in hand. Was she scolding him? Was she taunting digging up Debu's very existence and burying it? In any case, Urmila stood there for that long. She was right in front of me. I couldn't see her, but the feeling that Urmila didi was there guarded me, waited for me. But for myself, there was only darkness now. There was a lamp inside the house, Debu was there, but there was no light, no street to see. I knew; a guilt that would bring a grimace to his face would be floating on his face, the kind of disgust that floats on the face of a man caught in bed with a prostitute. Not just that, but also the disgust she felt towards herself for the relationship she had with Urmila. It seemed as though every relationship he had wanted to establish had been exposed.
Page 179
He would not forgive this in his life. More than that, find the affection in the eyes of this girl, Prarthana, directed towards him in this lifetime. For the first time, a storm brewed within Prarthana. She had no need to repent for it. Her life until now was a curse. To offer herself to him with so much affection, and he was lying there, rolling around with Urmi. With two women! With Didi... "Damn Debu," she thought, it was impossible to stand there any longer. She turned towards home, not looking back even once as if for the last time, and walked back into the dark passage, heading towards the street. If she had looked back, perhaps the directions of her life and Debu's life would have changed. Because Debu, who had suddenly come to the door, watched Prarthana walking away with shock. His sharp eyes were fixed on the bareness of her back, not covered by her blouse, and in those eyes, an age-old desire was yawning. "So, will she come back?" Debu said to himself. "Urmi will never come to my room, to my evenings, to my proximity, again. Because she saw me with that Mangalore woman. She is possessive. She will never forgive me. But Prarthana is not like that. She might have confirmed that the Mangalore girl was with me. heard every word Urmi cursed me with. She might even know about my relationship with Urmi. Still, she will come back. Because she is emotional. The sulking of emotional girls doesn't last long. She cannot live without me. At this point, she won't go back to Himavant either. Prarthana is helpless." Thinking this, he went back inside and locked the door. The lingering effects of the champagne he drank in the afternoon still felt in his lungs. Such evenings call for whisky. He took a bottle of whisky from the liquor cabinet. As he gulped down the first sip, Prarthana had reached the turn of the road in front of the gate. Had half an hour passed? Didi would be waiting in the room. But for him, time seemed to have stopped. It was like a lightning bolt striking the fortress of her innocent feelings. Two years ago, in the village house, with her sister-in-law, seeing her father in that state...
Page 180
"that shock." Even then, seeing the innocent, her heart became so emotional that today not just the strings of the veena of her mind, but even the strings snapped at once. Himavant was her support. How many years have passed since then? What a wonderful time it was, days passed by without her realizing it. As she thought this, Prarthana's mental composure was lost. This is the right punishment for you, Prarthana. This is a curse. This is Himavant's curse. A curse for his goodness. your deception. Even then, the time spent with Debu, what a wonderful time you think it was? Is there anyone more deceitful than you? For these very two years, Himavant toiled, his body drenched in sweat and blood for your sake. You don't even remember it. Is there forgiveness for someone like you? Her mind turned upside down. I don't want any of this. There's always a bus to Shivamogga. Should I return to Himavant? She thought. That small room, the terrace that could be reached by climbing onto the window ledge, the stars in the sky, the stone bench on the bank of the reservoir. Himavant's sweat. Everything came back to her. She recalled them one by one. Her mind felt immense joy. But, no. The attraction didn't ignite. Her mind wasn't moving towards Shivamogga. She knew; if she went there, Himavant would embrace her with a thousand arms. No matter her condition, no matter what she had become, he wouldn't tell her to leave that small room. "You are still young. Younger than me. I have to correct you. nurture you. Study diligently for just three years, Prarthana, become a doctor, and come back. I will stand at the grand door of my dreams and wait for you," he would say. But what is the need to go there? Debu wouldn't know. Even if he knew she had returned to Himavant, it wouldn't hurt him. He wouldn't get angry. Debu knows better than me that I worship Himavant, but I don't love him. He will be at peace. Debu shouldn't you bastard. Flirting isn't just for you. Don't you know I can do it too? Right here in Davanagere, in this same college, I will walk around with my arms around another man's shoulder right in front of your eyes. Let blood flow from your eyes. If such fickleness disturbs the harmony of companionship, let me also understand what its consequences will be. Bringing a woman from Mangalore... it hurt me.
Page 181
did not feel like a companion. What do I know about the desires of a male body? I am just a body from Mangalore, only flesh; you can adorn it as you wish. I have no sorrow, desire dies very quickly, but you should not have loved Urmi. I cannot regain my composure without teaching you a lesson in return; not sometime later, but today, right now. Deciding so, as if driven by madness or impulse, she walked briskly down Balaji street and entered a telephone booth. There was no need to find the boys' hostel number. But she didn't know his room number. What's in the number? "I am Prarthana speaking... Is Sujoy, Debu's roommate, there? Sujoy Malhotra? Could you please call him urgently?" she blurted out. For a moment, she couldn't explain her own behaviour. Why Sujoy Malhotra above all else? Had her mind, without her knowing, ever thought of him before? No chance. Her mind was drowned in thoughts of Debu. Sujoy never even crossed her mind. If he started talking about Sujoy, she would dismiss him with, "Talk about something else, Debu." He might be handsome, true, but she had never, ever thought of Sujoy in this manner. So why did she tell them to call him at the boys' hostel? For some reason, a victorious smile seemed to play on Prarthana's lips. If she wanted all of Debu's peace, pride, and happiness to be shattered, it had to be him, his closest friend. A boy who considered Debu superior in everything. Debu was not as rich as him; not as intelligent as him. His stature was also not noteworthy. His appearance was also nothing to write home about. But watch, Debu, I will defy you and walk arm in arm with someone inferior to you. The whole college will laugh at you. What did I say? Are you not a master flirt? You cunning fellow, who probes into Urmi Didi's inner self while appeasing me. Do you think I cannot do what you did? Watch. As soon as she finished speaking, a voice from the other side said, "Sujoy here." "Come immediately. I know you don't have a car. Come on the bike. Today I am in a very good mood. The faster you drive on the Harihara road, the better company you'll be. I need a drink. Please, Sujoy."
Page 182
Prarthana said that day, "Prarthana, if anything happens to Debu..." Sujay shivered. "But..." she interrupted, pressing the cruise control button. "You come with me." Just as she was about to return, she thought, "Oh, he's going to the dhaba with the boot owner." "What a coincidence, isn't it? I'm my roommate Debu." Prarthana, who had just experienced something with Sujay, began to enjoy it. Within a surprising stride, Sujay, dressed in a dark t-shirt, white pants, and a Pathani suit, appeared before her on his motorcycle. There was surprise in his eyes. She quickly got on the seat behind the motorcycle and held Sujay's back with her right hand. She felt it would have been better if she had worn a churidar instead of a saree today. As Sujay's motorcycle moved slowly towards the Pune road, the wind blowing said, "Sujay took a bath a little while ago. Some powder is emanating from his back. He smells like a newly bathed elephant. Humming some old song while driving..." She held his shoulder even tighter. Debu felt like she was groaning somewhere far away. It wasn't a joke. She hadn't brought Sujay out tonight as a mere excuse to hurt Debu. If Debu could roll around with that Mangalorean body all afternoon, she too could sit clinging to Sujay's back until they reached the dhaba. If Urmila and Debu could roam around in the Mercedes at an odd hour, drink wine with Sujay at the dhaba, looking into his eyes. "May this night truly be a happy one. Let Debu die! Debu, goodbye," she told herself. Prarthana was even more pleased to see four couples from their college "Tomorrow morning, Debu will get the news!" She called the dhaba boy and ordered herself. Sujay was reluctant to drink whiskey, but he was not a man of few words. She ordered a gin for herself and started the conversation. "Why did you remember me today? Where is Debu?" Sujay asked. "He is with Urmila," she said, her voice devoid of warmth. "Oh, is that why you remembered me?" Sujay smirked.
Page 183
You've always been fond of me, Vruti, and you've always wanted to know about my relationship with music; discuss it with me. But I never thought that music would be so captivating for you, and yet, a bit apathetic about it. You've never shown any interest in it, and whenever I've tried to talk to you about it, you've always seemed disinterested. But now, I've come to realize that you're not just disinterested, you're actually very passionate about music.

You said to me, "I've always liked you, Vruti. I've always wanted to talk to you about music." And I said to you, "I've about music, but I never thought you'd be interested." And "I'm very interested, but I've never had the courage it."

I remember that night when we were at the Shivamogga house, and you were drinking your third whiskey. You were looking at me with a mixture of sadness and anger in your eyes, and you said to me, "I've always liked you, Vruti. I've always wanted to talk to you about music." And I said to you, "I've about music, but I never thought you'd be interested."

You started crying, and I could see the pain in your eyes. You said to me, "I've never had the courage to talk to you about music because I've always been afraid of what you'd think." And I said to you, "I've always thought that you were interested in music, but I never thought you'd be passionate about it."

You started sobbing, and I could see the tears streaming down your face. You said to me, "I've always loved music, Vruti. I've always wanted to talk to you about it, but I've never had the courage." And I said to you, "I've about music, but I never thought you'd be interested."

As I was watching you cry, I felt a pang of guilt in my heart. I realized that I had been wrong to assume that you were not interested in music. you were passionate about it, and that you had been afraid to talk to me about it because of what I might think.

I took your hand in mine, and I said to you, "I'm sorry, Vruti. I had no idea that you were passionate about music." And you said to me, "I'm sorry too, Vruti. I've always wanted to talk to you about it, but I've never had the courage."

As we sat there, holding hands, I felt a sense of connection with you that I had never felt before. I realized that we had been missing out on so much by not talking to each other about our passions.

And then, I heard a noise coming from outside. It was a faint rustling sound, and it seemed to be coming from the direction of the house. I looked at you, and I saw that you were looking at me with a mixture of fear and confusion in your eyes.

"What was that?" you asked me, your voice
Page 184
When I, who had left, returned before midnight, why should anyone else not have returned from here? - God forbid that doesn't happen, she thought, knocking on the door even more firmly. "Deb," Prarthana said as the latch inside the door clicked. "Oh... Come in, Prarthana," Deb opened the door. The dim blue light that was burning in the house was enough to determine that no one else was inside. Without even bothering to reply to his exclamation, Prarthana walked in. Deb, closing the door behind her, followed. "What is this... like this so late at night?" Deb kept asking in surprise. She didn't answer anything. She knew Deb took a bath in the evenings. At least lukewarm water would be left in the geyser. Thinking that the water would heat up in ten minutes if she switched it on again, Since it was clear she wouldn't speak for a while, Deb also gave up those questions and sat quietly on a chair in the hall. By then, the initial surprise of why she had come so suddenly had passed, and he was sure that Urmile would not return. But his hope that Prarthana would not be able to leave him came true, and Deb's mind, like Chandranath Bandyopadhyay's lustful eyes, began to see a new desire. But what explanation could be given for the incident that happened in the evening? Deb began to think. "Deb Babu, I'll take a bath and come. Fix a drink for me, I have a lot to talk about," Prarthana said, pulling the bathroom door shut from the inside. She began to wonder about him. He is a man. He must be doing a thousand things. A pure adulterer. Besides, has Deb ever hidden anything from her regarding the affairs at Kaveramma's house? Where did he claim to be a person of immense restraint when it came to women? Of course, his behavior with Urmile might have been wrong, and at that moment it seemed unforgivable. But what had she done? Himavant first, then Deb, then Sujay at night! Where has she come to and reached? This day's experience was terrible, like Bhagiratha. It's been about one and a half years since she met Deb, such a petty adulterer. But with her, he has never...
Page 185
He hadn't thought that there could be such a wicked demon within Sujay. While pouring lukewarm water over her body felt soothing, standing naked under the dim light of the room, she clearly saw the marks on her body that looked like they were stained with blood. For some reason, tears welled up again. As if shedding the sins of countless births, Prarthana finished her sinful bath. That evening, she didn't want to touch the sari and blouse she had worn. Wearing one of Debu's t-shirts, brought from his wardrobe, and tying a lungi with small checks around her waist, she came out of the bathroom, wrapping Debu's towel around her wet hair. It felt as if all of Sujay's taint had washed away and Debu had completely enveloped her body. The bitterness of the evening also seemed to have melted away on its own. Most importantly, she didn't remember Himavant even once. However, as she came out of the bathroom and entered the living room, seeing Debu's condition, she stood there with a broken heart. It was dark when she entered the house; he hadn't noticed her. Now she noticed that Debu's eyes were swollen like blood balls. He had been crying all evening. He was still crying; his face was full of the wrinkles of remorse. In just a few hours, Debu had become a living corpse. The chest hair on his chest lay helplessly under his soaked shirt. He hadn't eaten or drunk anything since the evening. He sat hunched over, like a stone. On the teapoy in front of him was a bottle of gin, and next to it a bowl of lemon cordial; but Debu refused to drink. "You have kept only one vessel for me, haven't you? Prarthana, I won't even ask for your forgiveness. Until this moment, I haven't been able to forgive myself. No one can care more about me than I do about myself. I am not just a flirt. I am lecherous! When I was with that girl from Udupi, the insult of Urmila arriving didn't remain with me. When Urmila stood at the door, scolding and reviling me, I wasn't even sad that you heard it. But how could I hurt a girl like you so much? No excuse; I left thinking I would break Urmi's pride, and without reason, I rejected and insulted the one who had humiliated me, take revenge by mentally destroying her. Somewhere, seeing my closeness with her, you..."
Page 186
I made the foolish mistake of thinking you would be around forever. But now, Prarthana, you must leave. Don't let yourself be bothered by what happened with a stranger from Mangalore, someone whose name I don't even know. It’s a habit that can be broken any day. But I regret hurting you in this matter… I hate myself.”

As soon as he spoke, Urmila covered her face and began to sob. His hard exterior seemed to melt away. For a while, Prarthana didn't say anything. She took a new blanket from the cupboard and spread it on the bed. Wiping herself with the towel she was wrapped in, she swept her hair back and tied it in a small knot. She then took a bindi from where it was stuck to the bathroom door and placed it on her forehead. Even on such a confusing night, a flicker of pride enveloped her, the thought that her full arms, chest, and glistening neck looked incredibly attractive in a dark t-shirt. She poured gin and lemon cordial into a glass, pulled a chair in front of the deity, and sat down. For some reason, she suddenly felt everything was peaceful, calm, and under control. Though it felt slightly uncomfortable to sit in a lungi as she wasn't used to it, she relaxed, thinking that the one in front of her was her deity, wasn't he? No, Debu didn't even lift his eyes; he looked like a wounded bird. Remorse was eating him alive. He seemed to have fallen into an unsolvable sorrow. I had thought that he might have been shattered by Urmila's taunts that day. I thought he would be reeling from the whirlwind of humiliation. I thought his masculinity wounded. But what is likely consuming Debu is a psychological torment. The regret of having hurt someone like you. Prarthana looked at him with an inexplicable affection. "I did hurt you. It’s true I might have neglected you for a few days without my knowledge, and that's also true. But I never betrayed you, Prarthana. Even in Urmila's matter, I set out to break her pride, not to betray her and celebrate. I have never suppressed my bodily desires. But, I swear on the person I love most in the world, I never yearned for your body to be mine.
Page 187
I never felt the urge to rush through life; I had bowed my head to your feet, remember? I desired that a part of you, completely, should be mine. Wanting is not wrong, but I did not suppress my other selfish desires. The one who should have opened his heart to the waterfall, for the sake of a nameless Mangaluru girl, is regretting it. I am not ashamed of that, but Urmila, have I hurt you? For that, what forgiveness is there, Prarthana? Debu asked in a voice where tears were streaming profusely from his eyes like fiery embers. Prarthana, having finished her second peg, set down the glass. The wax had melted completely. Using the teapoy in front of her, she went and stood very close to Debu. She gently caressed his disheveled hair. "Debu Babu, men should not cry. Did you truly love me that intensely? I am asking for the last time; answer me honestly just once. I have come here very hurt," she said. Time stood still in the room. Debu did not speak immediately. For a moment, he stared intently into the eyes of the woman clinging to him. In a small pool of tears, the swing of his life was rocking. Only the last particle of wax remained to melt. "I will never ask for your forgiveness, Prarthana. But by the child I might have someday, I loved you, I love you, I will love you till the end. Even after your rejection, I will keep loving you. Only you." Debu's words ended. Prarthana hugged him tightly around her waist like a baby and began to sob uncontrollably. The tears that had been held back, which had erupted on the Harihara road and chased her into the darkness of the passage in front of Debu's house, now poured down like a great storm. "You are the one who should be forgiven, Debu. I was doubted your love. decided to teach you a lesson. standing at the door, believed all the angry words of Urmila Didi and became stubborn. called Sujay. took him to the dhaba where we spent hundreds of evenings together. Where was that infatuation? In which bad cell of my body did it hide? Facing you, I said, 'I love you, man!' and sat behind him on the bike. Forgive me, Debu! Sujay is not a human being."
Page 188
"This is the right punishment. I shouldn't have lied to Debu, whom I love more than my life. From now on, I will never doubt you. I will not leave your shadow, move away from you. No, don't make my love seem like a lie. Please forgive me." Prarthana was still crying, but Debu was turning to stone. It felt as if the flirt in him had died right then and there. The figure of Sanjay Malhotra became clearer in his eyes. He realized that some things a woman should never reveal to her husband, in the dead of night, in a drunken stupor, in her eagerness to prove her love for Debu, more than her life. That innocent girl, Prarthana, had blurted it out. She thought she had unburdened herself, but she had become hollow. Without even a slight apprehension that the confession of that night would torment her until the last moment of her life, Prarthana had fallen into a deep sleep in Debu's embrace. In her state, there was no possibility of her digesting the colorless alcohol she had drunk and continuing the conversation. Debu himself gently carried the girl who his embrace and laid her on the bed. At that moment, he stood by the bedside, staring at Prarthana's face. He felt as if he had destroyed the innocence that was present in every line, every movement of her face. On her arm, on the slope of her chest, there were blood-red marks. They were the fingerprints of Sanjay Malhotra. For the first time in his life, he felt he had lost. He drank the remaining gin in the bottle without adding water, gulping it down his throat. It wouldn't be difficult to knock on the door in a while. After all, he was from the same room. Should he just go and kill him and come back? Debu began to feel disgusted. He was in a daze, not fully conscious. His clothes were scattered. After a year and a half of dealing with him, his body was broken. She was not even in a condition to protest. Just surrender to him. She would agree. Wasn't that what he had been waiting for all these days? Debu started to rage.
Page 189
But Sujay Malboh's memory killed his desires. Forget about getting married; his mind didn't even dare to touch her. The rest of the night was completely empty. Somewhere outside the house, the third watch turning. He covered Prarthana for the night with a blanket and lay down on a mat on the floor. Just as sleep was about to take hold, he suddenly sat up. "What exactly did Sujay Malboh do to you?" he asked in a clear voice, but it felt more like he was asking himself than Prarthana. Without the slightest inkling that in his future life, he would have to wake up like this in the third watch of many nights and wear himself out by repeatedly asking this same question, Debashish Chandrakant Bandopadhyay had fallen asleep that day. A different answer, completely unrelated to his question, had enveloped his inner being by morning. "No, this is not adultery; it's not flirting. If I hadn't loved Prarthana with all my heart, this fact that she rode on a bike embracing him would not have bothered me so much; I been so wounded. Why any doubts about this?" I love Prarthana. And love Prarthana. Debu's lust, Debu the flirt, from last night... died! Just as he decided this, Prarthana came out from the inner room. The day was shining.

Prarthana's eyes were serene. The anxieties of the night had vanished. After her bath, wearing Debu's lungi and t-shirt again, she came out of the bathroom with her wet hair falling down her back. She was an absolute nymph. Debu was staring at her. She wasn't exceptionally clever. She had just enough intelligence to get into a medical course. There was no sparkle of immense wisdom in her eyes, but there were the innocent charms of love. Prarthana was not a girl with very peculiar tastes, but she sang beautifully.
Page 190
The admiration of people is not like that of a god; but one in a lakh might find someone who loves as intensely as Prarthana. Prarthana is not wealthy. But she has enough wealth to overcome her poverty. someone who blossomed in the culture of her Bengali family. But her goodness is greater than any culture. She has no qualities that are intensely repulsive or objectionable. She loves him enough to go away, but not so possessive that it suffocates him to death. Does he truly love her? Enough to marry her? No chance. A girl marrying someone as handsome, intelligent, and wealthy as Debu cannot be as ordinary as Prarthana. She must have unimaginable attractions, such a charm. Does Urmila have that? Absolutely not. The very memory of her is bitter. She might be intelligent but not as empathetic as Prarthana. Whatever charm Urmila possesses is suited for a lover, not necessarily for a wife. Being good is enough. But in his life, the word "lover" has no place anymore. He will never flirt with anyone The reddish scars that appeared on Prarthana’s body, the tight grip of Sujoy Malhotra, are enough. The flirt inside him has died suddenly. He has no other desires now. He is content if a girl like Prarthana, who loves and adores him more than God, walks with him till the end, never insults him, and has a calm personality, becomes his wife. Thus, his mind had decided. But will he find another girl like Prarthana? Or will he marry Prarthana herself? Debu was confused. The many events that transpired in the last twenty-four hours had left him shattered. How much had he drunk yesterday? It felt as if all the strength had drained from his body. Urmila barging in when he was with a girl from Mangalore, her cruel words, Prarthana overhearing all of it, him suddenly feeling insignificant in front of both of them, and then Prarthana returning at midnight with injuries on her body, explaining the words of his fleeting desire Sujoy Malhotra – it all began to make him feel cornered. From the day he started chasing women until now, he had never been this vulnerable. He, who loved a girl like him, after all, a boy like Sujoy Malhotra, on the road to Harihara...
Page 191
If she could take her body and go with someone in the middle of the night like that—what is the meaning of the word 'love'? What is the meaning of freedom? What is the meaning of Prarthana's honesty, her devotion, her faith, her goodness? If she was truly good and honest, why did she leave Himavant, who loved her so deeply, and come to me? And after coming to me, why did she as easily go with Sujay in the middle of the night on the dark road of Harihar? Prarthana is not honest! She also has different faces, and innocence is just one of them, which can change at any time. I boasted that I won this innocent girl, that I snatched her from Himavant and triumphed. I thought I won a girl like her. But the one who got ruined was me! I got defeated in front of an ordinary guy like Sujay. Prarthana might give excuses for this: 'Because you wandered on the Harihar road with Urmi, I also sat on your roommate Sujay's bike, hugging him. Let's end it there. My true love is only for you, Debu Babu. Forget what happened.' But does the mind forget? A man might be a great adulterer, he might bring an adulterous woman to satisfy his afternoon lust, but as long as they are together, he expects even that adulterous woman to look nowhere else and be loyal to him alone. In such a case, can I ever forget the brown wounds on Prarthana's body? After all that, will I be able to marry her and be happy? What has Prarthana done! She has shattered the most delicate dream of my life. He sat staring at the wall with empty eyes. "Get up, Huzoor. Go take a bath quickly. You didn't even eat dinner last night. Your stomach must be burning. Let's go out, just the two of us, eat something, and spend an excellent afternoon together. Today I will sing all the songs I kept for you... Only for you," Prarthana pulled him up by his arm. As Debu was bathing, she stood in front of the full-length mirror, combing her tangled hair. She felt the drops flying from her hair falling onto a sheet of paper on the table in front of the mirror. Thinking it might be some important paper that would get wet, she quickly picked it up.
Page 192
She began to read it as if it were a mantra. Before she could even finish two lines, her emotions changed without her own realization. Prarthana read that letter with her eyes wide open. And without turning the paper over, she quickly scribbled down the address found in the corner onto a small slip of paper. 'Chandranath Bandopadhyay, Number 43, Banjara Hills, Hyderabad' — she could have easily memorized those four lines without writing them down, but she felt she might forget the address after reading the letter, so she hurriedly noted it on a small slip. That night, in her rush, as soon as Urmila fell asleep in the hostel, Prarthana sat up and switched on the table lamp to write two letters that would change the course of her life. The first was written to Chandranath Bandopadhyay; the second—to her father, who had long seemed erased from the canvas of her memory. To Himavant, the boy she loved more than life itself, she never once felt the urge to write such a letter. She stood just a few steps away from the throne of her aspirations.

"Respectful greetings to Father. You may not be aware that I am studying MBBS at Davangere Medical College. Since I left home, the direction of my life has changed. Until today, I had never felt the need to share the details of all that with you. However, I have decided to marry my senior colleague, Debashish Bandopadhyay. His father is coming to speak with me from Hyderabad this Sunday. In this situation, if you were with me, it would be a great help; you, the father who gave me life. I conclude this letter with the belief that you will come. Prarthana."

She finished the second letter, noting the address. Having already sealed the first letter with the hostel's return address after writing the Hyderabad address on the envelope, the lines she had read at Debashish’s house began to echo in her mind. It was a letter written by a father to his only beloved son: "Dear Sunny; you are the center point that gives our life both joy and anxiety. When we think of you, your speech, your appearance, the marks you earn, the speed with which you win—it makes us feel there are no parents as fortunate as we are. However, the rumors and details that reach us about you leave us deeply unsettled."
Page 193
Who is this girl named Prarthana? Where is she from? You probably haven't asked what her caste is, and we do not ask for 'details' about money or wealth. need that, but what kind of a girl is she? Like other friendships that have been drifting along for a long time, this one does not feel temporary. It seems as though Debu is going to marry this girl, as Uncle Shivarajayya has written in his letter. If all of this is true, there is no one happier than us. But Debu, we are worried about what kind of person your 'girl' is. Apart from you, we have absolutely no one else in this world. Because we know your nature, we are asking this: write to us and share your opinion about Prarthana. If there is a shortage of time, or if you have no objection to talking, tell us to speak over the telephone. Or, if you want me to come to Davanagere myself, I will come immediately. Debu, you are not a child. You are a grown man. Grown children become like friends. As a friend, I am asking you…

By the time Debu finished his bath and returned, Prarthana had read the letter from 'Dad' so intensely that every letter was etched in her memory. From the moment she read it, her heart began to overflow with a new enthusiasm, a new hope, and a new joy. For a girl from a poor family in a tiny village called Bilisaale, near Channarayapatna, to become the daughter-in-law of Chandranath Bidish Bandyopadhyay, the ruler of India’s pharmaceutical empire—was that just a small thing?

Prarthana’s respects to the elders. Please permit me to write this letter. I, Prarthana, am poor by caste. I am not as intelligent as Debu. But I am decent enough to be liked by you. I love Debu as much as I trust God. I have respect, regard, and love for elders. I am a girl who has grown up in the lap of struggles. Debu has loved me, and because of that, our hearts are this close even today. Restraint governs us. I do not even know if Debu has the intention to marry me yet, but I have already decided to spend my life with him. You must bless us. Debu is not one for words. He is too lazy to write a letter. I am writing this on Saturday with his permission. Please...
Page 194
Come to Davangere. Decide the direction of our lives together and bring mother along; what else should I call her but 'mother', just like your love? My love waits for you and Debu until the very end. With these prayers, Prarthana thought over the letter a dozen times, placed a photograph of herself inside, and sealed the envelope. Just then, for some reason, she was reminded of Himavant. It felt as though Urmi Didi, sleeping on the cot in front of her, had turned over in impatience. Is this a sin? betrayal of God? Prarthana asked herself; she did not have the patience to seek an answer. She could not fall asleep after tucking the letters into their envelopes. Everything that had happened at Debu's house kept playing over and over in her mind. Truth be told, similar incidents had occurred in that house even before; while they seemed sweet at first, they eventually became tiresome. She often felt that if only she could escape Debu’s embrace, it would be enough. But what happened yesterday was not that. It was the complete opposite! Having just come out of the bathroom wearing only a lungi, Debu was glistening like a statue; his eyes were swollen and turned a fiery red from the night's drinking. He seemed to have lost his usual charm. However, the letter from his father that he had just read, and the mention of her within its lines, had brought such happiness to Prarthana that she had fallen into his arms as he stood there smelling fresh from his bath. If given even the slightest chance, Debu would hunger and yearn; he would fill the car with the resonance of desire in the desolate darkness of the Harihara road. More than love, a sense of urgency roamed within him; he would plead with his eyes alone, his fingers would begin to strum like a Rudraveena. She loved all of that too; such is the sweetness of romance before marriage. But it is beautiful only if it stops there, isn't it? It wouldn't stop. Whenever they were left alone at home, Debu would become like a demon. Many times, arguments would erupt precisely because of this. All this beauty, the warmth of the body, the grace; all its darkness is yours, my lord, but let there be restraint until the hour of our marriage, Prarthana would say. Debu would flare up and become annoyed. Many times, she had left him in that state and walked away. But today, she herself had been prepared. If the scandal involving the Mangalore girl had not occurred the previous day, the bud would have blossomed by the break of dawn.
Page 195
She leaned into his embrace, guiding him gently toward the cot. The warmth of her chest pressing against him was an experience that felt both heavy and exhilarating for Prarthana. Debu felt comforted, and his breathing grew shallow and rapid. The moment of culmination was near. While Prarthana trembled with a surge of excitement, Debu turned rigid. He was staring fixedly at the brown bruise that had suddenly appeared on her full arm. He kept asking himself: "Tell me the truth... what happened there?" Prarthana was in tears.

"Urmi Didi, please look after me with love. Bless me so that everything goes well for me when you send me off. Tomorrow, my life will find its direction. Debu’s parents are coming; they are coming specifically to see me. The day after, my own father is also coming from Channarayapatna. Please don't be angry, Didi. I know you don't approve of this decision of mine, but what can I do? I cannot bear to be separated from Debu. I don't know why, but my inner self keeps telling me that everything will turn out well. Forgive me this one time, Urmila! Who else do I have in this world but you? Himu knows nothing of all this. I am anxious about how he will react once he finds out. He isn't exactly emotional, and that is my fear. Emotional people cry, laugh, curse, get angry, and eventually quiet down. But Urmila Didi, Himu is very introverted. I keep worrying about what he might do to himself. I get scared deep inside, but then I console myself and move on. What if I walk a different path? He will suffer for four days, and then his dreams will sprout elsewhere; he will find someone else. Himu is better than me—he is a man like a god. God will not be sad. angry.

"Is it true, Urmila Didi? You might be thinking, 'Is this the extent of your devotion?' wondering how much you truly love Debu. Before we even got intimate, Debu had become so dear to me that I felt like I would die without him. I deeply attached to him. There was a rare kind of friendship between the two of you; even if you felt angry at his generosity, you always forgave a good friend..."
Page 196
"Urmila akka, before that, you must forgive me. I know I am the biggest sinner of all. But youngest, younger than you, younger than Debu, younger than God himself. Can’t you forgive me? I will be indebted to you for the rest of my life. There is no one else who can drape a saree as neatly on me as you do. Tomorrow, the groom’s family is coming to see me; if you were here, mother wouldn’t have to struggle alone, would she? In the morning, bring me a flower or a kiss, and send me home. Once I return, I will drown you in kisses, Debu."

Prarthana wrote this letter in her lovely handwriting, placed it in an envelope, and without sealing it, left it prominently on Urmila’s bed pillow. Then, as if remembering something, she wrote 'Prarthane' on the envelope. Having just seen Urmila reading very seriously in the library, she concluded that it might be quite late before she returned; so, Prarthana turned off all the lights in the room and lay down.
