Sleep did not come immediately. Why did he ask that question, when he should have been responding to the invitation of pleasure? Why did the question of what Sujay might have done on the Harihar road haunt Debu, especially in such intense, final moments? Did Debu babu suddenly doubt her? Does he really think that something improper happened between her and Sujay? Impossible. Debu is not that kind of man. A man with such an open mind, who would sit for hours and talk about his hundreds of trivial details, could never harbor such petty suspicions. Perhaps he would never ask such a question again. If he were to start asking, couldn't she ask as well? Kaveramma's house; the Muslim girlfriend from his third year; that girl Hemalatha from the senior batch; and finally, Urmila didi! Did he have just one or two? Such things are known to all; in a man's life, there may be hundreds of such instances. A wife must ignore all of them. How you were until yesterday, or why you were that way—I don't need to know. From today, you are mine, only mine. And as for me, you know that I was with Himavant for months; if one starts digging, there is a history to that as well. But what is the use of digging? They are things that died long ago; digging them up only makes the stench unbearable. Forget all that. From tomorrow, a new life must branch out, Mitra. She decided to explain this to him. Her mind turned to Debu's parents.
Page 197
She was reveling in the feeling of being cherished. Would she be liked by both of them? And if, by chance, they did not like her? Would she defy them and still marry Debu? But how could she know until they met? Wasn't father coming from Channarayapatna only after the first round of talks? That man would agree to this marriage; there was no question of him not agreeing. She no longer held any respect for him, yet his presence was necessary for the talks—at least for the sake of formality. If only Himavant were here at such a celebratory moment! Before she could finish the thought, Prarthana fell asleep. However, the one who tossed and turned all night without a wink of sleep was Urmila. Suddenly sitting bolt upright in the middle of the night, she felt like heading straight to Shivamogga. Realizing this was no time to leave, she recited a prayer in her mind and calmed down. Her heart would sometimes soften towards Prarthana. As she herself had said, she was the youngest of them all; let things happen as she wished. Who knew? Perhaps Debu would change. A person who has reached the extreme of decadence can suddenly discard all that indulgence and become a gentleman. Why talk of others? Look at me, for instance. Today, the very thought of a cigarette feels repulsive; the mere mention of whiskey is sickening. Debu, too, might change. Both their lives could become beautiful. But what of Himavant’s fate?

An indistinct image of Himavant began to drift across the canvas of Urmila’s mind—Himavant, sitting cross-legged in the middle of the open fields of Gadikoppa, sketching a lovely blueprint of a hospital. In his imagination, he wondered: at which portico’s shade would Prarthana’s car be parked if she arrived suddenly? And through which door could he enter to reach the pregnant woman crying out in the throes of labor most quickly? At which corner of the hospital terrace could she curl up and sing to her heart’s content when she was overjoyed? Where would the visitors meet and talk to her instead of the patients? So many dreams he built. What if she is young? Can she betray such a Himavant? Even if she does and gets away with it, will she ever be happy? One may not believe in curses, but the sigh of a love betrayed will never let anyone live in peace. Whatever else may be, once a girl comes of age, a shadow or two from an unexpected stranger is bound to drift through her life. Does she need an example better than herself? After returning from Kodagu, she had felt that life without him was no life at all. And then… he had drifted away, and Debu had arrived.
Page 198
It was impossible to tell when he had come or left. A girl's heart can shatter like a fresh slate at any moment. Prarthana too would forget Debu within a couple of days. Is it even possible to live a happy life after marrying such a man? Or will life even be possible at all? But under no circumstances should Himavant be betrayed. "I will make sure no betrayal happens," she said firmly to herself and sat up. Somewhere in the distance, the first bell of the fourth watch of the night was heard. Rising hurriedly, she read Prarthana's letter one more time. She switched on the geyser. Reciting the morning shlokas in a soft voice, she gently shook Prarthana, who was in a deep slumber, and woke her up. Pressing a gentle kiss on her forehead, she whispered "Good morning" in a loving voice. Then, pouring two drops of oil from a small bottle onto her palm, she massaged it into Prarthana’s hair. For the first time in many years, it felt to Prarthana as if she were sitting bowed before her mother, getting her hair oiled and washed in the bathroom. "I will be born as your daughter in my next life, and I will be even naughtier than I am now," she said. As Prarthana stepped out from under the shower after her bath, Urmila noticed something. "What is this, Prarthana? What are these brown bruises? Has it already gone this far? You had it with Debu…?" Though Urmila felt her voice becoming harder than necessary, she couldn't control herself and asked anyway. There was an unexpected intensity in her eyes; the very thought that Prarthana had already lost everything she called her own to Debu had left Urmila shaken. But as she stared at the faded brown bruises on her arm, the tone in which she had asked the question began to feel wrong even to her. Just as she was about to ask again, Prarthana reacted in a strange manner that was far beyond Urmila’s imagination. "Urmila Didi, that is entirely my personal business. Mind you, it is true that I respect you a lot—not just today, but always. I, too, desire that every morning of my life should be graced by your good wishes. Never ask such a question ever again. And yet, since you have asked, and since you asked it so bluntly, I am telling you—even if it is a matter of self-respect, today you have..."
Page 199
Listen, these are not marks made by Debu, it was somebody else. His name might even be Sujay Malhotra. I think this explanation is enough for you. Give me my bag," she said, and without even waiting for Urmila's reaction, Prarthana walked out of the bathroom. Urmila had already decided: she would not let Himavant be betrayed! That morning, Urmila had begun the first and greatest battle of her life.

"What am I supposed to do? You have decided that you won't leave Shivamogga for months; you have made up your mind not to even look towards Davanagere. For how long go without seeing you? I missed you so much in the early hours of the morning that I just got on a bus and came here," Urmila said, standing in front of his stone-walled room in Gaadikoppa, observing the surprise in Himavant's eyes. Even though there was fatigue in the eyes of the girl who had traveled from Davanagere, the joy of seeing Himavant was flowing in them like a flood. As the thought flashed through his mind—*has this girl started to love me?*—Himavant felt shocked for a moment. It should never be like that. Just as it feels unbearable to love someone who doesn't like us, it becomes equally difficult to endure it when someone we don't like loves us. Is it possible to share love with another soul in this life besides Prarthana? In my world, there are only two images: Prarthana and Doctor Prarthana. Even a third shadow is forbidden there. In Urmila's eyes, there might be excitement and joy, or perhaps it is just friendship. Why am I seeing it as something else? Should I misunderstand her innocent excitement? Even if Urmila feels this way, I should tell her the truth this time, he thought. But Urmila was lost in a different kind of excitement. The open field of Gaadikoppa, which she had seen months ago, had changed beyond recognition; foundations had sprouted even in the wild bushes. Everything in the middle of the field had changed. Two tall granite stone pillars had been buried; between them, a small arch had been constructed. A brass bell had been hung at a height that was within reach; mango leaves had been tied to both pillars.
Page 200
"Tell me, what is the reason?" Himavant crossed the pillars. The foundation had been laid. The second floor was just rising. Like a Ganesh idol that had just been worshipped, there were signs of a temple standing on this high mound. Prarthana stood there, stunned. "What is this? It has stopped. Shouldn't there be a hospital here?" she asked, surprised. "According to the plan, a temple should be built at the foundation of every hospital. Whether God exists in such hospitals is something I don't know. But where a human exists, humanity resides. Prarthana Hospital did not rise here, but a sanctum sanctorum for a hospital is what stands on this mound. Someone told me that in the foothills of the Himalayas, they make Ganesh idols like this. I have decided to build a hospital for that Ganesh, to serve humanity within it," Himavant said, stopping the car. "First, let's go to the portico in the middle of the hospital to see the patients, and then we shall offer our salutations to Ganesh."

"Such eccentricities are always there; forget that, Dr. Prarthana. Whatever I do, it with great dedication for the sake of Prarthana. Let all that be; how is Prarthana? Is she studying well? Is she keeping well? Does she still get those stomach aches?" Himavant kept asking one question after another with unmatched affection. Urmila could not continue her speech; she began to sob. "You are very good, Himu... why do you come looking for me like this? If there is anything good, it must be in your mind or your personality. the personality of the girl named Prarthana. If not, why would we talk about her more than ourselves? The number of people in this world who are as pure as nectar is very small, Urmila. Come, shall I give you a glass of milk?" Saying this, Himavant led her towards his stone house.

Inside, more surprises awaited Urmila. From the outside, it looked like it always did, but the stone house was adorned and expanded from within, waiting for his light. Large windows with big wooden frames; deep black doors that looked as if they hadn't been carved for decades; preparations for laying carpets on the floor; and a separate inner room.
Page 201
As soon as Prarthana came home from her hospital, she closed the door behind her. There was a small, neat sink; a glistening soap beside a tap to wash hands; a tiny artificial waterfall in the room; and two musical instruments lying there, as if waiting to be played. Then, another bed nearby. The beds were joined together. "Whose is the third bed? For a baby?" Urmila asked with a laugh. "No, that’s also for Prarthana. Sometimes when she comes back after a night shift, she feels like just collapsing and sleeping, doesn't she? So, I made this arrangement so I wouldn't disturb her when I come in!" Himavant said. For the second time that day, Urmila’s eyes brimmed with tears. Attached to the bedroom was a room specifically designed for Prarthana; shelves were built from the floor all the way to the ceiling just to store her books. The lighting was arranged so that her eyes wouldn't strain no matter where she sat to read. There was an easy chair to lean back on if she grew tired of reading. For now, there was nothing else in the room besides that chair. But, seeing the kitchen, Urmila was truly stunned. What wasn't there? It was as if everything was for Prarthana alone. "Himu, please don't be upset that I'm asking this, but would you have done all this even if Prarthana wasn't in your life?" Urmila finally asked. Himavant, who was heating milk on the stove, turned to look at her for a moment. Seeing the silence in his eyes, Urmila felt that perhaps she shouldn't have asked such a question. But by then, Himavant had already begun to speak. "If I had never met a girl named Prarthana, you would never have seen the Himavant standing before you today, Urmila. Who tells a roaring ocean that today is a full moon? On all other days, the ocean remains within itself, so why does it fall into such a frenzy when the full moon arrives? It’s the same with me. I was just a man wandering the streets of Shivamogga, selling sweets. I never even had the thought of building a home of my own. Look at me today; how many people am I building homes for? If I fell ill, wasn't there a hospital in Shivamogga for me to lie down in? There were plenty. But I set out to build a hospital for those who fall ill—that is the power of love, Urmila; it turns a human into a god. From the moment I wake up in the morning until the very moment I drift into sleep at night, whatever I do, it only for Prarthana. If I plant a sapling, it is because, many years from now..."
Page 202
Prarthana sits under this tree and hums in a soft voice; that is why I water it. A person in love is incredibly selfish. As Urmila's selfishness grows, love only deepens; and that is not just the selfishness of wanting to possess her. It is not sleeping with her, but rather, when eating a morsel of food mixed with a little curd, the thought of her makes it feel so delicious. Many years ago, in Birur, I felt as if Prarthana’s thumb had been split in the same way some girl had tripped, broken her big toe, and cried with a stinging pain in her eyes. And behind that, how much further is Davangere? At most, two and a half years. Prarthana will surely come. To how many thousands of such girls will she stand as a sister? Thinking of that, I swell with pride, feeling like an ocean caught in the pull of a full moon. Urmila, if she were not there, could I have done any of this? If I think of a day without her, then for me, the sun does not rise; dreams do not take birth. You know, a person who cannot conceive a dream cannot achieve anything. In Himavant’s voice, there was an honesty that only gods possess. Although Urmila wanted to ask the question, "If, by any chance, we lose Prarthana... what will you do, Himavant?" she did not have the courage. But, as if to make Urmila shudder, Himavant himself began to speak of that very matter. For some reason, his lips trembled, his eyes turned to stone. A whirlwind seemed to rise from within him, beginning to envelop him. "I love her so much, Urmila. Have I ever thought about what might happen if she becomes unreachable to me, if Prarthana slips out of my hands? For some reason, I am terrified, Urmila. Not about her—it is the fear itself that terrifies me. Because I know very well: the day she leaves my life, the foundations of every house I have built in the fields of Gadikoppa will crumble. The water in the wells I have dug will dry up. Every sapling I have planted will wither from the roots up. The milk of the cow in my backyard will turn to poison. You do not know, Urmila, but total destruction falls like rain pouring from the sky." Saying this, he stopped. For a long time, both of them did not speak. Then, taking Urmila along, he showed her, with great excitement, a small garden he had built right next to the hospital, where none of the saplings were ones he had [planted] before.
Page 203
It seemed like things he had never seen before, gathered from all over his wanderings; he had brought and planted all sorts of medicinal herbs: perhaps they might come in handy for Prarthana someday. In the shed next to the garden stood a gentle cow. It had calved just fifteen days ago. A fluffy white calf with black eyes and soft lips, overflowing with love, began to jump the moment it saw Himavant. Urmila went over quickly and hugged it like a flower. She felt they could give it a beautiful name. "Its name is Prarthana," Himavant, who was standing behind her, spoke just as her thought was blooming. Urmila fell silent. The very thing she had come to Shivamogga to tell Himavant—she could not muster the courage to speak about it. Let Himavant not know about all that; at least, not right now. Why break a dream that is destined to shatter today? Nothing would turn out as terrifyingly as Himavant thought. He would be wounded, true; but just as all wounds in the world heal, the wound in his heart would also gradually heal. He would begin life again from somewhere else. No matter who goes astray, deceives, she would at least remain to fulfill the contract of friendship with a friend like Himavant. Does she have the power to stop the clouds of total destruction from opening their mouths? She did not know; Himavant would grant that too. She had to learn that from him alone. These houses in Gadikoppa, this deity on the mound, the hospital surrounding the deity, the saplings in the garden, the tree in front of the house, the calf jumping in the backyard—everything must be preserved just like this. Even after Prarthana's departure, she must ensure that Himavant’s life does not scatter. Does she have that power? Urmila asked herself again. The answer stood before her: love possesses all power. Then, is it true that she loves Himavant? She asked herself. It felt like the answer could not be found for the time being. Before leaving for Shivamogga, for some reason, Urmila touched her forehead to Himavant’s feet in reverence, but Himavant’s legs had gone numb. He did not say a word. After seeing her off and seating her on the bus to Channarayapatna, he said, "This time you didn't tell me anything about Prarthana. Don't do that next time. Come prepared to talk only about her; wait for a couple of months."
Page 204
"I cannot step out of Gaadikoppa," he had said; "Has Prarthana really left?" Urmila shook her head. The bus departed. Urmila’s mind remained fixed in the innocent, pitch-black eyes of a calf named Prarthana, which was leaping in the backyard of the house. Hadn't those same eyes of Prarthana held that same innocence just two years ago? She wondered. Looking out the window, she saw rows of houses on the outskirts of Shivamogga.

Debu walked up with indifference, inserted the key into the interlock of the front door, and opened the lock with a click. Seeing the scene inside, he was stunned. The house was the exact opposite of his state of mind. Prarthana’s patience, love, devotion, and agility were reflected in every orderly corner of the house. Everything was spotless. It was only that day that he noticed there was a prayer room in the house. In the last two years, all the books he had read and discarded were neatly stacked there. There was no clutter. small photograph of Ishwara. Ishwara with half-closed eyes on the snowy mountain, the Ganga gushing from his matted hair, his skin a shade of blue, and the serpent with its hood raised—the eyes of the serpent looked alert. Come to think of it, shouldn’t Himavant be Ishwara? For just a moment, the question slithered by like a serpent's hood. It must be. Himavant must be Ishwara. That is why she had brought and placed the photo of Ishwara. For her, Himu is God, and he is exactly where he should be. But where is his own place? Was Sujay Malhotra in the place have been that night? God knows. Prarthana is not opening her mouth. Every time he sits or stands, the same confusion lingers. Was he truly there? Enough to bruise her entire body? He had met him at the college in the morning. He spoke as usual. He felt the urge to slap his face, grab his shirt, drag him like a leopard into the corner of the corridor, press him against the stone wall, and ask, "Tell me, what happened that day?" But he could not ask. What if, instead of answering, Sujay Malhotra just laughed, pulled his shirt from his grasp, straightened the wrinkles, wiped the blood from the corner of his lips, and walked away in silence? And if even he chose not to say what happened on the Harihara road, then what would be his own fate? That, above all else.
Page 205
He did not want to say 'hello' to Sujay who was coming from the opposite direction, nor did he want to sit in the cinema hall just to pass the time; he had reached a point where he felt he needed nothing at all. He sat on the stone platform and smoked a cigarette. The thought of being here was becoming tiresome; his mind was unwilling to accept it, as if his very entrails were being wrung out. A junior girl came by on a Kinetic; she stopped the vehicle in the shade of a tree, adjusted the scarf that was wrapped tightly around her neck, and asked mockingly, "Why so looking melancholy? Couldn't find any girlfriends? Feeling lonely? If your Mercedes car is boring you, tell me; I’ll take you around the town on my Kinetic. Your mood will brighten up, what do you say?" Deb knew her; name was Susan Rodrigues, an Anglo-Indian girl with blue, eyes. She had been with him for ten days last year. Throughout those days, he had behaved in a way that gave him a strange sense of satisfaction—that he was deceiving this blue-eyed girl so elegantly and easily, without her ever understanding it. On the eleventh day, he had brought her back to Davanagere and said, "Enough, Susan. Enough is enough. My life is more important to me than all this. There are only two months left for the exams; I need to sit and study. You won't find me from tomorrow. Let's not meet." When he said that, she had wept as if the valves of her heart were breaking. But that was only for that day. The next day, she was with another boy. Deb thought she was just going around with someone else to vent her hatred and frustration towards him. Within two months, the two of them had left their respective hostels and started living in a separate house. "To hell with you," he thought to himself and crushed the cigarette under his boot. You girls are all the same. Today, you weep so loudly that you might as well die, and by evening, you are filled with such cruelty that you kill us with your laughter. Standing next to another man, for the mistake of ten days of love, you demand the tax of an entire lifetime. I should leave everyone and go somewhere. Prarthana, Urmila—they shouldn't be there. Not even Susan—a place where no one is around. Even Kaveri’s house is a closed chapter now; the lust of the lower belly is dead. Enough, of wandering among women, enough of being drowned in the 'stuff'. It should end. I should go somewhere, be all alone, and unburden my heavy heart to someone. Who are these people? Prarthana, Urmila, Susan, the girls from Kaveri’s house.
Page 206
Who were they all to him, anyway? He, his intellect, that richness—they were all a world apart. None of them were his equals. If some Prarthana were to spend half the night on the Harihara road with some Sujay Malhotra, with her body marked and bruised—why should he be so troubled? This was not love, nor friendship, nor loyalty, nor even sorrow; it was merely a knot of suspicion. She had no loyalty whatsoever. The same woman who had bowed her forehead to Himavant’s feet and prayed to him, taught him the lessons of restraint to curb his desires and longings, was the one who had now returned with her body marked on the Harihara road! He felt she wasn't even worth his grief. Yet, the moment he felt like detaching himself from here and leaving for somewhere else, why did the memory of that Himavant from Shivamogga—leaving everyone else behind—suddenly surface in his inner mind? The very man was a comfort. Perhaps, for the first time since his father, he was able to love a man this much. If he left Davangere now, his car would find its way to Shivamogga on its own. When he first saw him, he had seemed like such a dull, insipid man. Seeing Prarthana’s forehead touch his feet, he had marveled at how such men were worthy of such devotional love. Gradually, a jealousy had grown toward Himavant. But that day, when he had chased him down in his car? He had felt as though he had achieved a victory over that man. He had labored under the illusion that he was a god, hadn't he? that he had broken that illusion and reduced him to a mere mortal. He had convinced himself powerful enough to snatch a girl like Prarthana from the embrace of such a god-like man. But today, he did not feel that way. He was right. He was truly a god. Perhaps that is why Himavant remains a devotee to his god, while his Prarthana here is gradually becoming a discordant note. In his embrace, she is melting away like a moth caught in a spider’s web. The moment to end this is not far off. And yet, Himavant lives in peace in Shivamogga; he is ruled only by faith. Loyalty sustains him, and his love elevates him. But what about him? Prarthana, whom he snatched from Himavant, is now in his own arms. She is ready for everything now, but the hearth inside his own breast has gone cold. There is no desire left for the body of the woman who returned from the Harihara road; wherever he touches her, it feels like...
Page 207
It feels as if it has already been touched. Even when I touched the "deplorable" women in Kaveramma’s house from the depths of my navel, I never felt like this. I was not in love with any of them. Then, why am I feeling this way for Prarthana? Am I in love? Am I really her? If so, do I love her as much as I love Himavant? I should just ask him. What is love, Himu? What does it mean to love them? After all the betrayals and the resulting pain, if we still remain, do we become gods like you, Himu? I want to live as a human for them, and yet, become a god like you, who has endured all the deceits just as I have. Show me the way, Mitra. Even if I cannot talk about all this directly, I must go and visit Himavant, I thought. This thought struck me with such intensity that I began packing a few clothes, a towel, and a brush into a small bag. Just as I decided to leave, I came home and parked the Mercedes, only to be left stunned upon seeing the state of the house—Prarthana had cleaned everything, including Ishwara’s photograph and its surroundings, and she looked like a woman who had just finished her bath. As I wondered through what magic she had managed to do all this, I remembered that neither Prarthana nor Urmila had been at college that morning, but Sujay had been there. When nothing seemed to add up, just as I was about to light a fresh cigarette, I heard a voice clearly. I rushed to the front door, and it coincided exactly with my father, mother, and Prarthana stepping over the threshold. "What is it, you lazy sloth? Should the daughter-in-law have come to fetch us from the train? Couldn't you have at least brought the car to the railway station? How are you, wretch?" my father grumbled, placing the briefcase he was carrying onto the cane chair on the veranda and pulling Debu into a tight embrace with both arms. Saying "Amma," I slipped out of my father's embrace, hugged my mother like a bear, and kissed her cheek. The gloom, the unbearable weariness, the disgust, and the uncertainty that had been weighing on me since the morning vanished in an instant, and a sense of affection blossomed instead. Immediately after stepping off the train, Chandrakant Bandopadhyay had keenly observed the young woman in the pale pink saree waiting for them, noticing through his spectacles the innocent poverty that, unknown to her, had fashioned her into a hidden gem of refinement.
Page 208
She had walked up in a manner so innocent that it seemed as if she were unaware of the significance of what she was doing, standing there waiting for them at the station. Chandrakant Babu told himself, "The girl is lovely." Prarthana, however, felt no confusion. The moment she saw the tall, slender figure in white pajama-jubba and the deep-red silk saree, with skin as fair as milk, descending from the first-class compartment, she felt, "This must be why he is so beautiful." She ran up to touch their feet in reverence and took the suitcases from their hands. "You are as beautiful as your name... Prarthana," Debu’s mother had said. It felt as if a Veena from some distant era had taken the form of a woman and was speaking to her as Debu’s mother. She walked toward the station exit, looking at Prarthana’s long braid with deep affection. "If you had looked yourselves, perhaps you would have found a better daughter-in-law than me, but I am the one who found Debu. Meeting such wonderful parents convinced me why Debu is the way he is. Even though he knows you are coming, he doesn’t know for certain that you are arriving today; I didn't tell him because I wanted it to be a surprise. He must have woken up and gone to college by now; I bunked my classes and ran here to receive you. Even if my own parents had come today, I wouldn't have been this happy. I pray to God to keep this joy with me as long as I live. My God will not disappoint me, because my name is Prarthana." As they walked toward the taxi, Prarthana said in a soft voice, "Do you sing, daughter?" Debu’s father had asked. The answer to that came at night. She had prepared the meal with great devotion; there was a cozy moonlight on the terrace of the house. Spreading a mat, the four of them sat down to eat. As Debu’s mother folded a betel leaf and handed it to her husband, Chandrakant Babu lit his cigar and began to exhale small clouds of smoke. Lying down on the mat, with his head on his mother’s lap, Debu was picking out the stalks of the betel leaves that she had spread out, eating them like a little child. Sitting in the lotus position, Prarthana sang in a voice so tender that even the moonlight seemed to grow damp, drenched in joy: "Ghar Aaja Ghir Aaye."
Page 209
The joy on the faces of Debu’s parents was unmistakable. They were immersed in praising her; the taste of the meal Prarthana had prepared was still lingering on their tongues. The cleanliness of the house, her soft-spoken words, the reflection of her upbringing in every gesture—the couple had clearly reached the decision that Debu had not made a mistake in choosing this girl. He was like a child playing in the celestial path. After the meal, Prarthana came down and went into the inner room. After laying out the beds for Debu’s parents in the hall and for Debu outside, she sprinkled a little water to settle the dust, served them water to drink, and said, "I will head to the hostel now." No matter how much they insisted, she refused to stay the night. Even when Debu offered to drop her off at the hostel, she stopped him. "It has been months since the three of you met, sit and talk comfortably. I have some studying to do tonight. Dad is coming in the morning, and I will be here by breakfast time. How far is the hostel from here? There are streetlights all along the way; in front of the iron gate in five minutes. Since I already told them I would be late, the watchman will be waiting at the door." Saying this, she smiled at the elderly couple once more and left.

"How did you manage to cook all that? There wasn't even a proper vessel in the house!" Debu asked in a whisper as he walked her to the house gate.

"Debu Babu, poor girls know the art of creating a meal out of nothing. More than that, love makes everything possible. By the way, I had to tell you one thing: your mom and dad are much nicer than you!" Saying this, Prarthana walked briskly toward the hostel.

By the time Debu recovered from her remark, some time had passed. As Prarthana walked out of Debu’s house, two sharp eyes began to watch her. Like a python that has tasted blood circling a dove's nest again and again, those eyes followed Prarthana’s trail. Prarthana knocked on the latch of the iron gate and stood waiting for the old hostel watchman to come. The sharp eyes moved closer. "Prarthana, I have been waiting for you since evening. Love you."
Page 210
"Prarthana..." He spoke in a voice that sounded coarse, and placed his hand on Prarthana’s back. "Dog!" Prarthana turned around suddenly, startled, her voice tinged with disgust. Just then, the watchman arrived and opened the gate. The sharp-eyed figure quickly moved into the darkness on the side of the road. That figure’s name was Sujay. Urmila, sitting at the room's window, had recognized him from that distance.

She watched Prarthana as she stepped into the room with expressionless eyes. Although Urmila was aware that Sujay had come and tried to stop and talk to her near the compound, and that Prarthana had promptly resisted him, she was surprised to see how Prarthana, who had rushed from the gate to the room in a state of agitation, suddenly relaxed the moment she stepped inside. Urmila had expected Prarthana to tell her something about Sujay saw her face, but that did not happen. "What a great day! Where had you gone since morning, Didi? Debu's parents have come. My father is coming tomorrow. I am excited," she said, leaning her back against Urmila's bed and resting her head on her lap. Prarthana's eyes were shining; there was a flood of happiness. Life was taking a turn faster than she had expected, which is why she was celebrating.

Back there, Himavant was waiting for that much-anticipated moment after five years of waiting, with the patience of a sage deep in meditation. Nothing that is formed in such a hurry and at such speed lasts for a lifetime. Life is not built overnight. It requires years of penance, patience, and faith. It has to be built brick by brick. Himavant knows this. The moment he is waiting for may not arrive even after five years, but the palace of hope he has built will not collapse all at once. Every brick of it stands on solid foundation. Himavant knows this, which is why he is waiting with such endurance. After reading the letter written by Prarthana, let alone doubting her, he had not even visited Davanagere for fear that his presence might disturb her studies.
Page 211
Prarthana does not yet understand the extent of his sacrifice, nor does she realize how his life has blossomed like a flower overnight. She is lost in her own excitement. Thinking this, Urmila caressed Prarthana’s cheek. For some reason, Urmila felt that way; perhaps because of the situation. "There is nothing I can do about this. Everything must be left to the decision of time. How time changes our lives, isn't it, Didi? Previously, when you both were talking in the car, I would sit quietly in the back seat. Now, Debu is sitting in the front, and the steering wheel is in his hands. You are standing behind me, blessing me. Aren't you, Didi? By this time tomorrow, the engagement will be over. If Debu agrees, I will vacate the hostel and move into his house. The date we went to his parents' place is drawing near. If I am with Debu, he will guide me very well. He is a brilliant fellow, of course. After shifting to that house, I will miss you a lot, Didi. I don't know a good person or not, but I am certainly not a bad one. I might have made mistakes without knowing. You will forgive me, won't you, Didi? You must be with me tomorrow. Don't get angry with Debu and push me away." As she spoke in an excited tone while lying on Prarthana's lap, Urmila felt like asking her, "Don't you remember Himavant even once?" But she didn't ask, because she realized she was remembering Himavant more often than Prarthana was. He probably didn't even know about this matter. In the house behind the Gundi Chowltry in Davanagere, with Prarthana, Debu, and both their parents all gathered together, they were writing and signing a tragic fate for Himavant. Unbeknownst to him, she was the only one left here to shed tears on his behalf! Swallowing the exclamation, "So sorry, Himu," Urmila fell silent. Urmila stood in amazement, watching the way Prarthana behaved the next day, the changes in her demeanor, and their engagement, which proceeded as if nothing could stop it. By the time the day dawned, Prarthana was already dressed in a brand-new peacock-colored silk saree, with her hair braided loosely, and on her forehead...
Page 212
The large *kumkum* on her forehead seemed to be competing with her smile. As soon as the hostel warden told her that someone had come to meet her, she said to herself, "It must be Himavant," and rushed out of the room. It was him, indeed. She stood under the neem tree in the premises and spoke briefly with her father. The excitement of seeing her father after such a long time, the restlessness of having taken so many decisions without his knowledge, the eagerness to inquire about her mother, her elder brother, and especially her sister-in-law—none of that surfaced. Remaining completely detached, she spoke only four words and returned to her room. She had given him some money and instructed him to buy a new shirt and come to the Gundi Chatra by ten o'clock. She had already purchased a light yellow shirt and a dark blue pant for Debu from Shivamogga, even though he wasn't there. Was her behavior throughout the day the practicality of a very clever woman, or the last-minute cruelty of a heartless girl? Urmila could not decide.

After the engagement ceremony, as she was returning to the hostel after bidding farewell to everyone, the watchman at the reception handed Prarthana a packet. Inside it was the coconut candy that Prarthana loved the most. Written in bold letters on the packet was: "I had come to Davanagere; I am returning just like that. I will not disturb you. Exams are near; study well. Your Himavant." As soon as she went to her room, Prarthana collapsed onto her bed and began to wail.

*Barkha bahar aayi, ras ki phuhar layi, ankhon mein pyar layi... sajna.* Outside, it was raining heavily without pause. Standing by the window, Lata was humming a soft song by Mangeshkar. Prarthana’s silhouette, dressed in a nightgown, was visible only in patches. Both her eyes seemed dedicated to the rain. A little...
Page 213
Standing there slightly bent, for some reason, she reminded him of a sitar that begged to be played. Debu, who had been lying half-reclined on the bed, his desires stirred from within, began to gaze at Prarthana. Who was this girl? Whose garden did this bloom grow in? How did she effortlessly come from nowhere and establish herself here so surely? What his father said about life was true; she wasn't headstrong, but Prarthana knew very well how to implement her decisions. She didn't put on a great act before her parents, but she was different than ever before. There was a clear humility in her speech. As much as she knew they were observing her every action and work, Prarthana continued to impress them. Only his father had melted away while watching Prarthana. He believed that such a virtuous girl was needed to rein in a son who had fallen into his own whims, without him even realizing it! Apparently, he had even told his mother this before the engagement. Whenever she was around, hadn't he called out, "Prarthana, come here!" every hour, made her sit in front of him, and insisted she sing without pause? "Look, daughter, we had everything in our house, except for music. Debu also sings, just like me, but the two of us can't come out of the bathroom until we finish our song. Now that you have arrived, look! If your husband wants to go to hospitals or nursing homes, let him go, but you just sit in our bungalow and sing your favorite ragas. You haven't seen Hyderabad. From a distance, the city looks dirty, just dirty. But I feel as if the ragas that were sung halfway and left unfinished still wander through its minarets, mosques, palaces, and domes. There are Ustaads who sing ghazals wonderfully. Sit with whoever you like and learn. Dakhni Urdu ghazals were born in Hyderabad. Just sit on the sun-drenched balcony of our Banjara Hills bungalow and sing to yourself. You will forget the world exists. Once the habit of listening sets in, that’s it; your Debu will never leave the bungalow again. It has been many years since Lakshmi graced our home, daughter. Now that Saraswati is coming, what more could I want for my happiness? Our wedding is confirmed," Chandranath Bandopadhyay had said. Prarthana had not shown her happiness immediately. That night, while returning to her hostel, she had written a note that was clearly visible to both Debu and his mother, and handed it to Debu's father before she left.
Page 214
156. Anson was trying to remember something; how many years had it been since that sugar-filled memory? Only Prarthana’s name was written in it. In her handwriting, language, words, songs, silence, laughter, cooking, or behavior—there was no pretense in anything. Just when Mother would feel that perhaps she could have found a prettier daughter-in-law, Prarthana would behave in a way that made one conclude that only a boy as fickle as Debu could be tethered by love alone, and for that task, no one other than Prarthana would be suitable. "You saw her roommate, didn't you? The name Urmila itself sounds good. Intelligence sparkles in her eyes; courage and self-confidence overflow. One look is enough to know she is smarter than Prarthana. Her family background is also quite good," she would say. Apparently, she was the first one to be friends with Debu. But for a boy like Debu, only a girl like Prarthana would do: a girl who would endure everything, no matter how much trouble he caused or how many mistakes he made—that is the only way a family lasts until the end. Someone like Urmila would not be able to live with him. Debu's mother had spoken to her husband on the third night. Both shared the same sentiment regarding one aspect: only Prarthana could endure Debu!

Prarthana was not present when he sat down to talk with her father. It was unbearable to see her father shrinking, sitting with folded hands, and talking pitifully before the wealthy couple. It had been decided beforehand that Father should speak only what was necessary and not discuss any family matters in front of Debu's parents. As for the matter of giving and taking, what did Father have to give? And did he even have the personality required to receive? A father was needed as a formality; he had been called. Let them talk and go. An engagement is a small fence to ensure that Debu, having held hands with her, would never go astray in the future. Along with the three elders, Urmi didi was also enough. When will the marriage actually happen? Debu says to let the course finish. At the very least, if the seal of the engagement is stamped, that is enough. And if it falls in the presence of her father, it is even firmer! Leaving her father to talk with them, Prarthana had found some excuse to take Urmila and step outside. By the time the two returned after about forty-five minutes, Debu’s parents were [handing something] to Father on a silver plate...
Page 215
Wearing a silk dhoti, carrying fruits, and offering milk and honey in a silver cup, Debu was prepared for the ritual of formalizing the word, according to their family tradition. He had brought a colored shirt and clothes for Debu at his own expense, along with a six-hundred-rupee wristwatch and a thin gold ring that looked like foil. Debu’s father had recited the engagement mantras. When asked when they would wear them, he had sat there shyly, fidgeting, as if afraid that the engagement would break if he spoke. "Both are studying; at the very least, I don't see the wedding happening until he finishes his course. If both of you are prepared by then, we will take care of the expenses on both sides and get you married ourselves," Debu’s father had said voluntarily, easing my father's worries. After finishing the engagement and folding his hands to both sets of in-laws, he had just said "I'll be going" to Prarthana and left. Prarthana herself felt that there was nothing more to be done by him staying there longer. Everyone was wearing new clothes; they went to Chetana Hotel, had a meal, and returned. Saying goodbye to them at night, as Prarthana walked towards the hostel with Urmila, the one thing she couldn't understand was his silence. His name was Shivarajayya! Like a wholesale medicine dealer in Davanagere, he was known to Debu’s father in a business capacity; a family friend. When Debu came to Davanagere and joined medical college, he had been his local guardian; an elder who kept a watchful eye on Debu. After many days of observing Debu hanging out with Prarthana, Uncle Shivarajayya had written a letter to his father. It was only when Chandranath Babu wrote a reply to that letter that it fell into Prarthana's hands, and matters had reached the point of marriage. However, during the engagement, Uncle Shivarajayya had sat without uttering a single word; perhaps that was just his nature. Prarthana had thought she would thank him after the engagement ended. The day Prarthana went looking for Uncle Shivarajayya’s wholesale shop herself. Seeing her at the door, he stood up and, as if to say "Come, girl," he led her to his chamber and sat down opposite her. Shivarajayya... "I came to say thank you to you, Uncle, for helping settle the debt."
Page 216
"I am a girl from a poor family; it is impossible for me to ever repay your debt. You stood in my father’s place and performed the *sagai* (engagement). It is only because you wrote that letter that this marriage has become firm," she said, touching Uncle Shivarajaiah’s feet in reverence.

"I wrote that letter, dear, only so that you wouldn’t have to suffer what my daughter went through. Where is the question of a debt in this?" Shivarajaiah said.

"What do you mean, 'what she went through'? What happened to your daughter...?" Prarthana asked immediately.

"The world did not let her live, my daughter," he said, his voice choking with emotion.

It felt as if a thunderbolt had struck the very depths of Prarthana’s soul. She had not even imagined that Shivarajaiah’s silence could have such a profound reason behind it. Not knowing what to say to him or how to console him, she sat in silence for a long time. She hoped Uncle Shivarajaiah might say something, but it felt as though the words within him had died long ago. She touched his feet once more, rose, and left. It began to feel as if behind Debu’s face, there was another, more terrifying face.

However, neither of Debu’s parents was ready to accept this as an official engagement. In a town that wasn't their own, with no relatives around—did they think it was over just because four people sat and talked? It might have been years since they left Kolkata, but in Hyderabad, where they had settled, they had a world of their own. They had relatives, Bengali families, and Muslim friends. If they held the engagement of their only son in secret, would people let them be? They would think Chandranath Babu was just trying to save money.

"There is no other way. Officially, according to tradition, another 'Sagaisang' must take place. Come to Bengaluru in the Mercedes, and from there, fly to Hyderabad. The travel agent will bring you the air tickets tomorrow," Debu’s father had commanded before leaving. He hadn't listened even when told that the exams were very close; he had simply said to manage and come over the weekend. He had offered to arrange air tickets for her friends as well, but when they said they couldn't come with exams approaching, he had remained silent.

On Friday evening, with two suitcases in the Mercedes, Debu drove the car toward Hiriyur, listening to songs in silence, not speaking a word.
Page 217
As the car continued to cross the stretch, Prarthana asked in a voice that sounded quite natural, "Does Shivarajayya uncle have a daughter?" Debu was startled, but he didn't show it. "He had one," he replied briefly. "What do you mean 'had'? Doesn't she exist now?" Prarthana asked. "No; she died. She killed herself." "Committed suicide?" she asked, her voice rising and falling. "Why?" "God knows. Why does everyone commit suicide? They do it when they are bored with life; she must have done the same." Saying this, he stopped. "What was her name?" Prarthana asked persistently. "Some stupid name. Sarvamangala, I think. Let it go. It's a good thing she died. So ugly. Forget it, why bring that up now? Change the cassette, Mukesh is becoming boring. For a journey, only Kishore is right. By the way, had you gone to see Shivarajayya?" he asked abruptly. "Just like that... to thank him," Prarthana said. "Don't repeat this. Don't go anywhere without my knowledge; I don't like it," he said, and after that, he didn't speak a single word until they reached Bengaluru. For the first time, Debu felt that she was beginning her life with a murderer. As she stepped out of the car in front of the Windsor Manor hotel in Bengaluru, she felt like taking a bus back to Davanagere—no, to Shivamogga—and just leaving. She couldn't go. It rained very heavily in Bengaluru that night. Wearing a nightie, Prarthana stood by the wide-open window, watching the rain. That was when Debu felt she was like a sitar, and the question arose whether he should play it. But he felt as though he had become numb, unable to even rise from the bed he was lying on. This was because two names kept haunting him repeatedly: Sarvamangala and Sujay Kulhotra.

By the next morning, Prarthana had woken up with a completely different mindset at Windsor Manor.
Page 218
The pleasant weather of Rona brought a new sense of liveliness. After finishing her bath, she kissed Debu’s forehead with her petals-of-parijata lips. They shared a cup of steaming coffee. For some reason, all the weariness and bitterness of the night had vanished, and a new longing had sprouted in both of them. After many days, Debu was dressed like a pure Bengali in a white jubba and tight pyjamas. Prarthana had emerged immersed in a pure silk saree, smelling of the earth. He kissed her so deeply and for so long that he couldn't pull away. Just as their bodies were about to tune into each other, forgetting all that was old, the first flight to Hyderabad had arrived. Leaving the Mercedes car they had brought from Davanagere at the hotel, they had rushed to the airport in a taxi. There was a sense of wonder in Prarthana’s eyes. "Truly, am I boarding a plane?" The only memory she had was of holding her brother’s hand in the courtyard of their house with the white stone pillars beyond Channarayapatna, looking up at the sky. A small, bright light, like a tiny moving star amidst the clusters of the night, was a plane—had she ever imagined this? She had only felt that perhaps some prince would seat her on a horse, ride off to an unknown direction, reach a palace chamber, and say, "Prarthana, you are mine now." Beyond that, where were the dreams? Back then, when Himavant, who had walked with her all the way from Channarayapatna to Shivamogga, was washing the wound on her broken big toe and bandaging it, she had felt she had found her black-horse prince.

Now, Debu was fastening the airplane seatbelt around her waist. In the eyes of the air hostess, there was a faint flicker—from where to where had Prarthana arrived? As the plane kicked off the ground and stood in the sky, rising higher and higher, a sunbeam piercing through the window began to play golden games on Debu’s cheeks. A man so handsome, so wealthy, so intelligent—could he not be even better? "Debu?" she said. "What?" "We will always hold each other like this, and love each other so intensely that we cannot bear to be apart even for a moment, right?" she asked. "I swear. Above all, you are my wife. love," he exclaimed, leaning toward her. The air hostess, standing at the tip of the curved airplane aisle, turned her face away with a slight impatience. Hyderabad was within sight.
Page 219
"Debu, the two of us have lived for twenty years in different places and with different experiences. Whatever happened in our lives, let us not talk about it anymore. Let us forget it. From today, I am only yours, and you are only mine—in body, speech, and mind," Prarthana said, burying her face in Debu's chest.

But Debu thought, "If that is so, should I just remain silent even when I know the details of how you were that night with Sujay Mallik? Should I not ask you, Prarthana, about with such a man? stay quiet about where you were, how long you were there, and what you were doing?" He wanted to lift his head and ask her sharply, but for some reason, he just stared at the ground.

At the airport, the celebrations and the opulence of the Banjara Hills had gathered in the entire mansion. Seeing the way Debu’s parents and their Bengali relatives welcomed them like children, tears of joy welled up in Prarthana’s eyes. Where had she come from, and reached? Prarthana stood in the marble-smooth courtyard of the Banjara Hills mansion named 'Himanshu' and asked herself. She stood there, looking at the expensive cars parked on the ground, vying for space.

As a girl from Shivamogga, she had once stood her ground firmly before Rasool Jamadar in the town's Shivamurti Circle, with the self-confidence that wealth provides, and declared, "We have decided to walk together until the end." She hadn't even thought of the memory of the candy-selling boy, Himavant, who used to stand at Debu's house. What was his fate?

The huge lamp in the room where she lay was glowing, but there was no celebration here. silence that filled the courtyard of the mansion, and danger of going hungry. But Himavant sat in the middle, wearing a rough lungi and a single worn-out jubba, eating plain rice boiled in an aluminum pot. He had nothing but that one jubba, while she was dressed in luxury.
Page 220
Reason to Tell / 215

But how much of a hunger it was for opulence; Prarthana wondered if perhaps celebration didn't have a hunger of its own. Once the festivities were over, as she took off all the jewelry she had worn and entered the room that had been left for her, she began to extinguish the lamps. Suddenly, for some reason, Himavant started coming to her memory like a stinging wound. Where was I, and where have I ended up? Where are you, Mitra? Did I leave you behind, or did you get lost? I remember you taking me from the courtyard of the white school to your nest-like house in Shivamogga. holding my hand and leading me. kissing the injury on my thumb. Your house was small... but I remember filling it completely. Today, Dev has filled the house, but he himself is not by my side. Back then, you had I didn't stay in that house. Why did this happen, Himu? Where was I, and where have I come to? Where were you left behind?

The mansion was silent. Near the compound gate, under a dim light, a Gorkha sat on a small stool, singing something to himself. Although the song was unclear, Prarthana understood that there was some kind of pain in the melody. Had she walked down the bungalow steps and stood behind the Gorkha without making a sound, she might have known. He was singing a Mukesh song: "Ruk gaye ho toh mujhe yaad bhi aaya na karo... aawaaz na do!"

That very day, Himavant had sold all the sites in the layout and had gone to Shanthappa’s house to hand over the accounts. There was still a lot of work left, but all the money that was supposed to come had been received. "If I settle the accounts up to this stage, it will be better for both of us. Along with these accounts, there are also the details of the loan I took from you for Prarthana's sake. the expenses incurred for food and travel over these twenty days. Every single rupee is accounted for—there is no difference. Please sit down and check the accounts once," he had said. Shanthappa had not spoken. He had called out to his wife inside the house, "Haimanna has come; bring some coffee," and then turned to Himavant, "Look, brother, I..."
Page 221
"Why should I have told you? I never asked for your accounts, did I? Did I ever know who returned the money I had invested? If I had looked at everything, I would have ended up like Nandakka, losing my mind in calculations." Tears welled up in her eyes. Just then, a woman from Nandakka's place came out holding a glass of buttermilk.

'Such foolishness. Nandakka is not a woman of ordinary stature. If she keeps someone, people will gossip. It is said that this relative simply went to Shanthappa’s place and brought his wife back after performing the wedding rituals. It is a common social practice for a man to be distressed after losing his wife. But to forget that grief so soon... however, who am I to decide if this is right or wrong? If it happens suddenly, will I also bring home another woman like Shanthappa did? What if she dies?'

Himavant spoke out loud without realizing it, "Where would I even be alive?" They looked at each other, wondering what that meant. Shanthappa did not understand it. He just smiled and got up to leave.

Coming out, he stood for a moment at the Shivamogga circle. Suddenly, the entire weight seemed to lift, leaving him feeling empty inside and out. If Prarthana were not there, what reason would I have to live from tomorrow? The work at Gadikoppa is more or less finished. Once the accounts are settled, I will get the money. With that money, I will complete the nursing home and a large sum will go to Prarthana. After a few months, it will all be finished. Then what, Himavant?

It is all empty. If there were no dreams, how much more peaceful would I have been? If Prarthana’s nursing home were not there, would it have been possible for me to come to Gadikoppa today? to swallow such immense fatigue and work? If only Prarthana were here with me in this exhaustion. Her cheap nighty; the moonlight in her eyes; the moments when she would plead to be held; and then, in some magical trance, the songs she would remember; the way the bodies would sway together, drawing their own lines of restraint, and finally falling into a cool, deep sleep. Prarthana should have been here today!

Even as he thought this, he pondered on something he had never even considered. Thinking that, he turned in another direction, rushed into the telephone booth located at the circle near Prarthana's hostel.
Page 222
"Tell me the reason."

"I am asking about the girl from Shivamogga, Prarthana. Please call her," Himavant asked in an agitated tone.

"Sir, that is exactly who I was talking about. She has gone to Hyderabad. Her engagement is the day after tomorrow. She will be back," the hostel employee was replying.

Muttering "Nonsense," he was just about to hang up the phone when he suddenly remembered and blurted out, "Look, there is a girl named Urmila who is her roommate, right? Call her."

If he hadn't remembered Urmila by chance, if he had hung up the phone called Kaveramma’s house by chance, and if, by chance, Kaveramma had come looking for Prarthana at the hostel, the lightning would have struck two days early.

"That's a surprise! Is that you calling? Prarthana and I were just talking. She just went to a friend's house to study. The exams are very close, aren't they? She is studying very seriously. Do you have anything to tell her?" Urmila spoke, burying the truth on one side. By mistake, she did not speak about Hyderabad or the engagement. She didn't even let him speak. What if Prarthana changed her mind?

As she placed the receiver back on the cradle, she felt it herself: "How long can I hold back this rising sea of destruction?"

Having paid the man at the telephone booth, Himavant started walking towards Gadekoppa, feeling a sense of turmoil. Although talking to Urmila had given him a little relief, he felt a wave of restlessness rising somewhere in the corner of his mind. Do boys feel such an inexplicable distress the moment girls decide to deceive them? If that is true, it is more terrifying than the agony of betrayal.

Feeling that he couldn't walk all the way to Gadekoppa, Himavant went to Rasool Jamadar’s house that day and ended up sleeping there. No matter how much Rasool insisted, he did not eat. Rasool made him lie down, covered him with a blanket, and sat silently beside him until he fell asleep. When Rasool Jamadar finally got up to head to his own room, the only thing he heard that sounded like a groan from Himavant was, "Prarthana!"
Page 223
Tell Me Before You Leave / 218

"Prarthana, in exchange for the joy you have given me today, I will keep you in the pupils of my eyes and love you. Even my mother hadn't given me such happiness in my entire life." There were tears of joy in Debu's eyes as he spoke, lying in Prarthana’s lap, watching her immense happiness. She leaned forward silently and kissed his eyes with her Parijata-petal-like lips. Having finished the engagement, both of them had returned to Bengaluru. Since it was already evening by the time they arrived, they decided to spend one more night at Windsor Manor and head to Davangere in the morning.

Happiness was flowing in both of them. Let alone the words of Debu's parents, their entire circle of relatives was left stunned by Prarthana's conduct, her beauty, her singing, and her kindness. Even if he had searched with a lamp in hand, Debu wouldn’t have found such a suitable wife. Everyone had said as much to Debu's parents right in front of her. The engagement had taken place grandly, just like in Bengali tradition. When they were about to leave, Debu's mother pulled him aside and said just one thing: "At least make this girl’s life happy!" Debu was tearful. A visible change had come over him; he felt that she had come to him with so much trust, and he had to make her life happy. The past was meaningless; the future had to be golden. He had decided this.

Upon reaching Bengaluru, he did not show any enthusiasm for drinking, nor did he show any urge to embrace her. But he was full of high spirits. leave her side for even a moment and kept talking continuously. Amidst those words, Prarthana asked in a calm voice: "Debu, let me go for just one day tomorrow. I want to go to Shivamogga and come back. You go to Davangere alone. I will then, after that, I will never step out from your shadow. Will that be okay?"

"To Shivamogga? Why now?"

"I have to pay off a debt. I need to be released from a bond. Don't say no this one time, Debu." She pleaded.

"Alright."
Page 224
It was as if someone had called out; Himavant flung open the door of his stone house and stepped out. Tears welled up in his eyes. Prarthana was standing there! She walked slowly towards him and rested her forehead on his feet. For some reason, the gods were absent from the sky that day. The sea had swirled and risen.

Time had come to a standstill in the open field. Himavant stood rooted like a stone statue. All his efforts had failed, and the tears that filled his eyes streamed down his cheeks. When Prarthana rested her forehead on his feet, his entire body vibrated like a veena. "I thought this moment would come only after three more years!" he said to himself. He had planned to go and bring Prarthana back himself after five full years. As they turned into the fields of Gādikoppa, the board painted in her favorite dark blue color, inscribed with 'Prarthana Layout,' should have caught her eye. Her eyes should have been overflowing with surprise, joy, and excitement—seeing the house he had built, every road he had laid, every tree he had planted, the Prarthana Hospital he had erected, the small temple in its bosom, the swing he had installed for her to relax in the medicinal garden behind his house, and her own separate room. He wanted to show her everything, one by one. He had imagined hugging her until she was breathless—she, who stood stunned as if unable to utter a word—and whispering, "Every minute spent waiting for you has not been wasted, Prarthana; every minute has been spent for you." Then, the two of them would sit silently for hours, as if having forgotten how to speak. Even after explaining everything, he imagined Prarthana would continue to murmur, "Did you do all this for me? Did you do it all alone? How did you manage it, Himu? My Himu, aren't you just wonderful, Himu?" Himavant had thought that for an emotional person like Prarthana, it would take a long time to digest the sight of an unexpected dream coming to life before her eyes.
Page 225
Standing in front of the stone house, she began to stare with indifferent eyes. "It feels like he never changes. Has he been like this ever since he was born?" she asked herself, and a smile came to her face. Ever since she had seen him in Channarayapatna, he had been the same. At most, he had tanned a little; his skin beneath his vest remained the same. His cheeks, covered in tight, stubbly facial hair, showed two years of aging. How many years and days had it been since she had last seen Himavant properly? Exposed to the open fields, the scorching sun, the battering winds, and the piercing cold—what else could he be but tanned? She thought she should find a girl who would take good care of him and tell him to get married. "I realized this only when I went to Nanda's house. You are so attached to all of this. Himavant, was it really necessary to leave the town and live all alone in these open fields? Do you think everyone who builds a house builds it in the forest like you? You have gone mad," she said, starting to climb the steps of the stone house.

"Whatever I have built is done with love, for the sake of love—nothing is madness, Prarthana. I have poured love into every brick, sprinkled love, and fed it love to turn this into a bungalow. And the building coming up next to it is the Prarthana Hospital. Do you think it is possible to build that without love?" Himavant spoke without moving from the steps; his voice sounded like the ringing of a temple bell atop a mountain. She did not have the courage to look him in the eye. Shanthappa had said that he had burned all his days and nights over the past two years for the sake of Prarthana Layout. But she had never imagined that an ordinary man like Himavant could conceive something so colossal. She had been stunned when she first saw the brochures Shanthappa showed her regarding the layout. Could Himavant, who lived in a rented room at the corner of Shivamogga Circle so small that one would hesitate to even stretch their legs, be building such a dream empire?

"Look at the coincidence. I had never even dreamt in my wildest imagination that you might suddenly show up, but today, while making the menthya curry, I felt that this was something Prarthana would really like. It feels as if everything I do, it for her. I keep thinking that I shouldn't eat or drink anything you like, and then I laugh at my own madness." Himavant was speaking as if all of this were his own internal monologue.
Page 226
Prarthana felt apprehensive: the entire open field was scorching, with half-built structures, exposed foundations, and solitary stone-laden, brand-new roads. On a large hillock near the house, a bell hung, and the lowing of a calf echoed. How was she to tell Himavant—who was living all alone in the midst of this wilderness with the dream of reuniting with her after three years—her decision in such direct words? Himavant’s heart would break. In this vast wasteland, there would be no one to hear his cry; he would be left speechless, consumed from within. What would become of Himavant?

More importantly, she should not have come alone to this desolate field in Gadi-koppa to deliver such a heavy decision; she could have at least brought Urmi-didi along. Debu was right; people like us do not have the strength to stand before Himavant’s guileless eyes. He had said, "Just write him a letter and explain everything." No, she knew what Himavant’s temper was like. After reading the letter, he would walk all the way to Davanagere if he had to. It would create an unnecessary scene in the hostel in front of her friends. Both of them still had to spend three more years in Davanagere; why invite such a mess? Moreover, Himavant had the right to get worked up and show up at the hostel. He loved her; he was not her father whom she could just tell to leave, and he would depart. a guilty father. She was the one who had made the mistake; to come and ask for forgiveness. Surely, God would not refuse to forgive.

Thinking this, Prarthana had come. But here, in this scorching, lonely wilderness of Gadi-koppa, would she be able to stand her ground and deliver her decision? Could Himavant, who was capable of getting so worked up that he might walk all the way to Davanagere after reading a letter, remain calm in such solitude? How might his wild temper manifest? Would she not be afraid then? She thought she should have considered this first. Now, Himavant, leaving no room for further thought, saw her enter the stone house, and a surge of joy erupted within him—a joy like that which bubbles up in small children. He first pulled out a chair for Prarthana and forcibly made her sit on it by holding her hand. Then, he sat down on the floor before her in a padmasana (lotus position), staring at her unblinkingly, like a madman.
Page 227
Tell Me the Reason / 222

In her unwavering eyes, a radiant light shimmered for a long time, as if she had attained something divine. Himavant kept looking at her, and then, his eyes became completely moist. Suddenly, he called out, "Prarthana!" He didn't even touch her, though he could have; he worshiped her while sitting at a distance. As if he were seeing God manifest before him, he looked at her as if performing penance. Finishing what he had to say, he took her aside, washed her hands himself, and seated her before a banana leaf he had brought. He served her steaming hot "Hitpala huli" (a traditional dish) which she loved. As Prarthana mixed it with thick curds and ate rice bite by bite, it felt as though the expectation of the last two years had reached its fruition. He sat a little distance away, watching her as if she were eating. After she finished eating and washed her hands, he gently wiped her reddish palms with a soft, snowy-white towel. "Did you eat, Himavant?" Prarthana asked for the tenth time. "I could live even without eating for three years," he replied. After she finished her meal, he brought her to the front room and laid out a mat for her to sit on, leaning against the wall. Until then, it hadn't been possible for Prarthana to look straight into Himavant's eyes. But as she looked up at him sitting on the veranda, Himavant had become serious, just as he was when he had left Channarayapatna for Shivamogga that day.

"Dreams, Prarthana! Dreams! How did I spend these two years? Today, it feels just like a dream. How can a man live without dreams? You left the White Salai house empty-handed, and I dreamt of making you a doctor. I shared that dream with you. I left you in Davangere, but I needed a different dream. See? I brought the dream of this 'Prarthana Layout.' If a man doesn't chase his dreams like this, how can life move forward, Prarthana? You must complete your course and come back. From the sanctum of this very hospital, you must erase the pain of thousands of women and children, and bring forth smiles. After that, a child named 'Himanshu' should be born to us. We should break off a piece of our dream and place it in his hands. Then, he will grow while chasing his own dreams. From the midst of his dreams, he will bring a new piece of a our hands. Isn't that what life is? To have a dream that no one else has, and to live happily in order to make it come true. The day the dream ends, no human being..."
Page 228
"Won't survive, will they, Prarthana?" When Himavant finished speaking, there was a strange silence on the veranda. Does a person die the moment a dream ends? "No, Himu; a person dies the moment blood supply to the brain stops. the heart stops beating. I am the one who has been studying exactly that in medical college for two years; I cannot think as emotionally as you," Prarthana said.

"Do you think birth and death are only physical? I was talking about a different kind of living. Not about brain death. Not about the clinical death they teach in our college. Forget all that. Tell me, how are you? Are you studying well? When Urmila called the other day, she said you had gone to some house to study. What did you eat there? Or anything at all? Sometimes I feel like I have burdened you with the weight of a dream unnecessarily because you are younger than me. It feels like I am sitting here cooking tasty food and eating to my heart’s content while I let you struggle and study there. When night falls, and you are there losing sleep to study, I feel restless thinking that I am sleeping soundly here, and I end up waking up at odd hours. You get stomach pain once a month, right? I just suffer here thinking about that. You remember me like this too, don't you, Prarthana?" Himavant asked, looking at her with eyes full of longing.

Prarthana’s lower lip trembled. She thought that if she had at least brought Deebu along, she could have avoided being caught by Himu’s eyes, his words, and his unbearable love. "How could I not remember you? I thought how nice it would have been if you had been there when they all came from Hyderabad the other day," she said. Suddenly changing her tone, she added, "Himu, I’m going to lie down for a while; I’m feeling very sleepy."

As soon as the mention of Hyderabad came up, questions rose in Himavant’s eyes, but wondering how late she might have stayed up studying, and that she had traveled in the morning, he thought she should sleep for a while. He spread a soft bedsheet on the mat and brought her a pillow. Prarthana lay down. A long time later, she opened her eyes simply and looked. He was sitting leaning against the wall, watching her with calm eyes.
Page 229
Prarthana had decided then that for a new dream to take root, the old one must die. Himavant was still clutching onto a dream from two years ago, while she had walked a long distance away during these years. She had to return and tell Himavant; tell him today itself. She would not be able to come back again. Neither the desolation of the fields of Gajanur nor the innocent love flowing in Himu’s eyes should hold her back. By the time the light broke, she would leave this place, having severed all ties. Debu would be waiting there, with the dream they had half-finished! Outside, it had slowly started to get dark. Himavant got up and switched on the courtyard light. In the shed, the calf let out a low moo. As he stood in front of the stone house, Prarthana stood close to him and spoke those words: "Himu, I am getting married elsewhere." He turned toward her slowly. A star fell from the sky.

Prarthana was startled by the cold indifference in the eyes of Himavant, who had turned toward her. There was only emptiness there. It was the strange calmness found a child who had just woken up from sleep—a child who has not yet started looking for its mother. A feeling like that. An inexplicable emptiness. Even though she had spoken the entire sentence, "Himu, I am getting married elsewhere," as clearly as possible for him to hear, it was as if he had not understood it at all. Or perhaps, it had no impact on him whatsoever. In reality, Himavant’s subconscious mind was not even ready to hear or accept such a thing. Prarthana could not decide. He looked at her for a moment with blank eyes. After a long time, as if it were not even a matter of consequence, he said, "Let it be. What is that talk?" With that, the topic was finished. He turned his gaze away from Prarthana and began to stare at the darkness that had spread thickly over the fields of Gajanur with the same indifference. With that single phrase, "Let it be, what is that talk?" Himavant...
Page 230
The reason for her telling him was that he was erasing a major decision as if it had no meaning; he was not even looking at her now. It is very difficult to talk to someone who stands staring into the darkness. But if Himavant were to look at her directly with his calm, unconfused eyes, she might falter and crumble before the stability of his love and the strength of his morality. It is better that Himu remains looking into the darkness; to say everything right at this moment as concisely as possible. There is no need to fumble for sentences. From the moment she left Bengaluru, Prarthana had decided exactly how each sentence, each word, and the inflection of each syllable should be. Having decided that she could no longer remain silent, she said, "Himu."

"I know you are about to lie. You will say you went to Hyderabad, that you got engaged; I know all of that is a lie. You might lie, but Urmi won't. At least, not to me. Not even for a joke, Prarthana. Come here. I will show you how pleasant the darkness is in the fields of Ghadikoppa. Let's just walk that far and come back. For some reason, I am unable to stay in the light, Prarthana. I’m scared."

By the time he finished speaking, Himavant’s voice betrayed signs of defeat. For the very first time, Prarthana had heard the words "I am scared" from his mouth. Is Himavant this weak? Weak enough to be unable to bear the light and be drawn toward the darkness? Or does it happen when one loves too deeply? As Himavant, a man of such a strong physique, stood facing the darkness of Ghadikoppa, it felt as if his entire body was trembling slightly. A profound sense of compassion welled up within her; she walked toward him and took hold of his arm. "Himu," Prarthana said.

"There are very few stars in the sky tonight, Prarthana. The moon won't rise today. I have walked all alone through even thicker darkness than this, but it has never been like this before. Every time I stepped into this field, I would imagine a 'Prarthana Layout' rising up, with hundreds of houses here, and lights glittering all over the field—how beautiful it would be! Walking alone, I used to feel like I should at least have a torch in my hand. But Prarthana, today, for some reason, the very sight of light scares me. I just feel like staying in the darkness." As he spoke...
Page 231
Prarthana was leading Himavant towards the darkness, as if he had no sense of direction, like someone who had lost his way. She took a step, and then he followed. Walking towards Shivamogga, just like the scenes from Channarayapatna, the memory of that day flashed before her. It was pitch black; apart from the occasional headlights of passing buses, there was only darkness. Prarthana did not feel afraid because Himavant was with her. Even now, Himavant was walking beside her. He wasn't saying a word, and he didn't seem scared in the slightest. However, Prarthana couldn't help but feel that the time she had chosen was wrong. Or perhaps the place was wrong? Or was it wrong to tell someone like Himavant about her decision at such an inconvenient time?

Standing in the open field at Gadikoppa, she turned to him, "It's not like that, Himu. The light of a lamp in life is not permanent, and this darkness isn't either. You'll see, everything will be alright. Even at this moment, I respect you just as much as I did back then. It would be enough if you just came closer with love. I have come to you like a child to her mother. You behave this way—you talk, you shout, you get angry. You might even kill me. I am willing to bear your anger, Himu. This is a life you shaped; you finish it. But hear me out, Himu... I have made the decision to marry Debu. Urmi didi lied to you. It is true that I went to Hyderabad; my engagement took place. I wanted to come to you beforehand, tell you everything, and take your blessings. But some events happened beyond my control. In fact, Debu was supposed to come with me today to seek your blessings, but I told him not to."

"Is that so? Why?" Himavant interrupted her suddenly, asking a question that sounded peculiar.

"Because," Prarthana hesitated, "I wanted to stand here alone and talk to you. You and I had a different kind of bond..."

"Because," Prarthana stumbled, "between you and me..."
