"You admit that this is the end, don't you, Prarthana? That is enough. I don't care about which Hyderabad, which Debu, or which Urmi it is. Why do I need all that, Prarthana? For all these days, you were not there, and I was alone. Our relationship existed then, and it exists now. You have come here to speak these words; let the relationship remain as it is. To me, it is of no consequence."
Page 232
"Why should I listen, Prarthana?"

Himavant’s voice sounded like that of a being who had no connection to the real world. Prarthana felt afraid, and that was when it struck Himavant. There is no state of mind more dangerous than that. She had to make Himavant talk, by any means necessary; bring him back to the reality of today and convince him that this relationship was ending right now. If he didn't feel pain, shock, get angry, blame her, curse her—Himavant would never behave like a normal human being again. The injustice done until now was enough; she could not leave Himavant broken mentally, standing in this lightless wasteland. He was God.

Prarthana grabbed the hand of the walking Himavant and stopped him. Not knowing what else to do, she leaned her head on his shoulder and began to sob loudly. Her warm tears began to soak the shoulder of Himavant’s rough jubba. But Himavant had become so stony that he didn't make a single small effort to comfort her.

They both collapsed onto a mound of earth nearby, as if their strength had given out. With her face buried in Himavant’s lap, she cried for a long time. Himavant sat motionless, as if waiting for all her tears to pour out until they were finished. After a long while, Prarthana sat up. Like someone utterly exhausted, Himavant lay flat on his back, leaning against the mound of earth. Both were silent for a long time, as if they had already said all the words that needed to be said.

Finally, Prarthana spoke: "You might think I am deceiving you a lot, Himu. But I am helpless. You never taught me what love is. You only taught me adoration. Even today, the devotion and adoration I have for you have not diminished by even a blade of grass. You will always be God to me! I have not deceived God. I have worshipped God; I have adored God; I still respect you. But I have started to love a human being. You might think this is just infatuation, Himu. I am a human. For those who are human, there is love, attachment, desire, longing, lust, urgency—everything exists. And all of those must be fulfilled by humans. What love is, was shown to me by the one who is God, and I could not push that away, Himu. I have reached this decision thinking like a mere human. I am telling you everything without hiding anything. You are with great hope and great difficulty, got me into college. After finishing the course, becoming a doctor, and coming to touch my forehead to your feet—you will forgive me..."
Page 233
"Tell me the reason," [he said].

"I had thought of standing before you and telling you about Debu only after giving you my word; but events unfolded beyond my expectations. I corrected it from Bilisale, called my father, and we performed the betrothal rituals at Debu's house in Davangere. Yesterday, in Hyderabad, they performed it again according to their customs. Not telling you all these days was my mistake. Today, feeling that I could no longer keep it from you, I came looking for you. Tell me yourself, Himu, what more can I do beyond this?" Prarthana finished her words in a choked voice.

Himu did not move. Lying flat on his back with his back against the mound of earth, there was not even the slightest movement from him. In the darkness, his face was not clearly visible to Prarthana. Having moved slightly away from him while talking, Prarthana moved closer to him after a few moments. She tried to lift his head, which was lying as if thrown upon the mound of earth, with both her hands and place it on her lap; and then she felt it—moisture!

Himavant’s eyes, cheeks, the slopes beside his eyes, his hair, the back of his neck, and one shoulder of his jubba—everything was drenched and had become a small pool of silent sobs. Without a sound, Himu was crying. The man who never wept was starting to cry! Tears welled up in Prarthana’s eyes too. He was no longer reacting to Prarthana’s presence; he lay motionless in her lap like a lifeless body. The words had ended all at once, and the flow of tears was continuous. Neither of them had anything left to say; it felt as though all answers had vanished. needed any questions now.

'I wish this night would end, or even better, if only I could finish off at least one night of my life,' Himavant thought to himself as he lay there. He tried to wipe his eyes while still lying in her lap. Just as he was about to turn to his side, he said, "They have come." Prarthana, who had just managed to catch the hem of her sari, was pushed aside by Himavant as he suddenly stood up, unaware that he had pushed her away from his lap. Two blazing lights arrived into the open field; it was Debu’s car coming from Gadikoppa.

Debu’s car did not speed. But in the light cast by the car's lamps, the killer...
Page 234
Prarthana, who had been pushed by an unconscious urge to stand up beside a mound in the middle of the barren, desolate field, was shivering in the darkness. Debu’s arrival was unexpected. There was no doubt or hesitation in her mind; she had decided that after staying with Himavant for this one night, she would explain her decision to him, seek his blessing, and leave for Davanagere as soon as dawn broke to meet Debu. Was that all? Had she not, even once, longed to experience that indescribable sense of security—lying on the same mat as Himavant, just as they had for so many years, with no line drawn between them, resting her head on his arm—once more? Her inner mind questioned her; had Debu’s car not arrived, scattering the thick darkness with its headlights, perhaps she would have answered 'yes'. From the moment they met until this very minute, she had been receiving everything she needed from Himavant. He had given everything without ever asking for anything in return, like a god. He gave her courage, gave her the opportunity to walk towards her goals, gave her sleep in his eyes even when he lay right beside her, canopy of moonlight, gave morsels to her hungry mind, and gave peace to her heart when it felt like she was being torn apart. In these two and a half years, he had poured everything he had into her, yet Himavant's vessel never ran dry. If she had asked him to hold her close again like he used to, spend one more night in that same silent, loving way... Himavant would not have said no to that either. Because a god’s palms never empty. She was the truly unfortunate one, standing there having taken everything! Was it the moisture in Prarthana’s eyes, a change in her decision, or the pressure of circumstances? Life had brought her and left her standing in this desolate field. Two years ago, didn't she dream of catching a glimpse of this same Himavant? Wasn't she in such a state that she couldn't bear to be away from him for even a second? Hadn't she wept a thousand times, asking if this life was destined for her? When Himavant would gently pat her body, which was like a tuned veena, and lay her down with cool, soothing words, hadn't she burned with desire and impatience? Her body had not known what lust was. The only one who knew was Himavant.
Page 235
Despite all the love, he had remained distant. Places changed, time passed. Debu, with his body like a bamboo grove, had shattered Himavant—who was like an elephant—bit by bit; how he had tormented him! He was the exact opposite of Himavant in nature. He was reckless, always in a hurry; just by coming close, the blood would turn to mercury, unlike Himavant who would lie still as a mace. Perhaps for the past few days, for some reason, he had been dull. Otherwise, Debu was always like a neighing horse, an elegant man who adorned himself with garlands of desires. But she had never experienced the feeling of being "secured" with him as she did in the embrace of Himavant. For that, it had to be Himavant. There is no connection between a girl’s mind and her longings. A girl who approaches a man with unparalleled passion, once she gets very close, desires only for him to lie silently, holding her without speaking a word for hours. Had she come to Himavant wishing for such silent happiness one last time? Was there more to talk about? Was there something left unsaid that she wanted to finish telling him within this night? Did she want to have a small, long-due fight and end it? the farewell, like the meeting, to be an unforgettable event? Asking for forgiveness and him forgiving her—this was not the only purpose of this night. Even if all the threads of the relationship were to be severed, couldn't some invisible bridge have been left behind today? Why did Debu have to come just then? Prarthana thought that he should have left this one night exclusively for the two of them. As Debu’s car headed towards Himavant’s stone house, for the second time, she muttered as if in a trance, "He has come," and casting a vague look at Himavant sitting in the darkness, she began to walk toward the car. Himavant did not have the strength left to raise his voice and ask, "Where?" His wet cheeks were smeared with dust. Like a blade of grass beneath a receding flood, he rose and sat up slowly. There was a roar like the sea echoing in his ears. Prarthana was abandoning him! If someone had said so two years ago, he would have just laughed it off. When he wrote the letter of resignation, when Urmila came and went repeatedly for no reason, and when he strongly felt that Prarthana was lying—every time, only one thing had held him back.
Page 236
"Who can ever deceive God?" When she had newly arrived in Davangere, Prarthana had written that very line across an entire sheet of paper. How much sincerity there was in her handwriting! In her words, in her expectations, in the speed with which she would run to the hostel gate like a whirlwind the moment I arrived, in the warmth of her forehead brushing against my feet, in the fervor stand on the terrace and suddenly become my voice—how much of me was there! How much had I been filled? How much of Prarthana had seeped into me? Is she saying that all of that is enough? Has life ended at this juncture? Is the dream broken? Must I continue to live even after it is broken? Doesn't a person die the moment a dream ends? Was just my delusion? He is stopping the car, and Prarthana is walking towards him in a hurry. In the darkness, she is not easily visible. He steps out of the car, turns towards the house, and calls out, "Prarthana." Should I get up immediately and kill him? Should I kill them both? If it is true that Prarthana loved me two and a half years ago, I must kill her. No; what she did now is not betrayal. She never loved me at all. The real betrayal was what happened Why kill someone who poor thing? If I had known back then, two and a half years ago, that this was a betrayal, would I have killed her then near the Gajanur dam? No. I had called her 'mother.' I hadn't; I had just considered her as such. Would someone like a mother betray? Because she wouldn't betray, I decided to become a god for her; wouldn't it have been enough if been Himu? Leaning against a hillock and talking to himself, Himavant felt as if his own words did not sound convincing to him. Was it the first time that the two of them had come to Shivamogga in a car? Had he already been cheated by then? Or was it that time I chased the car on my bike along the Harihar road? Had the betrayal matured by then? Why were such questions tormenting Himavant? Once it is betrayal, what does it matter when it happened? Does he not have the right to know how many weeks, how many months, how many years—he had been living bearing the burden of such a 'trickery'? Deciding that he must kill Prarthana, he tried to rise, but he swayed. A thousand trains thundered in his head; for many days...
Page 237
"Doesn't it happen when you get a beating?" It felt like such torment was spreading through his head. For some reason, he remembered his grandfather. He remembered how he had once taken him to the fields of Gaadikoppa, where he had walked briskly, holding a rattan cane in his hand, chanting in a strange, resonant voice, despite being told repeatedly not to go. He felt as though he didn't have the strength to stand up. He wondered if he decided at this very moment to hunt down and kill Debu, perhaps all the vitality would return to his body. But what had Debu done that deserved to be killed? If he were to kill anyone, it should be her. But had he not sat with her in the moonlight, mixing curd rice and feeding her with such affection? brought her from miles away and stood with her at the threshold of life? embraced her, put her to sleep like a child, drained away all the friendship of the day to become her mother by night? Had he not remembered her with every morsel of food? To her, he had been food, money, candy, letters, and even God! Could he kill someone like that?

Giving up the attempt to rise from the mound of earth, he simply sat with his hands hanging limp. Suddenly, an uncontrollable sorrow overcame him. "Prarthana—" he screamed, as if his insides were being torn apart, and falling onto began to wail loudly. For the first time in his life, he realized he could do nothing; he was too weak to even shake the dust off and stand up from this mound. Everything ended here. He had lost his very existence. He was now just a creature with long, grotesque limbs; no human could ever love him again. As the thought that he would no longer be understood by Prarthana crossed his mind, a torrent of tears, like a roaring ocean, overwhelmed Himavant.

Trying to catch his breath in between, he screamed once more, "Prarthana... Prarthana..." in a way that would shatter one’s heart. Then, opening his voice, he lay flat on his back, facing the sky, and wept loudly. The fields of Gaadikoppa seemed frozen; a dream had been murdered there. The darkness was dense. There were no stars in the sky to protect Himavant from the sin. After wailing for a long time, Himavant slowly sat up. Unconsciously, his two fists grabbed handfuls of earth from the mound. Suddenly, turning his gaze toward the house, he cursed loudly, "Die, you Prarthana," and with that, he [held] a handful—
Page 238
Somewhere in the distance, thunder rumbled. Then, Himavant let out a long, weary sigh. Filling his legs with all the life left in his body, he stood up straight. In the far distance, two figures were visible standing next to a car. Even from that distance, Prarthana’s silhouette was remarkably distinct; he thought to himself that not even such a dense darkness could conceal the beauty of this assassin. Both shadowy figures began walking toward him. There was no question of Himavant speaking to them both; he had prepared himself to ask only Prarthana, along with Debu, one thing.

"He is terribly upset!"

Rising from the mound of earth, she had walked hurriedly, approached Debu as he stepped out of the car, and said those words in a soft voice.

"Are you also disturbed!?"

Debu’s reaction had sprung out like the tip of an arrow. "I didn't think you would be here, alone with your old lover in such a silent, deserted field at night. I thought in the room behind Shanthappa’s shop near the Shivamogga circle. What is this, Prarthana? What kind of midnight repayment is this? Is this what you meant by breaking off our relationship?"

Although Debu did not ask these things in a staccato manner, his voice clearly carried all that dissatisfaction when he said, "Are you also disturbed?"

*Oh, you man! Is this all your trust amounts to? I yearned for you, endured all your deceits, and was ready to accept you with all your weaknesses. For a wandering flirt like you, I consciously deceived a friend like Himavant for two whole years. Today, when I came to offer a final goodbye to that god and ask for forgiveness for everything, you suspect even that?*

In that moment, a feeling of disgust toward Debu arose in her. She wondered if she could have just sent the car away—the car that had entered the fields of Ghadikoppa—and shunned Debu forever, had she not already told Himavant about her arrival. But she knew that was not possible for her. Over the last two years, she had very deliberately and gradually erased Himavant from her life. Debu might be poor. ugly. useless. Yet, none of that could turn her away from Debu and back to Himavant.
Page 239
This life cannot be endured by carrying it [as a burden] alone. Whether it was Himavant’s, or whether what she was doing was a betrayal, she had admitted to herself deep down. It was not a momentary decision, nor was it a deception sold in haste. It was only after she had systematically killed the innocent Prarthana within herself that she had established Debu in her heart. Let the world say what it wants; it was not a flirtation. Prarthana might be a deceiver, but that was in the past; she could not correct the past. Wasn't that the very reason she had come all the way to get Himavant? Everything had to be searched for and finished by this night.

Although it felt true that the car’s bright lights had suddenly appeared, Debu said, "It is good that you came. Do not hurt Himavant’s mind unnecessarily. When he left for Shivamogga, I did not have the slightest hint that Himavant would do something like this. Just as you went to Shivamurthy’s and from there searched for the fields of Gaadikoppa and arrived here, I too have searched and arrived. I am younger than you. I have been more honest with you than anyone else. Today, I am in a state that feels terrible; I need your support. Himu is very shocked. I am not afraid of his anger; I am afraid of the curse that his god-like heart might cast. Please, be with me. Let us both convince Himu; handling him is not something I can do alone. Stay with me, please Debu..." Saying this, she buried her face in his chest and began to sob.

Debu suddenly softened. He did not see any deceit in any of Prarthana’s words; all her confusion became clear to him. It was he who had broken her away from Himavant, who had loved her so much like a god, and drawn her towards himself. At first, it had all felt like a game. Then, it sprouted into pure desire. Now, it stands at the threshold of marriage. If he had not loved Prarthana so deeply, he would not have come searching all the way to the Gaadikoppa fields at this late hour. "Prarthana, my wife..." he thought to himself.

In the darkness, Himavant sat there hugging her shoulders just like that. It was then that Himavant’s eyes, which had begun to narrate the story of the hill, grew sharp! When was that? Was it when he had brought Prarthana along, leaving Achayya’s monastery, when his finger hit a sharp stone and the big toe...
Page 240
Was it when the bullet fired? she was brought down from the terrace to the room's door? on the Gajanur area, Mother was remembered as if from the depths of her navel? He could not remember exactly. But that day, Prarthana had been walking just like this; embracing herself—with the same affection, the same helplessness, the same slowness! She is the same even today; only the shape of the figure embracing her shoulder has changed. Every human being in the world keeps changing the shapes beside their shoulders like this. When the person who embraces moves away, they stand in the place of observes; that is why it hurts. That is all. That is what love is; what betrayal is; what disappointment is—the sum total of it all is just pain. The pain that Prarthana is moving away from him, she is becoming Debu’s share—what is the difference between the two? Thinking this, Himavant stood firmly, planting his feet. The two of them were close. Loosening the hand that was embracing Prarthana, Debu reached out towards him, saying, "Hello Himavant." It felt like touching the tip of an iceberg. It felt as if the mighty man named Himavant had suddenly died long ago. Debu immediately pulled his hand back; he felt that what Prarthana said was true—Himavant was in deep shock. Not knowing what to talk to him about, he stood there. He began to feel that he, even more than Prarthana, needed someone's help. But Himavant recovered faster than the two of them. "It's getting very dark, let's go towards that side," he said, and slowly began to step toward a small light visible somewhere in the distance. Debu no longer had the courage to walk with his arm around Prarthana’s shoulder. He tried to start some conversation but remained silent after seeing Himavant’s silence. He felt that in front of a strong personality like Himu, anyone would become weak; he wondered what would have happened to Prarthana if she had been alone this night. In fact, Himavant seemed like a man who had finished speaking; like someone walking towards the end of his life; like someone who had decided that his connection with the two of them and with this surrounding world was over. No, even if he had to be stubborn, he should have brought Urmila with him—Debu felt this a thousand times. If there was anyone who could handle Himu, it was her. But hadn't she said she wouldn't come at all? After sending Prarthana to Shivamogga, he had gone straight to Davangere and met Urmila first. She was sitting alone in the canteen drinking tea. She had said "congrats" in a cold tone for the Hyderabad engagement. But now, seeing that Prarthana has gone to Shivamogga to inform Himavant, he felt that he shouldn't have sent her alone.
Page 241
"Are you coming to Shivamogga? Or am I going?" As soon as he said this, Urmila had become a volcano. "Why do you drag me into your hell? Do you want to make me a partner to such a massive deception? Is it difficult for the mind of a Himavant, who has convinced the whole world, to deceive me? Don't ever call me for any of those things for the next two years. If I stay with Debu, Himavant’s dream will die. The desire forest he planted will die." She had said that. And it felt like every tree, every sapling, and every blade of grass he planted there would be destroyed. "After that, Himavant won't live for long. Once he dies, everything in this world will die." Saying this, Urmila had walked out of the canteen, running toward the hostel with a grief that had no remedy. In her tear-filled eyes, every word of hers felt like it was coming true.

But as he walked past the darkness toward the light, Himavant’s steps suddenly began to quicken. Once he was very close to the light, he turned around abruptly, looked at both of them, and smiled slightly. "You both look good together!" There was no distortion in his voice. He placed two chairs on the veranda and had them sit side by side. He looked at them both to his heart's content. "The pair looks good," he told himself, then immediately went inside and brought them some fruits and cow’s milk, placing them in front of them.

He went back inside, laid out a bed for both of them in the room, spread the blankets, lit a lamp with very dim light, and as he came out, said, "It is very late. You should sleep now." Seeing that the fruits and the milk in the glasses remained untouched on the plate, he took them away, placed them in their bedroom, locked the front door of the stone house, and walked alone into the darkness. They had neither the courage to stop him nor to follow him. As soon as they stepped into the bedroom, Prarthana began to sob uncontrollably; Debu had no strength left to console her.

By the time they both woke up the next morning, half of Himavant's day was already over. He had milked the cow, prepared everything needed for breakfast, boiled water, made coffee, and was ready to leave. Waiting for them to wake up, he paced on the veranda for a while, and then, for some reason, came out and stood silently in front of Debu’s Mercedes car.
Page 242
'Tell me the reason,' he stood there like stone. He must have left Davangere that very morning; the dust from the journey seemed to have dulled the color of that expensive car. He suddenly went and brought two large buckets of water, washed the Mercedes until it glistened like a child of the sun, and wiped it clean with his own dhoti. After finishing his bath and eating breakfast, he emerged from the stone house dressed in crisp clothes. Debu was smiling even more than Prarthana. "He is the right one!" Channarayapatna exclaimed to himself. As the two of them walked toward the car, Prarthana exclaimed once more, "You look great!" She bowed and touched his feet in reverence. Himavant stood impassively, like a tree that had endured a lightning strike. When shaking Himavant’s hands, which were as rough as tree bark, Debu’s palms were sweating; they were trembling. Neither of them spoke. Debu started the car, and Prarthana sat in the passenger seat. For some reason, suddenly, the cute white calf named Prarthana in the backyard of the stone house let out a tragic, mournful cry, "Ambaaa!" Prarthana began to cry. As the car pulled out of the open field of Gadikoppa and turned toward Shivamogga, she looked back once. Himavant was still standing there, like a lonely boat. It seemed as if his entire body was trembling as if it were about to shatter. The moment the car was about to disappear from sight, his right hand rose and fluttered feebly in the air. One word, one syllable, one sentence had remained stuck in his throat. It was a question that Himavant, as a man, had chosen to remain silent about, as if he could never ask the girl he loved ever again. "Tell me the reason?" He would never ask. After the car disappeared, for some reason, his body suddenly felt light, blood circulation began again, his fingertips grew warm, and everything seemed to return to a state of normalcy. As if he had decided long ago, Himavant went inside that same stone house. Without succumbing to any emotional outburst, he opened the lock of the iron chest. He didn't have to search for anything before his eyes. For how many years, months, weeks, and days had he been looking at that same iron chest? Every corner of it was familiar; he knew exactly where every object was kept. With both hands, he lifted that small bundle out of the chest. Once he untied it and looked at everything inside to his heart's content, only then did his lips tremble.
Page 243
Tears welled up in his eyes, but unable to control his agitation, Himavant suddenly stepped out of the house. Picking up a shovel that had been left by the door—kept specifically for the construction of 'Prarthana Hospital'—he reached the mound. He began digging briskly. Within fifteen minutes, a pit large enough to bury the bundle was ready. For a moment, he felt tempted to open it and look at the contents one last time, but his mind, bound by turmoil, restrained him. With caution, he lowered the bundle into the pit. He pushed the soil in, first handful by handful, then shoveling it rapidly. Within half a minute, the pit was filled and leveled.

"Mother's silk saree, a small gold mangalsutra, a pair of pearl earrings, and the silver idol of Krishna that she used to worship with such affection—I had thought that was enough for our wedding," a sigh escaped his lips. He descended the mound quickly and drew a bucket of water from the well to bathe. He wondered if he would feel better if he sobbed loudly like he did at night, but the tears would not come. He thought of meeting Urmila, but worried that he might lose his composure. going to Shantappa’s house, thinking she might be studying for her exams, but then remembered that Nandakka was not there. Kaveramma’s, but that reminded him of the Davanagere talk.

He wondered if he should just go to Channarayapatna. Who was there for him? He asked himself, "Is this all there is to my world? No matter where I set out for, doesn't every journey lead me back to Prarthana? Haven't I done absolutely nothing in these two and a half years that wasn't related to her? How small love makes the world!" As he sat asking himself these questions and talking to himself, something shifted within him. Suddenly, Himavant began to crawl from the veranda into the inner room—just dragging himself like a spider, like a multi-legged insect, like a wounded animal, like a crippled child. He seemed to have forgotten that he was even a human being. A strange sound, like a bizarre, wheezing laughter, emanated from his lungs. Crawling like a mentally challenged child, he reached the iron box and pulled its door open with a jarring sound. As he looked at the items inside, a peculiar light sparkled in his eyes.
Page 244
(Page 239)

...hair; undergarment; a half-eaten candy of hers, the spot where Prarthana stood = a piece of her saree pallu and her clear photograph. Smooth, Prarthana was smiling; like moonlight... He placed his palm over it, plunging it into darkness. Then he saw a shadow at the door. When Himavant turned around suddenly, what he saw was Rasool Jamadar!

Only after Rasool Jamadar left did Himavant feel a little relieved. In a single word, he had sent him back just as he stood at the door. "I'll meet you another day!" The voice of the man who said this was distorted. Himavant had gestured for him to leave immediately; in their two-and-a-half years of association, Rasool Jamadar had never seen Himavant in such a state. And why just him? Himavant himself himself condition. Is it possible for a man, once so tall, so healthy, and so robust, to become like this in a single night? Especially a man like Himavant? What love had enabled an orphan boy selling sweets like Himavant to become an adventurer who could build such a wonderful town on the barren plains of the railway station—that same love could also reduce that Himavant in a single night to a worm who couldn't even sell sweets, one who had forgotten how to behave like a human—how could that retired police jamadar know that? The light had faded from Himavant’s eyes. His hair was disheveled, and he felt as exhausted as if someone had sucked out all the strength from his body, leaving him limp. Was this betrayal? Injustice? Loss? Or was this his first death? Unable to reach any conclusion, Himavant felt that if he moved his limbs, it would be like a giant worm writhing; his inner self had become like the body of a child that had remained stunted no matter how many years had passed since birth. Many hours after the sun had grown strong, he remembered that he had not eaten anything, and that he hadn't eaten anything since the moment Prarthana had set foot on these plains. But there was no sign of hunger; if he just sat like this, he could remain in this stone house for days on end without eating anything, without speaking to anyone, and in the end, without even speaking to himself.
Page 245
The blood and marrow in my body might melt away and dissolve while sitting here; even my skin piece by piece and discharge, I wouldn’t know. But life, now, is only a memory; it will not return. The reason is that it has already departed towards Debu. For a while, I sat crouched like a worm in front of the iron box—why, I don’t know. Everything felt repulsive, so I crawled towards the front room. Prarthana had been here. She who was here is no longer here; physically, she is gone. Could it be that she was so terrified that she transformed the very environment she sat in? If Prarthana were still sitting here, wouldn't she have felt the same despair? What was her state of mind? Who knows? If she were right before my eyes, would I have questioned everything? Even Debu—had my love become so weak? Weak enough to endure being in Debu’s embrace? I don’t know. Perhaps it is not weakness. When one loves deeply, one inevitably falls into such compromises. If a husband, having lived for years, suddenly brings home some other woman, doesn't the wife endure it? If she were in front of me, I could have managed somehow. Sooner or later, I would have turned her back towards me. Love does not go empty. Can she, who defied me and left for Debu, not return to me? Prarthana is still young, innocent, and unaware of the world; she has made all these decisions without knowing. She must understand that Himavant’s love is greater than the infatuation with Debu. At the very least, she must realize that Himavant’s world is much smaller than Debu’s, and in it, there is no place for any soul other than Prarthana’s. If she realizes even that much, it is enough; Prarthana will return. When will she come? I do not know. Will she return someday? If she comes, will I accept her? My mind wavered. Didn't I accept Prarthana alone when she came walking from the outskirts of Channarayapatna to this very bus stand? Did only because she was alone and empty-handed? Wasn't there selfishness in that acceptance too? "My Prarthana, my friend, my Prarthana, my girl"—the one who would become my wife in every sense I imagined; my doctor. Doesn't that mean I am very selfish? Don't I love her deeply? Love, after all, is nothing but selfishness...
Page 246
Are they two sides of the same coin? Or a deception? My life for these past two and a half years—for her, solely for her. If so, was all of that selfishness? I had set everything aside—a house for her; a hospital swing in the cow within a shelter calf for her... Was doing all that selfish? No, I did all that for her with the same devotion a human has for their God. God is a hypocrite. God is selfish; he accepts everything, yet bestows nothing. miser. God is merciless. bastard! No, Prarthana is a bitch! From today, from this very moment, I must hate her! As soon as he decided that, Himavant felt disappointed for some reason. It felt as if a small spark of energy surged through his body. The anger that had started small spread throughout his body in no time. He stood up abruptly; there was no trace of the early morning weakness in his legs. It felt like everything was under his control. He walked to the backyard, untied the calf, and milked the cow. For some reason, he made coffee with great care and drank it. The sun seemed to be blazing. Instead of switching on the electric water heater, he felt like lighting a traditional stove and taking an oil bath. As he sat down to light the stove, he suddenly remembered something; he ran quickly, opened a cupboard door, and brought out a bundle of Prarthana’s letters that he had kept with the utmost care for two and a half years. He didn't even feel like reading them one more time. He began tossing them into the stove’s fire with great devotion. Only when he was about to push the very last letter into the fire, his hand trembled. Prarthana had written the single line, "Can anyone ever deceive God?" all across the letter. "I don't know. God becomes a demon, Prarthana!" Himavant said to himself in a loud voice and threw that last letter into the water stove too. By the time it was completely burnt to ash and every letter of Prarthana’s had melted and evaporated, a strange, unprecedented energy surged through Himavant's body. If love, the kind of love that exists, takes an entire night to reduce a grown man to a mere worm, then doesn't it take only a few minutes for the petty feeling of hatred—consisting of only two and a half letters—to fill that same man, who was in a helpless, exhausted state, with titanic strength? Truly, I... so wholeheartedly Prarthana...
Page 247
Do I hate? I asked myself. If it is true that I loved with all my heart, then yes; and that is the right decision, I concluded. By the time the realization reached me from head to toe, it was as if I had taken a purifying bath. Standing naked in the bathroom, Himavant looked at himself for a moment. He had never been intimate with a woman, and he never would be. Until now, apart from Prarthana, he had not loved any other woman, nor would he. There was no room left in his body and mind for love, only for hate. It was as if the word 'woman' had been cancelled out. 'I hate women,' he decided, and taking a clean jubba from the cupboard, he put it on.

He stood in front of the mirror with great devotion. Only a few hours ago, his face had been devoid of any expression, without even a trace of an indelible mark of grief. "You shouldn't be like this, Himavant!" he told himself as he looked at his reflection. He closed the door, locked the main gate, and noticing that work had begun in the open field behind his house in Gadikoppa, he pushed his moped and set off towards Shivamogga. As he rode, he monitored his mind to see if it would wander, but by the time he reached Shivamurthy Circle, he felt lighter and happier than he had for the past two and a half years. For the first time, he realized that hate had this kind of power.

However, in the eyes of Shanthappa, who met Himavant, there were questions for the first time. Just the day before, Prarthana had come looking for Himavant. A few hours after that, Deebu had come. Shanthappa had given them the Gadikoppa address and sent them away. Now Himavant had come. Having arrived, Himavant did not even exchange pleasantries. He had no answer when asked about Prarthana. Standing on the veranda without even entering, he had said, "I need one lakh rupees. Right away."

"Isn't that my own money? I will give it to you. Wait, maybe Prarthana needed it, come inside and sit, brother," Shanthappa’s words did not seem to reach Himavant’s ears. He took the bag of money Shanthappa brought from the inner room and left without even saying a 'thank you'. Himavant's behavior like his usual self. Just two...
Page 248
Within a few hours, Himavant, the boy who used to sell sweets, had purchased an acre-wide field in Gadicappa. On that land, he had signed a deal to build an incredibly rare, massive layout that no rich man in Shivamogga could have even imagined in his wildest dreams. Two and a half years ago, he had pleaded with an architect to prepare the blueprint for Prarthana Layout; now, he went to the same architect, placed a bundle of twenty thousand rupees on his table, and said, "I need the layout design by this evening." He had gone and given a fifty-thousand-rupee advance to the farmer who sold the barren land. To the stone-cutters, the road pavers, those installing electric poles, and those bringing in laborers—he had given small advances to everyone. Knowing Himavant’s name and the reputation of the Prarthana Layout he had raised in two and a half years, no one discussed money with him. No one asked when he would pay the large sums. But the architect preparing the map for the new layout asked without hesitation: "What name will you give Himavant?" "Prarthana!" Himavant finished saying without even a second thought. Later, he wondered to himself if such a name had even crossed his imagination. By afternoon, all those dealings were quickly finished. He got on his moped and started toward Gadicappa, but for some reason, as he was coming down Nehru Road, he changed his mind. He left the moped on the side of the road and walked into a clothing store with a leisurely gait. "New clothes for a new life!" an exclamation escaped his lips. The salesman at the clothing store looked up, as if he did not understand what he had said. Himavant did not reply. When he stepped out of the store, he was carrying ten sets of the most attractive, expensive clothes. Walking a little further down the same road, he bought well-crafted leather sandals and an expensive pair of boots. Do clothes change a man? Do they change his world? He did not know. But the world looks at a man’s clothes, and a man should keep changing his clothes! He felt satisfied when he told himself that. A man who never had anything before, he sat with such satisfaction that day, getting a neat haircut, with a face devoid of worry.
Page 249
He trimmed his overgrown beard. He went to his favorite restaurant and ate to his heart's content, as if he had been starving for days. While eating curd rice, he didn't even remember Prarthana. He walked out after telling himself a small lie. It was already afternoon. He came to the stone house and lay down, but sleep did not come. He could not understand if it was because of sorrow or the excitement of a new life. For the first time in two and a half years, everything he was doing now was for himself. This dream was entirely private. It had no partners. In this dream, there was no Prarthana, no hospital, no deity in the sanctum sanctorum, and no partnership with Shantappa in business. In fact, there wasn't even another person’s name here. Is my mother alive? If she isn't alive, then for whom am I doing all this? As he asked himself this, a drop of water, the size of a rangoli dot, welled up in his eyes. He felt that despite wandering everywhere, returning from everywhere, and conquering everything, the question eventually ends up right back where it started. He suddenly felt that he might even lose the life he had continued solely to hate Prarthana. He thought of opening the iron box once more. His mind did not agree. "God, let me not forget this hatred. I have already lost in love; let me at least win in hatred," his inner self prayed. He woke up in the evening, washed his face, and wore new clothes. Though he had not decided where to go, he thought of stepping out of the house. As he reached the front door, something felt like a stone shifting in Himavant’s heart. He had suddenly found a strong reason to hate Prarthana until the very end: Urmila was standing in the courtyard!

He was dressed in brand-new clothes, which he had worn with great care. He had put on soft socks on both feet and had tied the laces of one shoe. He was about to reach for the other lace when he saw Urmila. Even though he exclaimed, "What a surprise!" in this open space, in his mind, he thought that he should have been left alone today.
Page 250
Urmila stood at the door of the stone house, watching him. She felt that the surprise was not on her face. When Prarthana came to Davangere, she had come with the intention of telling Debu something about Himavant. The moment she got out of the car and ran into the hostel zone, crying out, "Urmila Didi," and sobbing into her arms, Urmila had felt that something terrible had happened. Debu had sent word from the visitors' room; she had no enthusiasm to get up and go see him, yet she had laid the sobbing Prarthana on the bed and gone. Standing at the door of with guilty eyes, Debu Babu did not say much. "Urmila, every time I come to stand before you like this, I am a culprit. This time too, father. Prarthana will give you the details; please console her. She is badly hurt. I knew this would happen. But Prarthana did not listen to me. She went alone. I just followed her."

"Did you go to that Shivamogga that you stumbled upon that day?" Urmila had asked abruptly. He had nodded as if to say yes. Urmila had returned to her room as if she could not stand there for even one more moment. She did not need any explanation from Prarthana. Because, once she knew that the two of them had been to the fields of Gaadikoppa, Urmila could imagine the kind of devastation that might have occurred there. She tried to console Prarthana, who was crying as if her heart would break, but suddenly she fell silent, thinking that one shouldn't console those who have been destroyed.

In fact, it seemed Himavant did not react at all. At first, he acted as if the things Prarthana told him were not even registered in his mind or thoughts. He spoke like someone who had lost all sense of rhythm. Later, he stayed silent as if nothing had happened. He had collapsed on the wounds and mounds of Gaadikoppa, crying, but then recovered immediately and walked away. He laid out the bedding for these two, put away the vessels, and washed Debu’s feet. "It is not all that, Urmila Didi. Why are you doing this? leaving me? marrying him?"—the words that even Prarthana could not ask, Himavant asked as if someone else was speaking through his throat. He did not drag her or stop her; he simply went silent like a helpless child who sits quietly when someone leaves. I...
Page 251
"Tell me, Urmi, isn’t that the very reason I am terrified? Will Himavant do something to himself? If such a thing were to happen, I could never live in peace. Urmi, I am truly a sinner," Prarthana said, beginning to cry again.

"Let Prarthana cry, let Prarthana die. May her life become so unbearable that she can never live happily!" Urmila thought of cursing her, but instead, she just left her alone. Before setting out for Channarayapatna, she packed a bag with a few clothes.

"Look Prarthana; I am telling you today. You shouldn't have taken the decision to marry Debu after leaving Himavant. Of course, that was your personal decision, but you could have let Himavant live, even if it was based on the lies you told him, for a few more days. You were in too much of a hurry. You acted too ruthlessly. Just for your own comfort, to lighten your own guilt, you killed the dreams of a man like Himavant in a single night.

I’m telling you today what I had kept hidden until now—listen. Without you knowing, I have gone to Shivamogga many times. I have seen with my own eyes the many stages of the 'Prarthana Layout' that Himavant was dreaming of. He is a great dreamer. More than that, human being. To decide that in this world, there is only one girl for him, and to build dreams based solely on her—that is only possible for boys like Himavant. It is not something people like me or you can do.

Look, I have noticed how much you have changed from the day you arrived until today. But you haven't meeting Himavant has changed me. It has been months since I had a drink or smoked a single cigarette. How you could leave a man like Himavant and head toward this disease called Debu—I still don't understand. Whether you get love or not is your personal matter, but if I were in your place, I would never have turned to look at a man like Debu, let alone a dog like Rasool Jamadar!

Debu just left after trying to console you. The necessity to be consoled isn't yours; it’s Himu's!"

Urmila walked out without stopping, her voice steady and devoid of fluctuations. Whatever she said, until she reached Shivamogga, there was only one restless thought: Himavant did not have a weak personality. But would he do something to himself? No, he wasn't that...
Page 252
Tell Me Before You Go / 241

It was getting late; still, after getting off at Shivamogga and taking an auto to the plains of Gadikoppa, by the time she reached the courtyard of the stone house, her heart was beating uncontrollably—what state would Himavant be in when he faced her? How could she console him? Standing before the door, she had never imagined that Himavant would confront her with such surprise, in such neat attire and posture. He didn't even ask, "Why are you leaving by deceiving me?" What does it mean to be stoic? The cold detachment in Himavant's eyes felt more frightening than any situation she had imagined.

In the meantime, Himavant stood up, placed a chair in the soft light of the veranda, asked Urmila to sit, and went inside the house. He came out carrying a steel glass filled with sweetened milk. Even though no words were exchanged between them, the instruction, "Drink this milk; let's go out for a while," was understood in his silence.

Urmila got up, went to the washroom, and washed her face. While drying her face in the backyard, she saw the calf standing in the shed staring at her with wide eyes, and remembering that its name was 'Prarthana', she felt as if her heart was being wrung.

"It's quite far, but let's walk. It will be a bit dark by the time we return. It doesn't matter; darkness is better than light. Even if it is a deception, it is not visible to the eye. Let's go!"

Himavant, who had locked the door of the stone house and started walking, had spoken his first words. After exiting the plains of Gadikoppa, he did not turn towards Shivamogga but began leading Urmila in the exact opposite direction. Try as she might, Urmila could not bring herself to ask about the matter. There was no briskness in their walk. After walking for a long time, the forest on both sides of the road began to thicken.

"See, from here begins my new authority and my new ego. In just a few days, I will come here from Gadikoppa. I will have a new layout made. With just bamboos, I will make a small estate for myself right here in the middle of the forest. I will build a small, hut-like house. Within the next three months, I will buy a car. The next time I come to Davanagere, I will come by car. This is the beginning of my second dream, the start ego. Do you see that small board there? It's getting dark already. Go a little closer and take a look."
Page 253
Urmila, who had walked four steps further than the point she had pointed to earlier, saw two swords that looked as if they had been carved with a wooden stylus on a stone wall just a few hours ago. For some reason, for the first time in her life, the darkness felt terrifying to Urmila. If Himavant hadn’t been standing a short distance away, the rustling of the tree branches, the eerie cry of an anonymous bird perched at the tip, and the silence spreading through the night would have killed her, she felt. Urmila walked hurriedly towards Himavant, who was standing by the road, and without a second thought, hugged his shoulder.

Urmila is scared! Himavant said to himself. He held her palm and signaled courage with his fingers. He was once again certain that the touch of a woman could no longer stir any other desire in him. Even as she hugged his shoulder, he led her back along the deserted road. It had become dark like this in the middle of the road before reaching Arsikere. Prarthana used to hug his shoulder like this, hold his fingers tight, and walk with the same sense of insecurity. It had been two and a half years since all that happened. It will be hundreds of years like this; I will keep walking on such paths without lights. Someone or other, scared of the darkness, will keep hugging my shoulder. Whoever hugs me, I remember Prarthana. Only Prarthana.

He thought, "Do you think Prarthana is leaving you for money, for cars and bungalows, Himavant?"

After walking a short distance, she moved away from the shoulder she had been hugging, pulled her fingers away, mustered the courage from somewhere deep inside, and asked, "Urmila, now you and I... I don't think anything. I don't ask anything. Just like that, you suddenly hugged my shoulder. Just like you suddenly left me once before. You are the one who has to give a reason; I don't have the right to imagine or ask, do I, Urmila? As for the matter of Prarthana, I feel ask her for a reason. All I have is the memory. And wait for the day she herself begins to speak. Even if it takes a hundred years, I am just waiting, hoping she will leave with a reason."

As he stopped speaking, the forest by the side of the road rustled, as if trying to listen to his words and the expressions on his face.
Page 254
It was not possible in the darkness. Likewise, the conversation ended there. As Himavant began to walk toward Urmila, Prarthana could not think of any other questions to ask him. She felt it would be impossible to discuss that subject with Himavant again. A proud man who had closed the doors of his heart! "Himavant, if you don't mind, I would like to stay with you for a few days. I have exams; I will go to Davangere tomorrow to bring my clothes and books. on the days of my exams, write them, and come back. I will just study quietly and stay with you, please." Urmila spoke, surprised herself at how those words had come to her so suddenly. Himavant did not answer. She had exams that day as well; she hadn't even been in a state to bring her own books and clothes. He had brought them himself. To ensure she wouldn't stay awake, he would stay awake with her, reading something. She would go and write her exams, and he would drop her off. Sometimes she would study on her own. He had stirred up the dreams himself. Urmila was now saying she would come and stay with him for a few days. He understood her love and her deep concern, but the one thing Urmila herself did not understand was that every attempt she made to help him forget Prarthana only reminded him of Prarthana. Not just her; no matter which girl in the world came and told him she would stay with him, he would only remember Prarthana, and it would only increase his hatred toward her. As far as this life was concerned, that was the ultimate truth. As the darkness deepened, Urmila held Himavant’s hand and walked on. Even after reaching the outskirts of Gadi Koppa, he did not answer the question Urmila had asked. Therefore, Urmila had decided that even if he said no, it was best for her to come and stay with Himavant for a few days—in this open field, in the forest, or wherever he was. However, a few hours after eating and falling asleep, Himavant woke up without waking Urmila. Just like the first day, he crawled toward the iron trunk, using both his hands and legs like a clean-limbed person. If Urmila had seen the expression on his face at that moment, her decisions would have changed that very day!
Page 255
Himavant’s eyes were fearsome. They were like the eyes of a vile sage prepared to pour down the curse of a helpless creature. His posture was strange; it did not look like a posture natural to a human being, but rather like that of a wild beast deciding from which end to start devouring its prey. His hands were shaking slightly. Deep inside, he remained conscious of the fact that Prarthana was sleeping in the room. Why was Himavant trying to open the iron safe? Why had he collected all those lethal materials with such dedication in that short period? Did he have a premonition that one day, Prarthana would betray him, destroy his entire empire, and leave without even offering a reason? He certainly did not have any such premonition. Only Ajjayya—who had sat by the throne in the cave at the side of the hill until he was worn out and weary—had known. It was true that Himavant had been searching for him, but in the end, it was Ajjayya who had come looking for him. The marks of death were clearly written on his face; even Himavant had felt that he would not live for many more days. By then, Ajjayya could not even swallow food. Occasionally, a scream-like belch would escape him. When Ajjayya yawned, it sounded like a beast crying out in a low, guttural tone. On a new moon night, he had woken Himavant up and taken him to the mound. "Why do I need all this, Ajjayya? Who to kill? Is this even a skill worth learning? It’s a vile practice," Himavant had said. "Where was the need for me, son? Didn't your father have this? Maataada Hirannayya? He brought it and left it at my feet. I practiced this for so many years, and what did I get? You know yourself how long I will live now that death has marked me. I am leaving both the good and the bad to you. Just go once a year; on the day of the new moon, light a lamp before the throne, bow your head to the throne, and sit there. I have made separate arrangements for the daily worship there; just provide six hundred rupees for the lamp expenses. When Hirannayya’s son comes to light the lamp before the throne on the day of the new moon, people from the surrounding areas will come and gather. Do that much as a favor; don't let it be said that it stopped."

"You said it is a form of knowledge, right? Surely, some part of it will be useful to you," [he said, as he] drew the lines of a rangoli on the mound of earth with his fingertips.
Page 256
After finishing the ritual, he pierced something like a thorn into the soft cartilage behind Himavant’s ears and made him chant a mantra eight thousand times in three stages. How much time does it take to learn the art of total destruction? "Should I destroy Prarthana?" Himavant asked himself. For some reason, he felt that even his inner voice might be heard clearly and Urmila might wake up. "Am I afraid of Urmila, then?" he asked himself. A crooked smile appeared on his lips. "I don’t even know the meaning of the word 'fear.' Until now, the only thing I have feared is love." But the word 'love' had lost its meaning. All that remained in him was the alertness of a killer; a cruel vigilance that he must not get caught. "Urmila must not know that I am doing all this." What if she does? finds out? She wouldn’t be able to stop it, but the image she has built of him in her mind would be shattered, and that must not happen. "So, should I destroy Prarthana? Today? If not today, then when?" Is this the only way to destroy her? "I could just go straight to Davanagere and finish it in one blow, couldn't I?" He could have done it that night when she was here. Physically, he was much stronger than Debu; it wouldn't have been difficult to kill them both. But he didn't. Police, court, jail—no, none of that scared him. That imprisonment is not imprisonment, and this freedom is not freedom. His mind is burnt to a crisp; love no longer remains there. Only hatred stands as the harvest. Now, his only desire is that Prarthana should not be happy. If he kills her, is it over? With her, joy and her sorrow both disappear, and so does her betrayal! Therefore, Prarthana must not die. She must live, suffering every second, until the very last moment! That was what Himavant was deciding.

That night, in another place, Prarthana was lying alone on her bed in the hostel room, staring up at the ceiling, remembering everything that had happened. What had become of her life? Where had it begun and come to a standstill? I am the one who, disgusted after seeing my father sleeping with my sister-in-law in the courtyard house, left home and came to Shivamogga with a god-like man named Himavant. What happened that day?
Page 257
Just as he had looked at Appa and the sister-in-law, Debu and me today—that was the feeling. The question of whether he was any different from Appa did not even cross his mind. Himavant felt it would have been better if he had tied me down himself. Before he could even think that, Debu came to mind. The love for him was pure. He was mine, only mine. If there were no property, no status, no beauty; would I have loved Debu even without those? Wealth, status—none of these exist in Himavant, and perhaps that is the very reason I turned towards Debu. If a situation were to arise where Debu suddenly had none of these, could I change my mind? Would I still love him then? Yes... I would love him. Now, life, wealth, status, and respect—all of these belong to Himavant and are dedicated to him. If he were to lose them suddenly, would my love turn towards him again? No... he is a god; I worship him even today. With that, Prarthana reached a decision; her love for Debu became even more intense. After visiting Himavant—especially after seeing him in that state—there had been a small anxiety that a fondness for him might arise in her again and that she might change. That fear subsided. He would always be a god, and I will worship the god, she thought and closed her eyes.

Urmila, who had woken up in the morning and sat chanting slokas, came to the backyard and saw Himavant sitting there, hugging a calf, stroking its body, and feeding it tender, light green grass. Thinking, "Isn't this calf's name Prarthana?", she cautioned herself not to accidentally say that name out loud. However, unaware that Urmila was standing behind him, Himavant, in an extremely gentle and affectionate voice, said, "Prarthana..." As he said it, he leaned forward and kissed the calf's cheek. The bell around its neck tinkled softly. The mother cow suddenly turned and looked. "How innocently a man who loves can love, Himavant! At most, one becomes a husband. But your personality is different: you become a friend, a mother, a relative, a god, a guru, a guide... you become everything," Urmila said.

Himavant turned around suddenly and asked, "When are you going to your hometown?" Urmila was startled.
Page 258
Not at all, look at his eyes. They were not just the eyes of someone sleep-deprived; what kind of eyes were those, Himu? But what is visible today is not what I saw then. For some reason, Himavant looks agitated and sorrowful. She had come with the intention of staying with him for a few days, comforting him, and returning to her studies once his mind was under control. But now, she is feeling afraid. He does not look like a sorrowful man; those are not the eyes of a person who has lost someone. He is behaving as if he is someone who cannot stand the presence of human beings, cannot bear the shadow of a woman. Himu was never like this before. There is nothing more terrifying than a person like Himavant becoming a misanthrope. It is as if his words have dried up and his mind has died. Is this happening because of the shock Prarthana left him with? Does Himavant need treatment? This time, when returning from Davangere, she thought she should speak with the psychiatry professor, Rudrappa.

"When are you leaving?" Himu asked again.

"Look, Himavant, my exams start in ten days. I have already finished reading quite a lot, and there is nothing much left to attend to in terms of classes. All that is left is to sit and study. Even if you say no, I will not listen. I will go to Davangere today, get my clothes and books, and come back. and return on the days of the exams. Not for your sake, Himavant; for my own sake, I need to stay here for a month. Don't say that I shouldn't be here since I am the one who has gotten us involved," she said.

Himavant did not speak. Only when she stood ready to leave for town did he come to the edge of the porch, and said, "Don't feel bad. I have no objection if you want to come and stay."

Urmila suddenly turned back, held his hand tightly, squeezed it, leaned forward, and kissed his forearm. In the backyard of the stone house, a calf mooed loudly. *Amba!* Had an unspoken love sprouted?

After that, some events took place in Davangere. By the time Urmila returned from Shivamogga, Prarthana had already moved all of her things from the hostel room to somewhere else, and Urmila was preparing to permanently move to Debu’s house in Channarayapatna. She did not feel like talking to her about anything, even though they had been friends for two years and she was younger than her. Right or wrong?
Page 259
Tell me the reason for leaving / 254

Having reached a decision and stepping toward a new life, she wondered if the life she had rejected could become her own. Who really knows the outcome of life? Why such harsh words? Urmila, who had treated her like a younger sister all this time, melted into tears as she held her when she was leaving. Sobbing, she said, "I'm leaving, Didi. Your love will always..." Urmila became tearful as well. Weeping, she said, "You fool, would my love ever change? May your life turn to gold. Build your life beautifully. Love Debu as much as you want, but don't imitate him. They say the lives of those whose first love has broken experience the most happiness in their second. Be happy. The more you value Debu in your love, the better he will look after you and love you. Life is like that. At times, I have spoken out of turn and said things because I lost control; do not keep those things in your heart. Tell Debu that I said the same to him. Prepare well for your exams. Write them well. Once everything is over, the three of us will go to a restaurant in Debu's car, eat to our hearts' content, and return. No drinking! From now on, you shouldn't drink either!"

Urmila’s eyes were filled with tears as she said this. Prarthana hugged her tightly as if she were hugging her own mother. After she left with her luggage, Urmila felt very empty. The only consolation she had was that she, too, would be vacating the room in a day. Her life thereafter would be in the open fields of Gadekoppa.

While packing her clothes, she wondered—what was she feeling? She went to the city and bought half a dozen cotton sarees. Was it to impress Himavant? She didn't know. He had never objected to her wearing jeans. Still, she felt he might like her in a saree. Why? Urmila didn't know.

After packing her books and clothes, and after the events of the month unfolded, once she went to Shivamogga, two strange things happened in Davangere regarding Prarthana. Those incidents were so trivial that Debu and she did not take them seriously. It was a Saturday morning, around seven o'clock, when someone knocked on the door. Sujay was still sleepy when he got up to unbolt the door. The figure standing outside said, "Debanna, they say you need to give the clothes..."
Page 260
Sujay stared at him intently; he had never seen him before. "Who are you?" he asked indifferently. "The one who is Swami Debanna's younger brother. I am the one who washes his clothes. Dhobi Thimma has settled down here, right? That Debanna left some clothes here, he said, behind the lodge," the stranger said. That is why I came. Debu had left the room a long time ago. If they met, there was nothing more than a 'hi-bye' exchanged, so Sujay thought there might be a couple of his own clothes left in the cupboard. He thought, "If any are left, let him take them," and as he moved his hand toward Debu's cupboard, he stopped and went back to lying down. Sujay realized much later that the dhobi had taken one of his own sets of clothes along with the three sets of Debu's that Thimma had shown him. He thought, "I will ask him whenever we meet," and kept quiet.

In Shivamogga, Debanna was met on the road. When the figure standing at the door asked if he needed to give away dirty clothes, Prarthana was surprised at first. Then, a doubt pricked her like a thorn, but it faded away for some reason. She was surprised because the man standing at the door resembled 'someone' she had seen. Scruffy beard, slightly squinted eyes, and a terrifying limp while walking; he was a man with no similarities to the dhobi Thimma. When he called Debu "Debanna," she couldn't hold back her laughter. After he left taking the clothes, when it occurred to her, "Doesn't the man who just came look so much like Rasool Jamadar from Shivamogga?" a thorn of suspicion rose suddenly in Prarthana’s mind, only to fade away as time rolled by. Although Debu, Sujay, and Prarthana spoke separately about a stranger coming and stealing old clothes, the fact that both Debu and Sujay's inner garments had vanished along with the old clothes did not come to anyone's notice, until a blood-red rangoli was drawn in front of Prarthana’s house, and she stared at Sujay's clothes among the garments brought by Rasool Jamadar.
Page 261
Himavant had said, "Whoever touches Prarthana should not remain alive!" As for how Himavant came to know about Sujay, or any other man for that matter, having been with Prarthana, it was she who had let it slip. Before she left, on a day when their minds were at ease before the exams, Debu, Prarthana, and Urmi had finished their meal at a dhaba. That day, while Prarthana was venting about some issue regarding Sujay, she had let it slip that both girls had vacated their room Prarthana, who had migrated to a small house, had poured out all her confusion to Debu, who sat there ready to study. There was no urgent need to study. He would sit and flip through his books once in the evening. After that, with Prarthana until the early hours of the morning, teaching her subjects. Even by mistake, they did not engage in anything else; because the pressure of the exams was so intense, neither of them had the focus. However, their relationship had grown deep. Once the confusion had cleared and they had grown closer to each other, Debu had said to her directly, "It feels as if a thorn has been removed from my throat, now that you have told Himavant directly! I know, he is a very good man; he loved you honestly. But you were the one who loved me! Whatever it may be, Prarthana, if any man finds out that his girl is acquainted with someone better than him, he is bound to be scared. You women melt easily for kindness."

"Aren't you a good person? There are so many good people in the world, but it is enough if my man is good to me. I have always liked your kindness, Debu Babu." Prarthana had ended the conversation that day, but she knew herself that there was no honesty in her words. Debu was not a better man than Himavant. He was a god; friend; mother; but Debu was the sum total of thousands of desires. He was her man. Still, she felt uneasy that he had called Himavant a "thorn in the throat." She was the one who had been a barbed-wire fence throughout Himavant’s entire life. Forgiving was not that easy. Perhaps due to the pressure of the exams, Prarthana was experiencing intense emotional turbulence.
Page 262
Tell me the reason / 257

The insecurity, fear, and anxieties of the first two years were gone now. She was no longer afraid that he might just use her and throw her onto the streets. The anxiety of "what if Debu finds out about me and Babu" had ended. How much confidence can such a small thing instill in a woman? That is why she thought that peace of mind settles in only after the wedding engagement. In her innocence, she had no hint that in life, things could turn to dust. Debu was much calmer than she was. He did not even look at the house of the embroidery ladies in Channarayapatna anymore. The girl who until recently had seemed like just a girlfriend now felt completely like a wife. He sits with affection and teaches Prarthana lessons. On days when they go to bed very late, he wakes up early himself, makes coffee, and wakes her up. He has even learned to make simple lentil soup. Whether it is because he quit drinking or not, there is no longer that restlessness in his eyes. His eyes are soft; his speech is as sharp as ever. When they feel sleepy at night, they both hold hands and go for a walk on the road in front of the Gundi Chatra. Sometimes, they wake up at dawn, switch on the geyser, and get into the shower together without saying a word to each other. Prarthana undresses there with hesitation. She knows Debu no longer has the wolfish hunger he once had. He is assured that a physical relationship is an event that can happen anytime in the future with mutual consent. Therefore, he is not in a hurry. But in the soft light of the bathroom, he makes Prarthana stand and just stares at her. His eyes wander all over her, searching for something. Prarthana also knows what he is looking for, but the wounds inflicted by Sujay Malhotra had healed and faded long ago. Still, Debu searches for scars, like memories of those wounds. Perhaps he will keep searching for a lifetime. Whose curse is this?

I do not know how Debu received the news of what happened to Sujay Malhotra when the final exams were beginning. Prarthana, however, as soon as she heard about it, said to herself, "A dog's death!" Sujay, who was so arrogant, had suddenly lost his speech and become paralyzed. He was studying for his exams and had come out of his room at midnight to drink tea. While descending the stairs, he reportedly heard a bird cry out strangely from somewhere near the last step. It felt as if someone stood behind him on the very last step...
Page 263
It was as if someone had pushed him, and Dhabala fell forward with a small shriek. He didn't speak. Was it paralysis? Did he have blood pressure? Was he in shock? There was no evidence for it. Sujay's eyes remained open, but he was unable to recognize anyone. His parents had arrived. College students gathered in the special ward of the hospital, but no one could determine exactly what had happened to Sujay. Would he survive? There were no movements of any kind in him. Only once, in the ward, he tried to say something and eventually let out a distorted moan. When his mother, sensing someone had come, stood up and looked out the window, she claimed to see a limping person hurrying out of the hospital. Prarthana, who had been listening to the other details about Sujay with indifference, became alert the moment she heard that the stranger had been limping. For a moment, the image of Rasool Jamadar, whom she had seen once in Shivamogga, flashed before her eyes; but how could that be related to Sujay's illness? She remained silent.

Debu looked somewhat melancholic. Whatever else, they had been friends sharing the same room for years. That someone so healthy was lying in such a bad condition troubled Debu. He would go to the ward to check on him frequently. He would console his parents. He would discuss Sujay's illness with the professors. He wanted to tell Prarthana everything he gathered, but for some reason, he would fall silent.

Finally, by the twenty-first day, it was decided to take Sujay from Davanagere to a hospital in Mumbai, because his right arm had begun to rot from the shoulder base. When seeing Sujay off as he was laid in the car, Debu's heart felt heavy, yet he was cheerful, just as one loves good people. Girls melt quickly whenever boys are in trouble; had Prarthana gone to the hospital to see Sujay? Without my knowledge? Debu, who had come to the doorstep, asked.

That day, for some reason, Prarthana set aside the book she was reading while sitting on the sofa, and without even noticing that the street door was open, she fell into Debu's arms and kissed him for a long time. As Sujay drifted away from their lives, a new joy settled upon them.
Page 264
Tell me the reason / 259

It felt as though they had settled down. Prarthana would blossom with joy every time she passed an exam successfully, and Himavant would be happy for her. Even when they finished one after another, the day the doctor couple would return to India from America by plane kept appearing and disappearing before Prarthana's eyes. In the middle of the night, if she were studying, Himavant would lie in her lap like a young boy, searching for her navel. If they were just lying there, he would snuggle up to her and count the moles on her body. Just and girl who have reached puberty and don’t quite know what to do with their bodies, just looking at each other with longing—they were in that state. For Prarthana, that was the happiest time of her life; she didn’t even remember Himavant.

For Himavant, nothing existed but her. Mercy and cruelty, forgiveness and punishment, love and hatred; his mind oscillated between life and death. Sometimes, he would sit alone and weep. He would hug a pillow named Prarthana. He would bang his forehead against the walls of the stone house and wail. He would climb to the terrace of his old room, lie flat on his back, and shed tears all night long. Suddenly, as if remembering something, he would think, "I haven't sent money to Prarthana this month, have I?" and would rush to the bank and stand there. He would wonder if he could somehow prepare sweets and get them delivered to her. He would ask himself, "Does Prarthana remember me, just as I remember her?" It wasn't possible. "Those who leave kill the memories behind them, but for those who are left behind, only the memories of those who left remain!" he would tell himself. One night, Urmila was sitting in the inner room of the stone house, studying diligently for her exams. Every now and then, she would jot down notes on a small piece of paper. Himavant, who was sitting on the veranda, walked in as if in a trance and said, "Urmila, I need a pen." He leaned forward himself and picked up a sheet of paper. He sat near the leg of her table and wrote: "Prarthana, are you doing well? Is leaving someone this easy? I don't know how you are, as if you have died. If it were the old days, I would have had some peace of mind."
Page 265
Tell me the reason / 260 I used to tell myself, 'In this world filled with so many people every single night, why must I be the only one awake? There are crores of people, isn't there one person who is mine? To her, I am the world; she is everything to me.' I kept telling myself this little consoling thought repeatedly. 'Prarthana is my whole world.' This one feeling was enough to live, to build your life, to weave millions of dreams. In your absence, how many thousands of hours did I spend talking to you? What happened to all those words, Prarthana? Today, in your world, I am nothing. Let it be; you didn't even leave me a world of my own. Does such a big betrayal deserve such a punishment, Prarthana? What sin did I commit, God? Himavant wrote this down hurriedly, and not knowing what to do with it, after a while, he turned to look at Urmila. She, who had been sitting on a chair, got up and came to sit beside Himavant. With an indescribable affection, she pulled him close, took him into her lap, and caressed his forehead. Then, the dam of sorrow broke! For the first time, Himavant acted so helplessly in front of Urmila; he fell and began to sob uncontrollably. Because of the intensity of his weeping, Himavant’s entire body was shaking. Urmila’s lap was soaked with tears. It felt as though his sorrow might not end tonight, this year, or even this lifetime. For hours continuously, until the fourth watch of the night, Himavant kept weeping. His strong body, having lost all its vitality, lay limp in Urmila’s lap. By the time the first ray of morning light hit the ground, Urmila got up, brought a large glass of water mixed with glucose, and fed it to Himavant. When he woke up, it was some time in the afternoon. Urmila was not at home, but there was a paper in front of him. "Dearest Himavant, I am leaving for the exam. You were asleep; I didn’t have the heart to wake you up. after touching your feet to my forehead in reverence. I will be on the bus to Shivamogga the day after the exam finishes. One word;"
Page 266
"Tell me the reason / 20 | As far as the world is concerned, I may not be the first person. But you are the only person who has completely filled my emptiness. Prarthana: You are not God, Himavant. You are a very good human being. I have your letter; it will remain with me. Prarthana does not even have the right to read it, forgive me, I am Urmila!" Reading Himavant's letter repeatedly, by the time she returned that night, her mind had come to terms. After dinner, the two of them sat for a long time on a stone platform in the fields of Shivamogga. That was when she told him about Sujay. Only after that did the talk of Sujay fade away.

"How quickly can a girl change a man's life and turn it into a flower garden, right?" Urmila asked herself, looking at both of them. The exams for all three of them were over. It was not a farewell feast, yet, since a few days of vacation would pass before returning to college, they wouldn't see each other for a while. Thinking that, Prarthana, Debu, and Urmila had gathered at a dhaba near the outskirts that evening. That day, instead of Debu, Prarthana had driven the car. Debu, sitting in the passenger seat, wore a white kurta-pyjama. A dark brown bag hung over his shoulder. Although the fatigue of studying for exams was still in his eyes, since he had quit drinking for many days, the puffiness on his face had gone and he looked handsome. Prarthana had dressed very specially for that evening: a sleeveless blouse, a light blue chiffon saree, loose hair, a snake-shaped bindi on her forehead, a thin line of kohl for her eyes, and a jasmine flower in her braid. Urmila thought that if they were sitting side-by-side, they would have looked wonderful. Debu sat alone in the front seat, humming some unknown tune. She hadn't realized he could be so accustomed to Prarthana's driving. While talking, Prarthana was driving the car effortlessly. There was some joy on her face.
