Was this the same girl who sat in the hostel room on the first day, thinking of Himavant? The question, "How quickly can a girl change a man's life and turn it into a flower garden, right?" came to Urmila once again.
Page 267
Saying "go" is easy, but then it occurred to her—a girl can just as easily turn a boy's garden-like life into a graveyard. Does a single girl have the power to achieve both dreams and destruction in one stroke? Doesn't she have just that? Urmila thought to herself. She remembered the half-built, dark-colored Prarthana Layout. Is that all? Is Himavant's entire life now ruined beyond repair? Can dreams never sprout there again? Urmila talked to herself. For a moment, she felt angry at Prarthana. What right does she have to be this happy? Whose grave did she pluck these flowers from to tuck into her hair? What is her "qualification" to be like this, anyway? Of course, Prarthana is not like me, someone who craved desires and lost all shame, someone who drank. She is not someone who smoked cigarettes incessantly like me. earned notoriety in college like me, nor someone who caught the eyes of thousands of boys. Even if she committed small mistakes, it was due to Debu’s company. Otherwise, where would Prarthana get the courage to drink wine? come out onto the Harihar road at midnight with someone like Sujay? From the society’s perspective, Prarthana is a "good girl"! She is the perfect girl for a rich, handsome man like Debu; someone who is visible to anyone's eyes.

But who has seen Himavant? Wasn't it for this very girl that he soaked his sweat and blood in money, only to send it to Davanagere? her that he would wake up at ungodly hours, pack a handful of sweets, and set off for Davanagere just as he stood? While he worried that drinking half a cup of coffee would mean less money to send to Prarthana, this girl would sit with Debu, drinking expensive liquor and stepping on his feet. While Himavant walked barefoot from the fields of the dark-colored layout to the markets of Shivamogga, she would sit leaning against Debu’s chest, learning to drive a car. While he was lost in dreams and hallucinations, naming an innocent calf 'Prarthana', she was celebrating by inscribing the name 'Prarthana Bandopadhyay' on the edges of her books. While Himavant collapsed on the mounds of mud, his chest bursting as he screamed 'Prarthana', Prarthana was moaning in pleasure within the embrace of Debu. What qualification does she have to enjoy a happy life? That is what Urmila thought.
Page 268
--- ENGLISH TRANSLATION ---

Think about how interesting a subject it is to talk about reasons, Urmi. "For what reason does Prarthana go all the way to the dhaba kitchen to get Palak Paneer made, pestering the cook? She learned to drive the car herself for fear that if I drank, we might meet with a car accident. She might let me drink, but she never lets go of the car’s steering wheel. What a change she has brought into my life. If she hadn't, would I even have had the face to show myself to someone like you?" Debu asked in an honest tone.

Summoning courage, she replied, "It’s nothing like that, Didi. Every woman, no matter how foolish, takes joy in cooking what her husband likes. As for driving, I don't know why, but I’ve become quite obsessed with it, so I keep driving it; it's no big deal. After all, I am just a girl who came from a village called Bilisale near Channarayapatna. Today, they made me the daughter-in-law of such a big house and gave me a life beyond my imagination. From which end of the ladder to which end he brought me, Debu? He is greater than I am. What do you say?"

Prarthana had asked in an equally honest tone. "It is true, my friend; from this end of the ladder to that end. It is true that you have climbed the steps. But there was an innocent step in the middle. It was happy, thinking that your stepping on it was its good fortune. Without even saying a thank you to it, you trampled it and came to stand at the end of the ladder. In a sense, you are great too," Urmila thought to herself.

However, for some reason, even after seeing both of them so intimate and together that evening, Urmila’s attitude changed a lot. What Prarthana did might have been wrong, but what could be done? Does the heart listen to anyone? What was done to Himavant a terrible betrayal. But can it be said that Prarthana is unworthy of enjoying such a peaceful life? Only when one life collapses can another bungalow stand in its place. Himavant is indeed a very good person. But a man as intelligent as Debu needed Prarthana more. If she hadn’t taken his hand, what would have become of Debu by now? Besides, the decisions have already been made, haven't they? What is the use of blaming anyone now? It is unnecessary to analyze what is past. They are both intimate; they are also rare. Both of them know their own histories; they have loved each other knowing that. It’s done, isn't it? Let them be happy now.
Page 269
It occurred to her: once dinner was finished, she would see them off to the bus stand near their house and make sure they boarded the bus to Shivamogga. She thought of taking an auto. She knew Himavant would not wait for her. He was not a person of this world; he would not remain as Himavant. She had thought of leaving early to spend more days with Himavant and try to set him right. She felt a responsibility towards him; but Prarthana would not let her go. As soon as dinner was over, Prarthana insisted that instead of leaving for Shivamogga at this odd hour and reaching at an inconvenient time, they should stay the night and leave in the morning. That seemed right to Urmila too. Besides, how long had it been since the three friends had come together like this? It felt as if no amount of talking would ever be enough.

After returning home at night, they sat down to talk again. They spent some time on the terrace. Under the dark sky, Prarthana, wearing a stark white nightie and leaning on Debu’s shoulder, sang two old Hindi songs. A flash of admiration flickered in Debu’s eyes; Prarthana had even learned some ghazals for his sake. She said, "Aren't we going to Hyderabad for the holidays tomorrow? There is a guru there who teaches ghazals. By the time college starts, I will come back having learned a lot of them."

Even though they came downstairs when it got too cold to sit on the terrace, it seemed the two friends’ conversation would not end that night. Debu was already sleepy. He went into the bedroom, switched off the lights, and went to bed. The two girls spread a mat in the hall, lay down; a quiet conversation continued. Neither of them showed any signs of sleep.

Then Prarthana sat up. "Urmi Didi, I have a question to ask you. May I?" she asked.

"Go ahead."

"Didi, you are going to Shivamogga tomorrow. You will stay there. You and Himavant will be together for so many days. You both will be in Gadikoppa. Just the two of you. Didi, don't feel bad that I asked you this—do you love Himavant?"

Urmila sat up abruptly. She had certainly not expected such a question from Prarthana. She knew her nature. She knew that Himavant had changed the very direction of her life. He was in unbearable pain; he was lonely. It was for that reason alone...
Page 270
As a small remedy for her boredom and loneliness, Prarthana had gone to Shivamogga. Knowing everything, she was questioning Urmila about matters that were already clear. "Do you think I love Himavant, Didi?" Prarthana asked. Urmila countered with another question, "I don't know, Didi. But I do know that you want Himavant to be loved. It certainly doesn't feel like things will be alright anytime soon. I get anxious thinking about what he might do if he is left alone. He is very emotional. His only sister, Nandakka, has also left him. Apart from Shantappa, he has no one else, no friends. Rasool Jamadar doesn't understand everything. Even Ajjayya is no more. Apart from that, Himavant has no other world. He is not a man who yearns for material happiness; even clear water won't taste good in a place without love. You know how he behaves when he goes to Kaveramma’s house. I get scared thinking about it, Didi. If I leave him alone in such a vast field, I feel like I won't be able to go anywhere. Is he truly in love with me? I am the same; I cannot think of anything else. But Didi, who will take care of Himavant? Who understands him better than you? Who can console you?"

Urmila said, "I don't know what you think as a doctor. As a woman, I am confident that whoever stays with Himavant will be at peace until the end. It’s not just about you... I just hope Himavant forgets me and marries another girl. That way, this guilt that haunts me until I die... I couldn't bear it, that's why I asked. I had no other intention, Didi," said Prarthana.

Now Urmila understood why all the girls in the world wish for the boy they left behind to marry someone else. It wasn't just because they wanted him to be happy; it was to finish their own guilt by letting him go! What else? 'You got married too, didn't you? So, let's call it quits with an agreement that you stay happy there and I stay happy here.'

"Oh, how simple," Urmila thought. But if she had wished for him to remember her until the end, to die while thinking of her, maybe this girl Prarthana could have been considered honest. But she is not like that; she is telling him to get married only so that she doesn't feel guilty. Is this what goodness is? Are these the ones who are called 'good girls'? it means to offer a confession for breaking someone's heart?
Page 271
Wishing for her boy to get married again, was it? For Urmila to ask such a question with such deliberation in the middle of the night did not seem appropriate to Prarthana. Urmila asked again, "If I love Himavant and marry him, will you be happy, Prarthana?" The answer came immediately, "Certainly, I will be content." "I am asking if you will be happy, answer directly and firmly," Urmila pressed. Prarthana did not speak for a long time. Being happy is one thing, and accepting something as a compromise is another. Urmila was waiting for a direct answer to her question—a question as sharp as a betel nut cutter. She knew very well that Himavant would never be able to love another girl in this lifetime. She also knew that if she changed her decision and went back at this moment, he would accept her with the same love as before; but her returning was an impossible affair. On the surface, she was telling herself that she wanted Himavant to be happy, but was she truly happy? What if, years later, Himavant met her and said, "What Urmila? I gave in to pressure and married her out of necessity. Even today, my love is only for you..." If he said that, would she accept it? That is a different matter, but she would certainly feel happy. Even so, she did not wish for Himavant to remain unmarried. If he got married, her guilt would be relieved. But what if that same Himavant decided to love Urmila? Uh-uh, she would not be able to bear it. Yet, she did not have the courage to tell Urmila directly. Because she had not left Himavant because he was a god, nor because she wanted a husband who was a god-like man. She was a complete egoist. What she did was merely a betrayal; she could give a hundred reasons for it, but her heart demanded an honest answer. Answering that was difficult. Now, Urmila was asking exactly that question. If she loved Himavant, would Prarthana be happy? Prarthana did not answer. That night, neither question received an answer. If Urmila loved Himavant...
Page 272
The first question was whether Prarthana would be happy. The second question, which she asked herself, was whether she had started loving Himavant. Urmila had bypassed these questions. For the thousandth time, light had spread outside.

When Urmila got ready to leave for Shivamogga in the morning, Debu hadn’t even woken up yet. If she left on the first bus of the day, the heat wouldn't be unbearable. "I am leaving. I will meet both of you after I return from Channarayapatna. Tell Debu as well," Urmila said. Prarthana walked her up to the Gundi Chatra circle, put her in an auto, and returned home. Prarthana, who was just about to leave from Davangere, boarded the bus and took a window seat. The moment she rested her head against the glass, Urmila fell asleep and only woke up in Shivamogga. The exhaustion from studying for exams, and sitting up talking to Prarthana for a long time at night—all of it combined to cause that deep sleep. As she got off the bus thinking this, a sudden shock awaited her. Himavant was standing there!

"I just had a feeling you would come on this bus; what they call a 'gut feeling.' So, I came to receive you and take you along. You are the one who surprises me every time regarding your comings and goings. I took a chance this time," he said.

Honestly, Urmila could not believe any of it. The shoes on Himavant’s feet were Reebok; they were not shoes worth less than five thousand. His jeans were not from India; the light blue shade felt like it resembled the sea. Over that, he wore a dark black shirt with a large collar. Himavant stood there wearing thin-framed glasses. If this hadn't been a bus stand, and if she hadn't already confessed that she loved him, she thought she would have jumped from the bus footboard and fallen straight into his neck and embrace. Compared to his smart stance, she wondered what Debu's charm was. Without giving her a chance to think about it further, Himavant snatched the bag from Urmila’s hand and started walking out of the bus stand. She started taking quick steps behind him as they reached the courtyard
Page 273
At 2:00 PM, another turn of events awaited. Himavant glanced at her and said, "I haven't yet gained the confidence to drive on a busy road; but on empty stretches, I think I can drive confidently. For now, it would be better if you drove." He stood waiting for Urmila’s response, his eyes sparkling with surprise. A dark red Esteem car was shining brilliantly in the soft sunlight. Had Himavant really bought it? Where did this new boldness come from? Was this the same Himu who, before leaving for his exams, used to lie on the floor sobbing uncontrollably? She couldn't believe it. He hadn't given a single hint about buying a car, nor did she know he was learning to drive. Perhaps a man who sets his mind to something can achieve anything, right? She thought Prarthana hadn't understood this at all. The very next moment, a sense of belonging enveloped her. This feeling that it was mine! If it wasn't mine, if Himavant hadn't felt even the slightest affection for me, would he have come here to wait for the bus? He is such a detached person; is he finally becoming mine?

She turned the ignition key, and as the car's wheels began to roll with a soft, gentle sound, a sweet melody began to play from the cassette in the car's deck: *"Ninna kangala koladi beladingalilidante, nannedeya kadaleke beegutihudo..."* (Like moonlight descending into the pond of your eyes, why is the ocean of my heart rejoicing?).

Although she felt shy, wondering what Himu would think if she let her joy show, she smiled slightly at the corner of her lips and looked at Himu. He was now looking out the window, with the same detachment in his eyes. Regardless, the question arose: where would Himu keep such an expensive car in the open fields of Gadekoppa? Had he built a shed? Just as she was thinking that expensive cars shouldn't be left out like that, he gestured to Urmila to drive in a direction unrelated to the Gadekoppa fields. "This is called Ravindranagara. It's a neat locality where everyone lives, from those who have recently become rich like me, to those who were born rich. Take a left here. Slow down a bit, honk. Stop here, honk once. There, see? Zabeen has come. She is opening the door. Put the car in the portico."
Page 274
The answer to the question of why he would have brought Jabeen if he had desired her, had been found after some time. Jabeen was his younger sister's last legacy. She was disabled from birth. Rasool Jamadar had told him that she could not speak; the strange thing was hear either. But Jabeen was sharp. She might be sixteen years old; she was very beautiful to look at, and her senses were keen. She could cook any dish requested. Since she could not speak, she did not complain; even communicate through sign language. She knew a beautiful language of her own! From the furniture brought into the house to the daily vegetables, everything was at her command. Himavant had once said, "Don't ever speak a word against her choices in front of her; Jabeen gets hurt." Urmila was not surprised by her sharpness. In the last two days before the arrangements were finalized, Himu had not done anything; so how had he organized everything? When? Even though she had been right beside him for days, she had not even caught a hint of Himu's actions.

For a moment, a thorn of suspicion about Himu had pricked her, but she had stayed silent. Himavant remained in the house, but Urmila did not notice any major change in his behavior or his usual silence; it was the same silence, the same seriousness, and short answers if spoken to first. Yet, Urmila felt that a major change had definitely occurred deep in some layer of Himavant's brain.

After their lunch, they sat on the sofa in the living room and chatted casually. Once, Urmila became suspicious about whether Jabeen was listening to their conversation, but it passed. Himavant noticed it but remained silent. In the evening, he and Urmila went for a short walk in Ravindranagar. He did not bring up the matter of Prarthana even by mistake. It was as if he had deleted her from his system; he was as calm and peaceful as he used to be during the days when he was in love with her. Everything in this house seemed to be in order, but Urmila thought that, for some reason, everything felt mysterious. And by mysterious, she did not mean that something terrible was about to happen, but rather that the current atmosphere was incomprehensible, unfamiliar, and beyond her immediate grasp.
Page 275
She wondered if she had settled in. She thought that once a couple of days passed, Jabeen's unfamiliarity and silence might fade away or improve. Himavant finished his dinner quickly and went to bed. Urmila also fell asleep. It must have been one o'clock in the morning when suddenly, someone rattled the outside gate. Then, the sound of a car starting was heard. Peering through her room window, she could only see the two red tail lights of the car as it sped away. "It wasn't Himavant who drove off," Urmila told herself. Because the car did not leave with the speed of a novice driver. Or rather, it had been only five days since Himavant had learned to drive; why would he hide that from her? She had been lying there thinking about just that. Wasn't it in the room opposite that Himavant was sleeping? Remembering this, she opened her room door without making a sound and stepped out. She just touched the door of his room with her fingertips; it opened. As she had guessed, Himavant’s bed was empty. As she turned back towards her room, her eyes suddenly froze. Jabeen was standing in the corner of the hall. Her eyes did not look like those of someone who had just woken up from sleep. She was staring fixedly at Urmila. There were no questions there. No surprise. It was just an observation. "It seems Himavant went somewhere, right?" Urmila asked. Jabeen remained in stony silence. "Didn't you get any sleep?" she asked again. Jabeen did not even blink. For some reason, Urmila suddenly started feeling more like she had come and stayed at a stranger's house rather than Himavant's. Himavant, whom she had seen a few days ago, the Himavant who had collapsed, shattered by pain—he felt more familiar. This did not even seem like recovery to her. She thought, a hurt soul becomes more guarded. That is natural; especially for women—she consoled herself and tried to sleep. When Himavant returned home, he said, "What to do, tell me? A cement load arrived suddenly in the night. I thought of going to pick you up, but you must have been asleep, so I thought, why wake you? I went alone."
Page 276
By the time the unloading was finished, dawn had broken. Himavant, who was sitting on the sofa talking, fell asleep; he was exhausted. But Himavant looked as if he had just come from a bath. Urmila, who had lost her train of thought, walked over to him and sat beside him "Himu, no matter how you are or what you do, I like you," she said. "The image of us remains within me. Be true to it. Don't spoil the image that exists within me."

Himavant was slightly taken aback; he looked at her with eyes that held neither laughter nor tears. He simply reached out and placed his arm around Urmila’s shoulder. There was some kind of promise in that touch. In the inner corridor of the house, Jabeen walked away impatiently.

They are together for a long time now. Himavant has no sense of time; he might leave for Gadi koppa at any hour and return home in the middle of some afternoon. Suddenly, he rushes Urmila to leave. His deep red SPM car races towards Jog, forgetting even that it has brakes, moving at a wild speed. Urmila shrieks in a mix of fear and excitement, and at the sharp turns, she loses her balance and leans against Himavant. Urmila, a girl as light as a jasmine flower, has a beautiful scent; she is always fragrant, as if she has just come from a pandanus thicket. She doesn't spend a long time adorning herself, but neat and fresh like a light-pink rose. "White clothes look very good on you," Himavant had said once. The car was racing towards Jog. Suddenly, at a blind curve, Urmila lost her balance and fell directly into Himavant’s lap; it was like a parijata flower falling onto parched, cracked earth along with its dew.

For the first time in many days, the strings of life within Himavant hummed. He felt that if he drove the car at this speed, he would lose control and an accident might occur. He pressed the car's brake, took his foot off the accelerator, brought the vehicle to a halt, and parked it under the shade of the trees by the roadside. Urmila was still leaning in his lap as if asleep, her eyes half-closed. Was it the excitement caused by the speed of the car? comfort of being held in his embrace? Was it an expression? Or overflowing of emotions that had been held back, untold, for so many days?
Page 277
By the time everything was unloaded and they returned, it was light out. Himavant was sitting on the sofa talking, his eyes heavy with sleep. However, Urmila looked as if she had just come from a bath; she walked towards him and sat beside him "Urmila, Himu, I like you no matter how you are or what you do. The image of you within me remains; please don't spoil that image," she said. Himavant was slightly stunned and looked at her with pleading eyes. Neither a smile nor anything else emerged. He simply reached out and placed his hand on Urmila’s shoulder; there was some promise in that touch. In the corridor of the inner house, Jabeen walked away impatiently.

They spent a lot of time together now. For Himu, there was no sense of time; he would go to Gadikoppa at some odd hour and return at another. Suddenly, he would start the deep red Esteem car. He drove toward Jog as if he had forgotten that the car had brakes. In that mix of fear and excitement, Urmila would shriek; at the sharp turns, she would lose her balance and lean onto Himavant. Urmila, a girl light as a jasmine flower, had a lovely scent; she was always fragrant, as if she had just emerged from a grove of pandanus. She was not one to spend much time adorning herself, but spotless. "Other than light pink, white clothes suit you very well," Himavant had once said.

The car was speeding towards Jog. Suddenly, at a blind curve, Urmila lost her balance and fell directly into Himavant's lap; it was as if a Parijata flower had fallen along with its dew onto parched, cracked earth. For the first time in so many days, the strings of life within Himavant began to hum. He felt that if he continued to drive at the same speed, he might lose control and cause an accident. He stepped on the brakes, took his foot off the accelerator, brought the vehicle under control, and parked it in the shade of the trees by the side of the road. Urmila remained leaning in his lap, as if asleep. Her eyes were half-closed. Was it the excitement caused by the car's speed? comfort felt by someone who had fallen into an embrace? Or expression of an emotional overflow that had been suppressed for so many days and could not be voiced?
Page 278
'What will happen to Urmila?' I don't know what is happening. I will lie down like this for a while. Urmila asked, "What is happening?" Himavant did not speak. For a moment, he was reminded of Prarthana. I know, she comes to mind every moment; it is an inescapable curse of this life. When she was with me, she didn't have a car. speed, but she had a thousand excuses to lean and lie on my lap. She used to get emotional just like this—even more intoxicated than I was. A warm breath touching the curves of her ears was enough to make her inner peacock feathers flutter. I was like a glowing ember sitting right there. What kind of restraint was mine? Even the last string of a veena, when tightened and tightened, becomes subject to millions of vibrations—I would feel an excitement as if it might snap with a 'snap' at any moment. But how patiently I used to endure all that! How lovingly set it aside every night, saying, 'Let it be for tomorrow.' Was what I had not goodness? Does goodness extract such heavy taxes? What did that girl leave behind for me? I became a man unreachable to anyone, didn't I? How did Prarthana change so quickly? Is two and a half years such a long time that even loyalty can change? If so, what is the lifespan of a woman's loyalty? What did Prarthana succumb to? His handsomeness? Money? The car? The speed of the car? How many places did they travel? How many such unexpected turns were there? How many times had she, just like Urmila now, pleaded with Debu, "Don't push me away," and sighed? The moment he thought this, a chill ran from the heels of Himavant’s feet, and his face turned dark. He looked at Urmila, who was still lying on his lap. She was still in the same state of ecstasy. However, he realized that he was stroking her forehead without even being aware of it. Was there passion in that stroking? He shook his head abruptly. This is impossible in this life. His hand, placed on her forehead, became numb and still. It happens to everyone like this. It must have happened to Prarthana for the first time too. Wasn't that why Prarthana used to panic and run to Shivamogga? Now he understood. He felt an unbearable guilt growing inside him. She used to shower him with kisses as if there were no tomorrow, "Accept me right now; I might leave tomorrow, so hold me tight, Himu. I might make a mistake," she would plead with her silent eyes. That was love.
Page 279
The reason | 274 If one does not go away—first the person experiencing separation feels a surge of grief. Gradually, for some support, loneliness seeks a foothold. A deep, unbearable sorrow, where is the woman who feels it? Someone says; then the man in the world swears lifelong vows; promises; love, companionship, he calls it love. The feeling that one is making a mistake intensifies. The girl, while melting away, cannot return. When the first love haunts, even if one returns to that place, it is already too late. In a world of billions... is Himavant’s love dying like this? He took his hand off hers. Love is not something that dies in this lifetime; it might be the sweat on Urmila’s forehead. But the life-veena (the lute of life) for Prarthana does not exist even for a moment. Because, today hatred rules; is it possible for love to die? Love will not lean towards a hollow life. That broken sacrifice has already begun in this lifetime. She gathered herself and sat up. "Sorry, I have lost," she thought, as if it remained in her lap. The scent of Urmila’s body still lingered in the car. Neither of them spoke until they reached Jog. They drove the car into the shade of a tree and went inside the traveler’s bungalow. By the time they finished washing, Himavant remembered Prarthana, who had not eaten anything since morning. Urmila said, "I will cook and serve you myself." It was clear that she was a guest in the bungalow. Himavant did not react. The meaning in her words was... They walked out of the room quickly; the roar of the waterfall was audible. The water seemed to leap from a height and fall to the ground, as if jumping—slowly and naturally. Urmila held Himavant’s hand. They began to descend into the valley amidst the mist. They walked in absolute silence, occasionally speaking, occasionally making space for each other, and laughing heartily at small talk. Suddenly, for some reason, Urmila’s fingers gripped Himavant’s shoulder. In the excitement of descending into the valley, she once raised her arm to hug his shoulder, another time she was stroking his back, sometimes she leaned against him, or wrapped her arms around him. Himavant, looking tired, leaned against a large boulder. Urmila, standing beside him, thought, 'I am a creeper.' By the time they climbed back up from the depths of the valley, the sun had begun to fade.
Page 280
Tell me the reason / 275

Himavant thought it would get dark by the time they reached. He immediately felt like turning back, but he remained silent. Urmila, unable to climb the path, started singing in a humming voice while sitting on a rock, with the light drizzle wetting her face: "Rhimjhim gire saawan, sulag jaaye mann!" By the time she finished singing, her whole body was drenched. The sun in the sky was already changing its tune. There was some kind of invitation in her song; there was a yearning, a question of "Can't you give me a little love, friend?" It was not a plea. Himavant realized this much: Urmila was a much more emotional girl than Prarthana! Emotional people only live like this, moving from one end of life to the other. When he saw her two years ago, a cigarette was trembling between her lips. Now, a song was dancing there. Was it my influence? If so, why didn't my influence work on Prarthana? Had I become too weak to turn her towards me? Am I leaning towards this girl now? Is that another weakness? He shook his head and said to himself, "It’s getting dark." Urmila heard him, but her eyes had vanished into the sunset. He walked slowly toward her, held both her shoulders, and jokingly lifted her up with ease, standing her on her feet. Urmila stood as if she had melted into his chest. Then it became dark. After that, for a long time, the two figures stood joined together, sometimes moving apart, holding hands just for the sake of it; supporting each other as if they were about to merge into one another; touching, distancing, teasing, and laughing aloud—they climbed the ridge of the Jog falls. By the time they returned to the tourist bungalow, it felt like the darkness was pinching their eyes, and Himavant felt like he was trying to escape it. "Himu," she said, "I have one request." "What?" he asked. "Can we stay here for just one more night?" Himavant did not speak; suddenly, the lines on his face twisted. Then, without giving her any answer, he reached for his [belongings] on the car seat.
Page 281
He brought the blanket and draped it over her shoulders. "Give me ten minutes, I will light a fire for the cold." Urmila wanted to ask, "Himavant, will the light of our bodies and the waterfall of our minds merge in tonight’s cold fire?" But he was not there.

Except for the roar of the waterfall, there was deep silence all around. The flames rose up, casting light on Urmila's face, making the cold seem to dance. Himavant was tending to the fire, pushing logs into the blaze. Urmila's mind sang, *Tumse pyaar aur nahi hai, tum toh pyaar ho!* (There is no more love than this, you are love itself!) If Himavant were here right now, there would be a hundred conversations, a thousand glances; at every step, such intense flirtation! The frenzy of brushing against him, the memory of the night spent in the guest house amidst the forest near Shivamogga comes to mind. Really, Himavant does not melt like the others. Whether it is physical desire, the proximity of a woman, or such divine solitude, Himavant's seriousness carries a weight of its own. Debu’s thousand attempts to flirt with him seemed insignificant in comparison. She knew him well; not everyone could love someone like Himu. It requires a different state of mind. Two and a half years ago, Urmila would have been bored to death. If the Urmila of those days were here, life would not have been a stormy waterfall by now. It a boat chased by madness. After being thrown out of the Kodagu estate house in humiliation, she had an urge to defy everyone and everything. After finishing her studies and discarding them, she had a longing to study history. Just as one cigarette is lit after another without pause, and peg after another occupies the entire body—she felt life should jump from man to man, from one excavation site of history to another. Even when Debu put his hand on her shoulder in that same forest guest house and said, "I love you," in thought, word, and deed... how had she reacted?
Page 282
"You say you love me, but tell me clearly," she had said, "a girl like me, from a lowly background... love and all that is not for us. Let's see if anyone even looks at us. If you are free one day, look for me. But I don't have time for all this nonsense."

How many months have passed since then? How did this Himavant manage to leave her? Is this what love is? they mean by 'devotion in thought, word, and deed'? If he hadn't reached this state of mind, would it have been possible for him to love Himavant?

Could Prarthana have been like this during those days they met in Channarayapatna? Could she have been restless, waiting just once for Himavant to look at her with love, waiting for him to say just once that he loved her? Back then, they didn't have such a deep history; they only had a future. It was first love for both of them. There were no old memories. Today, Prarthana's shadow is behind Himavant, and behind her, there is a terrifying story.

In such a situation, is it possible for Urmila and Himavant to love each other as intensely and honestly as he and Prarthana had loved?

Urmila sat by the fire, stretching her hands toward it, lost in thought. "While walking from Channarayapatna to Shivamogga, I remembered sitting by a fire like this next to a pond on the way," Himavant suddenly said. It was clear he had slipped into memories of Prarthana.

"Had you two never come to Jog before?" she asked.

"Prarthana didn't have time. She had college. I had to work for her. Maybe she came along with Corona? How would I know?" The deep sadness in his voice made Urmila think that perhaps she shouldn't have asked the question. She sat there, unable to think of what to say next.

He continued without waiting for a response, "Isn't it all so strange? Do you need a car to come to Jog? People come by bus too. If we had wanted to, we could have walked here. But Urmila, I used to keep quiet whenever I felt like asking her fearing it might disturb her studies. think that none of my desires should hinder her future. Did she mistake my goodness for weakness? Loving a person who has a car is one thing, Urmila, but leaving the person you love just because they don't have a car—isn't that a trivial matter, Urmila?"

Having finished speaking, Himavant looked at Urmila's face and gave a wry smile.
Page 283
He laughed; it felt as though such a laugh could only be born on the face of someone who had been deceived. Was it only after the mind reached such a conclusion that all these changes—his car and the rest—came about? The clothes, the new business, the car, the money she left behind; had she reduced herself to this for the sake of social status? Does such a rash nature exist even in someone like Himavant?

"If a boy who walked barefoot all the way from Channarayapatna could say he would get a girl admitted to medical college, even without a marks card in her hand—wouldn't he be able to buy a big car today? Build a bungalow? Become wealthy? Are you saying Prarthana didn't have that faith?" Urmila had stirred the firewood in the bonfire.

"She certainly had it. If she didn't believe that Himavant could achieve anything he set his mind to, who else could? But Urmila, Prarthana knew how to dream, but she didn't know how to share those dreams. There is a vast difference between the two—dreaming is something one does only for oneself. But sharing a dream? That requires a completely different state of mind. She only dreamed that 'I will become a doctor.' I expanded that dream, telling myself, 'I will make her a doctor, I will build her a nursing home, I will marry her.' She didn't even know the meaning of the word 'sharing.' She just thought, 'Once I become be happy.' And when one is happy, it doesn't necessarily mean that specific people must be by one's side, does it? She decided that if there were people happier than her, the happiness would only increase... she walked out, that’s all!"

Himavant finished speaking and sat staring into the darkness of Jog. The night had cast them both separately. The bonfire began to die down slowly. After a long while, they both reached the Jog traveler's bungalow. They ate together. The conversation was brief. Is it possible for love to ever sprout again in this man's heart? Urmila wondered. Prarthana is in the same state of mind as she was three years ago when she left Channarayapatna. A sincere mind; she has a desire to share her dreams with Himavant, and it is selfless. nature that can respond to his needs and desires, melting like snow. But when is that moment of melting? What is it?

Himavant came back after washing his hands and looked at the dark-colored lungi in his briefcase. She wore a tea-shirt. Before turning off the light, he looked at Urmila once.
Page 284
Wearing a white nightie, she was sitting cross-legged in the middle of the bed. Her eyes were closed peacefully, and her breathing was rhythmic. "She has learned meditation," Himavant thought. "Is this the same girl who used to say she would be a servant in Kaveramma’s house?" Without making a sound, he closed the door of the room, turned off the light, switched on the bed lamp, and went to lie down on his own bed. It was a spacious room with two beds and something resembling a dressing table; there was a gap of about two and a half feet between them. In the silence, the rise and fall of Urmila’s breathing could be heard. In the dim light of the bed lamp, she was visible. Perhaps she wasn't as beautiful as Prarthana, but Urmila was lovely.

"Am I not a good person, Himu?" Urmila asked, breaking the silence of the room abruptly as she lay flat on her bed after finishing her thought. It was as if someone had grabbed Himavant by the shoulder and shaken him while he was lost in thought. He immediately sat up. "You... you are a very good person, Urmi. If your goodness hadn't protected me... who knows what would have happened by now? You left your home, your studies, your exams—everything—to be with someone like me, especially in my current condition, and you have stayed here like this. One shouldn't always look for reasons for goodness, Urmila. You know, you are so valuable to me!" he said.

Even though he spoke slowly, there was his usual sincerity in his voice; it sounded firm. Urmila thought, "This man couldn't have told a lie since the day he was born." If it had been Rasool Jamadar, how many lies would he have told by now? How much color added? A flirt! For him, his pleasure and his love had only that much meaning and that much of a limit. But Himavant—he was like a waterfall that never runs dry. Urmila was lost in her own thoughts.

"Why did you ask that, Urmila?" Himavant spoke again.

"I don't know, Himu. Today, just lying beside you, I felt like I wanted to experience a secure feeling that I had never experienced in this life. I just wanted to be on your side. But I started doubting whether I was a good enough person to receive such comfort, and that’s why I asked," she said.

In the dim darkness of the room, no more words were born. For a while, Himavant remained still, without even the slightest movement. There was no answer of any kind from him.
Page 285
Himavant had said, "Let it be, it's 2x0." Prarthana had spoken without checking, as usual. She had spoken too much, and now she was getting sleepy. She lay down on her bed, in the mosquito net, and as the lights went off, Himavant got down from the bed and switched off the bedside light so that it wouldn't make any noise. He went to his bed and lay down, but he didn't know that Prarthana was still awake. He lay down beside her, and as he did, he gently placed his hand on her forehead. He whispered, "You are a very good girl, Prarthana," and kissed her forehead. That night, Prarthana, the young girl, slept like a mole, snuggled up beside Himavant.

At the same time, in the house of Debashish Chandrakant Bandopadhyay, a handsome young man from Hyderabad, it was a different scene. Debashish was not feeling well, and a family doctor had been called. Prarthana, Debashish's mother, was sitting beside his bed, and his father, Chandranath Bandopadhyay, was sitting on the floor, holding Debashish in his arms. Debashish was struggling to breathe, was trying to calm him down. The family doctor had been called, but he hadn't arrived yet.

As the doctor was on his way, Debashish's father and mother were getting more and more worried. They knew that Debashish's condition was serious, but they didn't know what was wrong with him. Debashish had never mentioned his symptoms to anyone, and the family was in a state of panic.

The house, called "Himanshu," was in a state of chaos. The family shock, and they didn't know what to do. Chandranath Bandopadhyay and Prarthana were the most worried, as they knew that Debashish's condition was critical. They had been to Davanagere, where Debashish's father, Shivraj, was the owner of a medical school, and they had seen the body of his friend, Sarvavunga, who had died under mysterious circumstances. similar to Sarvavunga's, but they didn't know what was causing it.

As the family waited for the doctor to arrive, they were getting more and more anxious. They knew that Debashish's life was in danger, and they were doing everything they could to save him. But they didn't know what was wrong with him, and worried by the minute.
Page 286
Debu had left for Hyderabad. Even on the day Prarthana’s exams ended, and they had gone to the dhaba with Didi, Debu remained tense. The next day, while he was still asleep, she had woken up and left for Shivamogga. She had dropped her mother off at the Gundi Chhatra circle. When she returned, she had felt for a moment that something had happened to Debu. He half-asleep and walked out into the courtyard; there, he had planted both his feet on a rangoli of blood that someone had smeared there earlier. After stepping on it, he realized it was blood, but he had just sat there as if he didn’t even know he needed to go to the bathroom to wash it off. Debu’s transformation had started from that moment.

Even before finishing her exams and leaving for Hyderabad, Debu had become dull. He hesitated to even cross the house compound without Prarthana. The day before they were to leave for Hyderabad, they went toward the college to meet a professor and then walked into the hospital right next to it. It was a very familiar hospital that they had walked in and out of hundreds of times over the last two and a half years. Debu was normal as he climbed the steps. But the moment he stepped into the corridor that led to the burns ward, where patients with burn injuries were treated, Debu’s entire expression changed. He was walking like a man who had fallen head over heels for Prarthana. His lips were completely parched; the light had died in his eyes. Only when a vague murmur, "She is calling," escaped his lips at the last moment of crossing the corridor, did Prarthana wake up. "Who is calling?" she asked immediately. Debu looked at her with helpless eyes and stayed silent. It was clear to both of them that no one had actually called out to him. Even after returning home, Debu remained dull. He just lay clinging to the bed. Prarthana thought he must be experiencing that emptiness one feels after studying, writing exams, and finishing everything all at once. After a while, she got him ready for an evening bath. A small attempt in the bathroom. When she stood under the shower and pulled Debu close to her, she remembered that it had been many days since he had become so stiff. As always, Debu moved his hands over Prarthana’s body; over the scars that had long since faded.
Page 287
He had tried to search, but that day he did not even have the patience to look at the scars. He just stared once, then leaned his head under the shower and closed his eyes. Has his desire for her faded away? Prarthana thought this for the first time. Before the wedding was fixed and before the unfortunate incident with Sujay Malco, Debu used to wait for any opportunity to devour her. But afterwards, he suddenly became neutral. Even by mistake, he would not speak about other women as he used to before. However, his love for her had not perished. That comfort was enough. More than that, Prarthana had sighed in relief noticing that Debu’s male body would become alert during the nights, even when he himself was unaware. There were many changes in his personality. Thinking that perhaps he had decided to keep the physical aspect of their relationship for after the wedding in pursuit of some ideal, she had remained silent. But the matter was not that simple; Himavant had already accomplished a deed long ago that made it impossible for them to ever unite as a true husband and wife in this lifetime! To go to Hyderabad, they had to come to Bangalore and catch a flight. This time, Debu hesitated to drive the car, so Prarthana took the wheel. Throughout the journey, he lay with his eyes half-closed. At the Windsor Manor hotel in Bangalore, he said he could not even drink a glass of whiskey; the man who would start dancing to the sound of a small guitar had behaved as if listening to music was torture. Lying in the room at night, he suddenly sat up and whispered in a low voice, "She is calling!" Prarthana was certain that Debu was losing his mental balance. She had asked repeatedly, "Who is calling?" Even until they reached the bungalow in Hyderabad, she had not received an answer to her question of who was calling. In a loud voice, he screamed "Sarvamangala!" It was perhaps the penance for the sins committed by the pharmacist! Debu was not groaning for no reason. Seeing the charred, withered body of Shivarajayya’s daughter, who had passed away years ago...
Page 288
He had wandered the entire city, she was told. It was said that they had met in front of the Baati Dhaba, and without even stepping out of the car, they sat talking for a long time. Was it a conversation? Or was it the grief of Sarvamangala, who had been deceived by the man she loved? How many arguments were there that evening? After the girl returned to Davanagere, as the car neared her house, she did not even wait for it to come to a complete halt. She flung the door open, pushed herself out, and landed on the ground. The only words she uttered as she jumped were, "Did you give me so much happiness only to cause me this much pain, Debu?" Was the man who drank not there? Without even the courtesy of offering her an answer, he had turned the car around and driven away. Uncle Shivarajayya, who had been waiting on the veranda with the lamp lit, had seen her crying by the roadside and brought her into the house. It is said he sat with his daughter all night long, trying to console her in her sorrow. When did they drift off to sleep? By the time they heard a cry from the backyard in the early hours of the morning, they rushed out to see—what was it? She had poured kerosene over herself in the backyard and was burning fiercely, the flames rising to the height of her chest. By the time they took her to the hospital, her body was already charred. The doctors said she would live for at most two more hours. Debu had not even looked toward the hospital. Even by the next afternoon, when she had not yet passed away, and a faint cry of "Debu Babu..." began to escape her throat every few moments, they saw no other option but to go to the hostel, fold their hands, and bring Debu back. They must have thought it was her last wish; perhaps her soul could depart in peace if she saw his face one last time. Debu had stepped into the burns ward with a grimace. He had the foolish, stubborn attitude of "What is my fault if she sang and died?" He was a medical student who could count thousands of women who had come and laid themselves down to die after burning themselves this way in that very burns ward. He was not usually emotional about death. Yet, those eyes he had kissed just last night—they were not completely burnt. Only the eyelids had scorched and curled upward. She must have been seeing him vaguely; she tried to reach out to Debu with longing. Her hand looked like a burnt tree branch. Debu felt a small shudder, that was all; the next moment, Sarvamangala’s life had departed. "Did you call me just to see this? Idiots!" he thought, and without even looking back at the corpse, he had walked away from the burns ward in a hurry. Hadn't Uncle Shivarajayya said that day, "May what happened to my daughter never happen to you"?
Page 289
"Tell me the reason why," 284 "I wrote you a letter, mother." Was something even more terrifying than what happened to Sarvamangala going to happen to Debu now? She was restless. Would there be such a precise punishment for a deception? As for Prarthana, the deception she had committed? For some reason, she did not even have the heart to think about Himavant, nor did she have the time to do so. Two days later, the family doctor had taken Debu to a specialist who examined him as a psychiatric patient. By then, Debu's behavior had completely spiraled out of control. He had started saying that he repeatedly heard someone calling out to him; if any of the children in the house turned on the TV, he would start muttering that Sarvamangala was appearing on it, talking about him, or telling something to someone. The doctors conducted dozens of tests and finally gave a name to Debu's illness: Schizophrenia! But by the third day after that, a repulsive sore had appeared at the corner of Debu’s lip. There were small black patches here and there on his body. Only a few days ago, the prince-like Debu, who had arrived in a Mercedes and caught the eyes of thousands, now looked so repulsive that one would find it difficult to look at him a second time while he lay on the bed. After the family doctor injected a needle into the vein of Debu's forearm to draw a blood sample, he handed a plastic bottle to Prarthana and told her to collect and send a urine sample by evening. The explanation of the matter was in his tone; the patches on Debu's body and the sore on the side of his mouth indicated a venereal disease. It only needed to be confirmed by the blood test. There was a possibility that his strange behavior and the venereal disease he might have were related. If that were true, Debu would require long-term treatment. "In a few days, Debu will start to smell terrible!" Prarthana’s eyes were filled with tears. "Those were just words of a day. I used to wish that a good girl would be found and our boy’s life would change, as any father of a wayward son would wish. But today, standing in your father's place, listen to me, daughter: Do not keep company with Debu. The time for him to suffer his curse has begun. Had you been married, it would have been a different matter. You are still young. I will take responsibility for your education, your expenses, your future, and even your trip to America—you are like a daughter to me. Debu will not return to college. You are mine."
Page 290
"Tell me the reason, or just leave. Don't marry Debu and share his curse. We are his parents... whatever happens, whatever we have to endure, we must bear it. If we can't do anything, he is in Hyderabad, at the Golconda Hotel, with a man called Nishani Fakeer. We will come, you just leave, daughter." Chandranath Bandopadhyay had spoken in a choked voice, having made Prarthana lie down in the inner room. Prarthana sat silently for a long time, then got up and went to Debu’s side. He was already burning with fever. She wiped the sore near his lip with a wet cloth, then placed her hand on his forehead. "Prarthana..." he groaned softly without pain. She sat on the bed, lifted his head, and placed it on her lap. It felt like holding a burning ember. She wet another piece of cloth, wrung it out, and spread it soothingly over his forehead. It was getting dark outside. She switched on the light. Still holding Debu on her lap, she closed her eyes and began reciting her evening shlokas after so many months: "Ya Kundendu Tushara Hara Dhavala..." Debu's father, standing at the door, began to sob silently. The night had begun like that.

"Why do you doubt? I have seen it with my own eyes. They call it Lilavati Hospital. They brought him to ward number 358. They said they would provide different treatment. I kept watching from the window. The boy hiccuped three times, and his mother went to the side to get water. He could see me standing directly at the window. He looked at me as if he wanted to say something, trying to sit up, lifting his back and waist, opening his eyes wide. His mother put a drop of water in his mouth—look—and just like that, he breathed his last!" Rasool Jamadar was describing every detail of Sujay Malhotra's final moments without missing anything. Himavant sat on the stone floor of the house in the Gadikoppa fields, listening to it all without showing a single reaction. The night was very dense. Rasool Jamadar also looked exhausted. There was not a trace of sadness or remorse in Rasool Saab for Sujay Malhotra's death, or for the fact that he himself was directly responsible for it. However, on the day he had drawn a blood-red rangoli in front of Prarthana's house in Davanagere, he did not seem to be his usual self. He had hesitantly muttered, "This shouldn't have happened..." The moment those words escaped his lips, Himavant looked at him.
Page 291
"Tell me the reason, 286 note is not being picked up." That had dampened all of Rasool Jamadar's protests; his was a demonic temper, "just like his father's." Having said that, it was Himavant who, for the very first time, had broken through the window of that demonic temper; after that, he had never spoken looking into Rasool Jamadar's eyes, but he had done something that Himavant could not have imagined even in his wildest dreams. Had he not done it, would have been impossible to save Debu's life.

After returning from Jog, a sense of peace seemed to have been established in Himavant; that night spent there was unforgettable for both of them. Like a peacock chick, Urmila had rested her head on Himavant's arm, curled up, and pressed her face against his firm chest, sleeping without the slightest hesitation. For the first time, she felt she was sleeping so securely with a man. Even while she was awake, Himavant had drifted into sleep. His sleep was not subject to the mind's command; he would not move an inch until morning. No snoring, no stretching, no yawning, no mumbling—uh-uh. When Himavant slept, it was like a volcano had gone to sleep. Forgetting impatience, forgetting agony, he was sleeping. Watching him for a long time, she had fallen into a deep sleep by the time he woke up in the morning. On the way back, they had come to a few decisions. Urmila would stay in Shivamogga until college started. Once it began, she would rent a house in Davanagere. Himavant would go to see her once a week, and if he couldn't, Urmila herself would come to the Shivamogga house. During the days she was there, Urmila would cook, wash clothes, and iron them. In the evenings, a walk together was mandatory. Himavant had agreed to everything, but, "Don't even think about sending Zabeen away. That girl will stay here. Don't ask why or what; she is a girl of few words. What trouble could she cause you? Just mind your own business," he had said. "Zabeen seems very mysterious to me. No matter what time I see her, she is always awake. I suspect she might have an illness called insomnia—it's a condition where one suffers because sleep doesn't come. Some people become mentally disturbed due to that illness. If left like this, it will be a problem for Zabeen in the future. For that reason, at least..."
Page 292
Tell me the reason / 287

"Let's send her for treatment. Every time I see her, she seems to be making herself an outsider. Is that the reason you have kept Jabeen in the house, just for that job?" Urmila felt like saying this to Himavant, but for some reason, she couldn't. Whatever she thought, Jabeen always seemed like a mystery to her. Himavant had gone quiet, as if there was no room for talk about Ekananda. However, from the day he returned, he had immersed himself in the layout work. Whenever he sat down to work, it was as if a giant force took hold of him; there was such dedication in the work he undertook. "This man works eighteen hours a day; is such hard labor necessary to forget the betrayal of one girl?" she used to think. But amidst all that, Himavant would make time to take Urmila for a walk or a long drive in the evenings. What Urmila noticed was that on days when they were certain to be away from the house for four or five hours, he would take Jabeen into his room just before leaving and bolt the door from the inside for five to ten minutes. Before that, Himavant would have bathed; he would be wearing fresh clothes, his hair neatly combed, and ready to head out with Urmila. If one observed him after he came out from being alone with Jabeen in the room for ten minutes, his clothes would not be rumpled, his hair disheveled, and there would be no sign of any confusion on his face. But even by mistake, he would never let Urmila enter that room to peek and see how Jabeen was doing. As soon as he came out of the room, he would take Urmila out, lock the front door himself, and walk away. By the time they returned, Jabeen would be sitting on the sofa in the hall as usual, looking like someone who had just woken up from sleep, with the satisfaction of deep slumber in her eyes. "Before leaving, does Himavant put her into hypnosis and make her sleep for exactly that many hours? Is that possible through hypnosis?" Urmila thought that she should ask the professor when she went to Davanagere next time. That was all; she never even dreamt that there could be some bizarre story behind Jabeen's silence or behind her sleep. The matter that truly disturbed Urmila was Himavant suddenly getting up and leaving in the middle of the night under the pretext that the stone truck had arrived! He would find one excuse or another to stay out of the house all night, leaving Jabeen under guard. As soon as he left, the atmosphere around Urmila...
Page 293
Himavant, who was returning from his morning walk, reached the same spot without fail. The day would begin with a morning stroll; he would have come after a bath. "Dear Prarthana, look, the directions of both our lives have changed. Who would have thought that I would spend my days like this with a friend at this turn of life? Himavant is such a wonderful human being. Ever since I made the decision to marry, he has become a little more silent than before. After that decision, Himavant's mysterious behaviors sometimes haunt me, becoming incomprehensible. He uses me as an excuse to step out of the house. On some nights, the tranquility in his eyes, as if he has returned from some meditation, is accompanied by a sense of anxiety. Does he fight with your memories? I don't know. Does he love me? I am not certain. Have I started loving him? But I feel comfortable with him. You are not the kind of small-minded person who would think I have occupied your place, are you? I should imagine your place as my own; if not in this house, then in this world. How are your days, Debu and Hyderabad? I hope they are lovely days. Let us meet as soon as the classes begin. Perhaps you will see Himavant with me. By the way, there is a phone at Himu’s house; call me when you are free. Convey my regards to Debu."

Urmila had written a small letter, enclosed the number of Himu’s house, and dropped it into the mailbox. While Urmila was writing the letter in the inland cover, she noticed Zabeen pacing the entire house with impatient restlessness. However, the true horror of the situation only dawned on her on the third day after the letter was mailed, when Prarthana called from Hyderabad. As soon as she said "Hello," she began to sob so heart-wrenchingly, calling out "Urmi Didi," that she was unable to explain what had happened. Overcome by uncontrollable sobs, she called three times in fifteen minutes and hung up each time. At first, Urmila thought that Debu had caused her some trouble and that she might have to leave for Hyderabad immediately. It was only when Prarthana swallowed her tears and began to speak that Urmila understood what her agony was.
Page 294
...tell me the reason, 289. It was schizophrenia; within two weeks, those symptoms were gone, and syphilis in its horrific stage appeared. Now that too is gone, and a new disease has begun in Debu. All his body hair has fallen out. What appeared as a small wound on his back has now grown into a deep, ugly ulcer as wide as a cobra's hood. They call it a carbuncle. The pain in his back is horrific. Since last night, he has been fainting continuously. No matter what scans they do, the cause of the disease is unknown. His mouth is foaming. I don't feel like Debu will survive, Didi. Would it not have been better if death had come to me instead? That day you left Davanagere—it seems something happened between the time I saw you off in an auto at Gundi Chhatra and returned home. Debu had stepped on a blood-rangoli someone had left in front of the house. There is no doubt; it was human blood. I am scared, Didi.

The moment Prarthana disconnected the phone, Urmila's resolutions began to intensify. No, what has happened to Debu is not a disease. It problem that can be cured or solved by ordinary humans; the problem has originated in Shivamogga! The fact that Jabeen had informed me that I had written a letter to someone had shattered his peace and stability for many days. It was rare for him to return home in those three days. He was undoubtedly spending his nights in the open fields of Gadikoppa. No, he would not let Prarthana live in peace. A man so gentlemanly, an eternal lover who lived thinking she was his entire world; a man of self-respect who wouldn't even touch others' money with his left hand; a noble man who had shut all doors, believing no other woman could ever enter his life; a kind-hearted man who set out to help the helpless and the destitute—but now, a demon has emerged from within him. That is the trouble. A very good man is more powerful than a very wicked one; his is a virtuous power. What if his conscience suddenly derailed? There is no one crueler than him. Has Himavant become cruel? What happened to his restraints? Where did his ideals go? What right before our eyes? Sujay stood still. As the memory of Muldotra surfaced, a chill ran through Urmila’s entire body. a murderer? Is this the murderer I loved?
Page 295
"Tell me before you leave. We were ready to be together." Urmila was confused; ever since she finished her shift and returned to Shivamogga, from the very moment she arrived, she reminded herself of Himavant's true colors. At no point had he promised her anything, and she had never mistaken him for a ruthless killer. But Himavant no longer carried the same pain as before; there were no agonies or anxieties from the days after Prarthana had left. internal explosions. She realized that she had overlooked this change—the volcanic silence of Himavant had suddenly become calm. She had not guessed the reason. His behavior had become mysterious; even though she was wary, she did not suspect him. Had Himavant changed while being with her? She suddenly turned and looked toward the kitchen. Her intuition was not wrong; a strange fear was dancing in the eyes of Jabeen, who stood at the kitchen door. She felt as though Jabeen could hear and had overheard every word she had spoken with Prarthana. The very next minute, as if deciding that the panic on Jabeen's face should show her the way and that she must find the answer to this problem in those trembling eyes, Urmila stood up. The first question that stirred in her mind was—Where is he? Rasool Jamadar? She did not delay further. At this hour, Himavant would be in Shivamogga, or if not, he would be immersed in the work at the 'Amma Layout' that had started on the Ayanur road. He was completely occupied with work during the day. The work was reaching its final stage. There was another project—the Prarthana Layout work in the Gadikoppa fields. Rasool Jamadar was the one overseeing the work. As soon as she thought of him, Urmila hailed an auto and reached "Sit down, Doctoramma." Rasool Jamadar placed two chairs under the shade of a tree and began to speak. "If you hadn't come today, I would have come tomorrow myself." Urmila noticed the wrinkles of worry moving on his forehead, but she spoke sharply, "Rasool, if I you would have taken a second life. Don't talk." Her voice rose higher, "It is not just because I am standing in front of you asking that I am angry." "Don't say that, Doctoramma, the one left at home..."
Page 296
"Tell me the reason, 20."

Rasool Jamadar sighed. "It was fate. A cursed outcome that silenced speech." For the first time, Urmila spoke, forgetting that she was a doctor. "Don't get agitated, mother. It’s not the child's fault. It has taken away her speech; sleep. Bringing her home was meant to protect you! Our Himavant is no longer the man he once was, mother." Just as Rasool Jamadar began to speak, a dark cloud floated in from somewhere and began to cover the fields of Gadikoppa. In a very soft voice, narrate the story of Himavant’s inhumane revenge.

By the time Urmila returned home, the evening had died and night had fallen. The SP's car parked at the door confirmed that Himavant was in the house; however, there were no lights inside. The door creaked open as if pushed. Although she was certain that the person slumped on the sofa in the inner room was Himavant, she stood at the doorway for a minute, unable to walk freely into the dark house. Himavant, who had been sitting as if lying down shifted slightly. Urmila’s eyes slowly adjusted to the darkness inside. "Come, Urmi," he said, rising from the sofa. A strange fear gripped Urmila in the darkness of the unlit inner room. It intensified the moment she saw Himavant’s face in the light. She stood stunned for a moment. It felt as if there was no connection between the Himavant she saw every day and the Himavant standing before her now. life in Himavant's eyes; they looked like two lumps of coal stuck on either side of his nose. His lips were completely dried out and appeared lifeless. The weariness of centuries seemed to have condensed on his face. His hair looked like the bristles of a broom that had swept dirt and been set aside. There was no vitality in his walk as he returned to the sofa after switching on the light. Asking, "What has happened to you, Himu?", she rushed toward him. But, suddenly, the story of Himavant’s revenge, which Rasool Jamadar had spent the whole day telling her, came to mind, and she stopped abruptly, feeling as though she shouldn't go near him.
Page 297
"What is wrong with me? Just a bit of exhaustion, that’s all." The voice, rising from the depths of a well, seemed to come from someone who could not sit upright on the sofa. By the time she reached him, Urmila’s enthusiasm for a confrontation had also withered away. Whether it was the growing impatience toward Himavant, or the natural urge of a friend to rush and see Prarthana, who had screamed "Urmila Didi!" and wept over the phone—she could not decide. What was the use of talking to him anyway? If she went to Prarthana quickly, she could at least offer some comfort to her friend. Before leaving, she had decided she would tell Himavant, "I am leaving for Hyderabad. Whether it is death or something else... I will be with her during her days of misfortune. I will never again step foot into this house, into your mysterious abyss, into this marketplace of death," and leave without waiting for his answer. But, could she leave Himavant in this state? And what could she even do by going? If she listened to what Prarthana had said on the phone, it seemed Debu would not survive much longer. Prarthana was not a fool to be scared easily. Having already seen death in the hospital daily, she knew how death lurks and approaches. They said all the machines of the body were shutting down one by one; in a sense, Debu was only clinically alive. She had said that death could occur at any moment. Was it impossible to stop it? She had asked Rasool Jamadar a thousand times, but even he did not know the answer for sure. One who has walked through a bloodbath cannot live for long. Seven days, twenty-one days, forty-two days...! What kind of resolution has Himavant made? "What can I tell you, Doctor-amma? I was the one who took him to the cave of the Ajjayya throne for the first time. I couldn't bear to see Himavant's condition. He used to mutter a lot, as if he was suffering from something in his head. Whoever came in front of him, he would start telling them, 'Look at it like this.' At first, it felt like a joke. It was surprising. I felt he should be examined. But when I saw Nandakka's death, I couldn't bear it anymore. I was the one who arranged a car and took Himavant there. 'Come, Sabanna, I knew you would come. bring him. on this very day,' he had said to me. Sabanna reached the cave entrance and spoke. We were there for three days, Himavant was silent..."
Page 298
He had been sitting there for three days holding onto a stone, as if clutching the feet of some deity on a pedestal, when he fell into a state of delirium due to a severe fever and lost consciousness. It felt as if there was no hope left for his survival, but then the new moon passed and the light of the first lunar day appeared, didn't it? He woke up muttering, "Avla..." His eyes cleared, the fever had subsided. He looked at me, and as he began to ask, "Hasn't Prarthana come?", he suddenly stopped. The old man, standing in the corner, laughed grotesquely. Himavant didn't like it one bit. But what did it matter if he had said, "From this time on, you must come here for three new moons." We both went and returned for all three new moons. By the time the fifth new moon arrived, the old man himself had come to Gadikoppa, hadn't he? Death seemed to have veiled the old man's eyes like a film. He had said, "I have come, my boy... now the responsibility of sending me off is yours." Right there, in the courtyard of this stone house, he stretched out his legs and lay down. Perhaps it would have been better if I hadn't seen it, but Himavant has a mother's heart. He watched over him until the very last moment. Every night, that old man would wake Himavant up, take him out, and make him sit on the mound next to the stone house. They said he would recite mantras until dawn. Doctoramma, how would I know what all that was? Whatever they asked for, I would bring it to them. After the old man died, Himavant didn't speak to anyone for a whole day. That was when he learned this craft, Doctoramma... was it even necessary to learn all that just to acquire the wealth that comes from trampling over a rangoli of blood and killing people? Rasool Jamadar had told him everything, leaving no detail behind. Even after speaking at such length, there was no disdain in Rasool Jamadar's voice toward Himavant. disgust either. Nor could one conclude that Rasool Jamadar naturally loved Himavant simply because he had become a partner in all his sins. Doctoramma, haven't I been watching that girl Prarthana since the very first night she set foot in Shivamogga? She stood near the Shivamurthy Circle under a dim light, holding onto this same Himavant's hand just like a little sparrow. I can still see it clearly before my eyes. Her toe had stumbled and torn, and he had carried her all the way from Channarayapatna in his arms. But once he brought her, how did Himavant take care of her? Let me tell you, Doctoramma—the cars, the house, the job, the layout that exists today—none of that existed back then, did it? The determination he had to make Prarthana a doctor—did he not suffer enough for that? If they managed to eat twice a day...
Page 299
"You may find many people, Doctoramma, who struggle through the day eating only once, fearing that the money they earn won't be enough. But to endure hunger while lying flat under the open sky, waiting for some light to appear somewhere, hoping that everything will be alright—to bear that pain, that goodness... except for Himavant, I have never seen such a man to this day," Rasool Jamadar had said. There was neither exaggeration nor justification in his words; he was speaking the truth. Just as it was true that Himavant’s thumb had been broken the very first day he arrived in Shivamogga, it was equally love for Prarthana, who watched the sky with her back pressed to the earth, hoping for a light to emerge for a better life, was genuine.

Prarthana whispered this to herself, reflecting on the tone of his words. But he was no longer lying there expecting a light to appear on the horizon for things to turn out well. He had finally reached the decision of a murderer: a grim dissatisfaction that sought to take a life. Was it the expectation that if he killed her after seven days of torture, she would return to him? Was it possible for such hatred to grow in someone who had loved her so much and taken such good care of her? Was there such a barbaric face behind that love? She did not know. But this was not the time for logic.

Having committed a betrayal that should never have happened, when Prarthana had returned to her hostel in Davanagere without telling Himavant, he had rushed to Shivamogga to comfort her. Was that the same friendship? It was for that very friendship that she had run to him, seeking his shelter when she was in trouble. "I must be with her," she thought, and headed quickly through the inner rooms towards her own.

Even in those moments of confusion, her eyes searched for Zabeen. As usual, she was not standing leaning against the kitchen door. Thinking she must be nearby, she entered her room and quickly packed a few clothes. Even when she came back with her travel bag, she did not see Zabeen. She glanced towards the corner where Zabeen usually kept her clothes, but in that corner...
Page 300
The fact that there was a girl who couldn't speak, and that Zabeen had left the house once and for all—it all became clear. Did Himavant know that she had gone? Did he know everything? Had he come here and lain down only after finishing his final task? Had he decided that he no longer had the responsibility of looking after Zabeen's resolve? She thought of asking Himavant, looking him in the eye, "Where did she go?" But suddenly, she felt that it wasn't a matter that concerned her. She came out of the room with her bag. Himavant had already fallen asleep and was sprawled on the sofa. She stood by his head for a moment and, as if slipping, said, "Himuu."

"Mm?" he muttered, but did not open his eyes. Feeling suspicious, Urmila placed her hand on his forehead; Himavant’s fever was beyond what a thermometer could measure. He was not even in a condition to sit up. She felt that if he weren't admitted to the hospital, there might be trouble. He was in such a state that he didn't even notice her touching his forehead. At this hour, where could she find Rasool Jamadar? If she went to the fields of Gaadikoppa, she might find someone who knew the Jamadar's whereabouts, but was it safe to go there alone in this darkness? Was it even possible for her to leave for Hyderabad? Urmila was caught in a deep dilemma. Just as she was standing by Himavant’s head, he turned slightly in his sleep and, without realizing it, groaned, "Prarthana..."

Stunned and feeling directionless, she placed her handbag on the floor and decided to soak a small towel in water. Just as she turned toward the bathroom, the telephone rang continuously. Placing the cloth she was holding onto the sofa, Urmila walked toward the phone, noticing Himavant struggling to sit up. If the person calling was Prarthana, would it be possible to talk in front of Himavant? Caught in that dilemma, she went and picked up the phone. Two minutes later, strange expressions flickered across Urmila's face and then faded.

"Didi, is Himu doing well now?"

Stopping mid-sentence, she dropped the receiver to the floor. No matter how many times she called out "Himu, Himu," he did not speak. All she could hear was someone sobbing uncontrollably on the other end. Then, someone else...
Page 301
"Tell me the reason, 296, how is Himu now, Didi?" she asked, and before I could even pick up the phone, there was an urgency in her voice. Did she mean to convey everything to Urmila before she returned home after finding out? Did Himu signal Prarthana to leave just as Jabeen had spoken after she finished talking? Did he end up in this state because he left the house with Prarthana? What did he say? Did Himavant tell her everything he had done after Prarthana spoke? Why such a sudden sense of peace in her voice? What a difference there is between her voice when she wailed, "Will Debu’s life be saved, Didi?" and now when she asks, "Is Himu better now?" What conversation happened between the two of them? What promise did Prarthana give? Did their broken friendship blossom again? What happened to Debu? As Urmila struggled, not knowing how to answer as no immediate answers to any of these questions were forthcoming, Prarthana started speaking again from the other end. "Tell Himu, Didi, that I have done everything he asked me to do; I have no other expectations now. Tell him I am just waiting for him to come to Hyderabad. He has become very emotional. The responsibility of looking after him for a night or two is yours. Please talk to him. I will call you again in the morning. Good night, Didi!" Having said this, she hung up. When Prarthana finished, Urmila noticed Himavant fumbling for water. There was a different kind of light in his eyes now.

Urmila quickly went and brought a glass of water, took Himavant onto her lap, and fed him water bit by bit. There was a weariness on Himavant's face like that of a soldier who had lost a great battle. Only once did he try to fix his eyes on Urmila, but he stopped; what was he trying to look for in her face? After drinking the water, Urmila was lost in different thoughts. Two gulps...
Page 302
